Ayahs

طسٓمٓ (١)
Ta, Seen, Meem. (1)
تِلْكَ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٢)
These are the signs of the Manifest Book. (2)
لَعَلَّكَ بَـٰخِعٌ نَّفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا۟ مُؤْمِنِينَ (٣)
You are liable to imperil your life [out of distress] that they will not have faith. (3)
إِن نَّشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ ءَايَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْنَـٰقُهُمْ لَهَا خَـٰضِعِينَ (٤)
If We wish We will send down to them a sign from the sky before which their heads will remain bowed in humility. (4)
وَمَا يَأْتِيهِم مِّن ذِكْرٍ مِّنَ ٱلرَّحْمَـٰنِ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا كَانُوا۟ عَنْهُ مُعْرِضِينَ (٥)
There does not come to them any new reminder from the All-beneficent but that they disregard it. (5)
فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا۟ فَسَيَأْتِيهِمْ أَنۢبَـٰٓؤُا۟ مَا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٦)
They have already denied [the truth], but soon there will come to them the news of what they have been deriding. (6)

Content of Surah ash-Shu'ara

It is well-known among commentators that all 227 verses of this Surah, except for the last four verses, were revealed in Makkah. [^1] The tone of the verses in this Surah is also completely consistent with other Makkan Surahs; and we know that in the Makkan Surahs revealed at the beginning of the Islamic call, the emphasis was mostly on the principles of belief—Monotheism (Tawhid), the Resurrection (Ma'ad), the call of the Prophets of God, and the importance of the Quran—and nearly all the discussions in Surah ash-Shu'ara revolve around these issues.

In fact, the content of this Surah can be summarized into several sections:

The first section is the opening of the Surah, which speaks of the Disconnected Letters (Muqatta'at), followed by the greatness of the Quran's status, the consolation of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله in the face of the persistence and obstinacy of the polytheists, and a reference to some of the signs of Tawhid and the attributes of God.

The second section recounts episodes from the lives of seven great Prophets and their struggles against their people, as well as the stubbornness and defiance of those people toward these Prophets. Some, like the story of Moses and Pharaoh, are more detailed, while others, such as the accounts of Abraham, Noah, Hud, Salih, Lot, and Shu'ayb, are briefer.

Specifically, in this section, reference is made to the weak and prejudiced logic of the polytheists in every age and time against the Divine Prophets, which bore a great resemblance to the logic of the polytheists during the time of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله. This served as a source of consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the few early believers, letting them know that history remembers many such individuals and arguments, so they should not allow any weakness or lethargy to creep into their resolve.

Furthermore, particular emphasis is placed on the painful punishment of these nations and the terrifying calamities that befell them, which served as an effective threat to the opponents of the Prophet of Islam under those circumstances.

The third section, which in fact serves as a conclusion to the previous sections, speaks about the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the greatness of the Quran, the denial of the polytheists, and instructions to the Prophet regarding the method of invitation and how to interact with the believers. The Surah ends with glad tidings for the righteous believers and a severe warning to the oppressors.

Additionally, the name of this Surah is derived from the final few verses that speak about aimless poets (Shu'ara).

It is also noteworthy that in terms of the number of verses, this Surah exceeds all other Surahs of the Quran after Surah al-Baqarah, although this is not the case regarding the number of words; rather, it is shorter than many of them.

The Virtue of Surah ash-Shu'ara

In a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the importance of reciting this Surah, we read:

"Whoever recites Surah ash-Shu'ara shall have a reward of ten good deeds for every person who believed in Noah or denied him, as well as Hud, Shu'ayb, Salih, and Abraham, and for the number of all those who denied Jesus and believed in Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله." [^1] It goes without saying that all this reward and recompense is not for recitation devoid of reflection and action. Rather, there are numerous indications in the narrations regarding the virtues of the Surahs showing that what is meant is a recitation that serves as a prelude to reflection, and subsequently, to will and action, as we have pointed out in the past.

Coincidentally, the expression used in the above Hadith also supports this point, because the entitlement to good deeds based on the number of those who believed in or denied the Prophets is due to aligning oneself with the path of the believers and distancing oneself from the path of the deniers.

[^1]: Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," Tafsir "Fakhr Razi," Tafsir "Qurtubi," and Tafsir "Tibyan." In Tafsir "Ruh al-Ma'ani," five verses are excepted, but some commentators such as Allamah Tabataba'i in "Al-Mizan" have not accepted the exception of these verses, and God willing, we will have more discussion under those same verses.

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," beginning of Surah ash-Shu'ara.

In the name of Allah, the Most Gracious, the Most Merciful.

Ta, Seen, Meem. (1) These are the verses of the Clear Book. (2) Perhaps you will kill yourself with grief because they do not become believers. (3) If We will, We can send down upon them a sign from the sky, so their necks will remain humbled before it. (4)

And no new reminder comes to them from the Most Gracious but they turn away from it. (5) For they have indeed denied, so there will come to them the news of what they used to ridicule. (6)

Commentary

They are Terrified of Anything New!

Once again, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter another example of the Disconnected Letters of the Quran (Ta, Seen, Meem).

Regarding the interpretation of these Disconnected Letters and their likes at the beginning of Surahs Al-Baqarah, Al-Imran, and Al-A'raf, we have had detailed and separate discussions, which we see no need to repeat.

What must be added here is that several narrations have been quoted from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله or some of the companions regarding the interpretation of "Ta, Seen, Meem," all of which indicate that these letters are abbreviations for the names of Allah, the names of the Quran, sacred places, or certain trees of Paradise and the like.

These narrations support the interpretation we cited at the beginning of Surah Al-A'raf in Volume Six regarding this matter. At the same time, it does not contradict what we presented at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah—that the purpose is to demonstrate the miracle and greatness of the Quran, showing that this grand discourse is composed of simple and small letters.

The next verse describes the greatness of the Quran as follows: "These are the verses of the Clear Book" (تِلْكَ آياتُ الْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ). Of course, "tilka" in Arabic grammar refers to the distant, meaning "that" or "those." As we have previously mentioned, in Arabic speech—and sometimes in Persian—the distant demonstrative pronoun is used to express the greatness of something; meaning the subject is so important and high-ranking that it is as if it is out of our reach and at the peak of the heavens.

It is noteworthy that this verse appears in this exact form, without any change, at the beginning of Surah "Yusuf" and "Al-Qasas" as well, and in all these cases, it follows the Disconnected Letters, which indicates the connection between these "letters" and the "greatness of the Quran."

Describing the "Quran" as "Mubeen" (Clear), which originally comes from the root "Bayan" (clarity/expression), refers to the manifest nature of its greatness and miracle, such that the more a person reflects on its content, the more familiar they become with its miraculous nature.

Beyond this, the Quran is the clarifier of "Truth" from "Falsehood" and the revealer of the path to happiness, victory, and salvation from misguidance.

Then, it proceeds to comfort the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, saying: "Perhaps you will kill yourself with grief because they do not become believers!" (لَعَلَّكَ باخِعٌ نَفْسَكَ أَلَّا يَكُونُوا مُؤْمِنِينَ). "Bakhi'" comes from the root "Bakh'" (to kill oneself out of intense sorrow and grief). This expression shows to what extent the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was compassionate toward the people and how much he persisted and insisted on fulfilling His mission; He was distressed to see thirsty people sitting beside the spring of the pure water of the Quran and Islam, yet still crying out from thirst.

He was distressed as to why a rational human being, despite having all these bright lamps, would still go astray, fall into a precipice, and be destroyed.

Yes, all the Divine Prophets were this compassionate, especially the Prophet of Islam, regarding whom this expression has appeared repeatedly in the Quran.

Some commentators say that the reason for the revelation of the above verse was that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله constantly invited the people of Makkah to Tawhid, but they would not believe. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله became so upset that the effects were visible on His face; the above verse was revealed and comforted the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. [^1]

The next verse, to prove the fact that Allah is capable of everything—even that He could force them all to believe—says: "If We will, We can send down upon them a sign from the sky, so their necks will remain humbled before it" (إِنْ نَشَأْ نُنَزِّلْ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ آيَةً فَظَلَّتْ أَعْناقُهُمْ لَها خاضِعِينَ). This is an indication that We have the power to send down a dazzling miracle, or a severe and terrifying punishment upon them, such that they would all involuntarily bow their heads in humility before it and submit. However, such forced faith has no value; what is important is that they humble themselves before the Truth out of their own will, decision, understanding, and reflection.

It goes without saying that the purpose of the "necks" being humbled is the humbling of their owners. However, "neck" in Persian, and "raqabah" or "'unuq" in Arabic, because it is an important part of the human body, is mentioned as a metaphor for the human being himself. For example, rebellious individuals are called "neck-extenders" (stiff-necked), bullies are called "thick-necked," and weak individuals are called "broken-necked"! Of course, other possibilities have been suggested for the interpretation of "a'naq" (necks) here: including that "a'naq" means "leaders," or "groups of people," but all these possibilities are weak.

Then, it refers to the stance of the polytheists and disbelievers toward the Quran, stating: "And no new reminder comes to them from the Most Gracious but they turn away from it" (وَ ما يَأْتِيهِمْ مِنْ ذِكْرٍ مِنَ الرَّحْمنِ مُحْدَثٍ إِلَّا كانُوا عَنْهُ مُعْرِضِينَ). The use of the word "Dhikr" (Reminder) points to the reality that the Quran is an awakener and an informer in all its verses and Surahs, yet this group flees from awakening and awareness!

And the use of "Ar-Rahman" (The Most Gracious) indicates that the revelation of these verses from the Lord stems from His "General Mercy," which invites all human beings without exception to happiness and perfection.

It may also be to stimulate the people's sense of gratitude—that these words are from a God whose blessings have enveloped you from head to toe; how can you turn away from your Benefactor like this? And if He does not hasten to punish you, that too is from His mercy.

The term "Muhdath" (new/recent) indicates that the verses of the Quran are revealed one after another, and each contains new content. But what benefit is there when these people are incompatible with these new truths? It is as if they have formed an eternal bond with the superstitions of their ancestors and are not willing at any price to bid farewell to ignorance, misguidance, and superstitions. In principle, ignorant, prejudiced, and stubborn individuals are always opposed to new things, even if they lead to guidance, awareness, and salvation.

In verse 68 of Surah Al-Mu'minun, we read: "Then have they not reflected over the Word, or has there come to them that which had not come to their forefathers?" (And they fight against it on the pretext of it being new).

Then it adds that they do not stop at "turning away," but rather they reach the stage of "denial" and, worse than that, "ridicule." It says: "For they have indeed denied, so there will come to them the news of what they used to ridicule," and they will become aware of the painful punishment for their actions (فَقَدْ كَذَّبُوا فَسَيَأْتِيهِمْ أَنْبؤُا ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ). "Anba'" is the plural of "Naba'," meaning important news, and what is meant here are the severe punishments that will overtake them in this world and the next. Although some commentators like "Sheikh Tusi" in "Al-Tibyan" have limited these punishments to the punishment of the Hereafter, most commentators consider it absolute and inclusive of both types of punishment, and in reality, it is so, because the verse is absolute. Furthermore, disbelief and denial have a vast and terrifyingly wide impact on the entirety of human life; how can it be ignored?

An examination of this verse and the previous verse shows that when a person places themselves on deviant paths, they increase their distance from the Truth in an ever-increasing manner:

First is the stage of turning away, averting the face, and indifference toward the Truth. But gradually, they reach the stage of denial and rejection. Then they go beyond this stage and subject the Truth to mockery, and following that, the Divine punishment arrives (a similar expression also appears at the beginning of Surah Al-An'am, verses 4 and 5).

Key Points

1- In one of the famous sermons of "Nahj al-Balagha," Ali عليه السلام pointed to the reality that Allah sent the Prophets in such a way that people could freely make decisions to believe; for if it were otherwise, faith would be forced and would have no benefit. He says:

"If Allah had wanted, when He sent His Prophets, to open for them the doors of treasures, mines of gold, and lush green gardens, He would have done so. And if He had wanted to send the birds of the sky and the wild animals of the earth with them, He would have done so. But if He had done this, the trial would have vanished, and the reward and punishment would have become ineffective." [^1] In the book "Al-Kafi," under the verse in question, it is stated: "If Allah willed, He would have sent down a sign from the sky so that their necks would be humbled before it, and if He had done so, the trial would have been removed from all people." [^2] It is noteworthy that in famous books such as "Irshad al-Mufid," "Rawdah al-Kafi," "Kamal al-Din" of Saduq, and "Tafsir al-Qummi," it is mentioned that Imam Sadiq عليه السلام, in interpreting the verse "If We will, We can send down upon them a sign from the sky...", said: "The reference is to the tyrants of the Umayyads who will see a heavenly sign at the time of the uprising of the Mahdi عليه السلام and will be forced to submit before it." [^1] It is clear that the purpose of these narrations is to state an instance of the broad concept of the verse—that eventually, at the time of the appearance of Hazrat Mahdi عليه السلام, the leader of the global government, all governments of oppression and injustice that continue the line of the Umayyads will, by force, bow their heads in submission before the power of the Mahdi and His Divine support.

2- We know that one of the long-standing debates in the early centuries of Islam was the discussion regarding whether the "Word of Allah" (Kalam Allah) was created (hadith) or eternal (qadim). The scope of this debate even extended to books of Tafsir, and a group of commentators argued for the createdness of the Quran based on the expression used in the above verses (Muhdath). However, as we have previously pointed out, the basis of this discussion could not have been logical at all, and it seems that the rulers of that time from the Umayyads and Abbasids were involved in fueling these kinds of deviant debates that distracted the minds of Muslims from important and serious issues. They wanted these issues as a means to keep the scholars of Islam occupied and to continue their own autocratic rule.

For if the "Word of Allah" refers to the content of the Quran, that has been in the knowledge of Allah from eternity and He was aware of all of it. And if the purpose is the revelation of the inspiration, then the words and letters of the Quran are certainly created (hadith). Therefore, in one sense it is eternal and in another sense it is certainly created, and there is no room for debate. The Islamic society, especially the scholars and thinkers, must be vigilant and not get caught up in deviant debates created by tyrants and enemies.

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, Vol. 8, under the verse in question.

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Khutbah al-Qasi'ah (No. 192).

[^2]: Al-Kafi, as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, under the verse in question.

[^1]: Tafsir "Al-Mizan" and "Nur al-Thaqalayn" under the verses in question.

Ayahs

أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ إِلَى ٱلْأَرْضِ كَمْ أَنۢبَتْنَا فِيهَا مِن كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ (٧)
Have they not regarded the earth, how many of every splendid kind [of vegetation] We have caused to grow in it? (7)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٨)
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (8)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٩)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (9)

Commentary

Gender in Plants

In the preceding verses, the discussion pertained to the turning away of the disbelievers from the legislative signs, namely the Glorious Quran. In the verses under discussion, the focus shifts to their turning away from the cosmic signs and the manifestations of Allah across the expanse of creation. Not only did they close the ears of their souls to the words of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but they also deprived their eyes of seeing the signs of Truth surrounding them.

First, He says: "Have they not looked at the earth—how many of every noble pair of plants We have caused to grow therein?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الْأَرْضِ كَمْ أَنْبَتْنا فِيها مِنْ كُلِّ زَوْجٍ كَرِيمٍ) [^1]. Here, the use of the term "pair" (zawj) regarding plants is noteworthy. Although most commentators have interpreted zawj to mean "type" or "species," and azwaj as "types" and "categories," what is there to prevent us from taking zawj in its literal and well-known meaning—which comes to mind before any other—referring to gender (male and female) in the world of plants? In the past, humans had understood to some extent that certain plants possess male and female types and utilized pollination to fertilize them; this matter was fully recognized regarding date palms. However, it was the famous Swedish scientist and botanist, Linnaeus, who first succeeded in the mid-18th century in discovering the reality that gender in the plant world is almost a universal law, and that plants, like most animals, are fertilized through the union of male and female cells and subsequently bear fruit.

Yet, the Glorious Quran, centuries before this scientist, repeatedly pointed to gender in the plant world in various verses (in the verses under discussion, as well as in Surah Ar-Ra'd verse 4, Luqman verse 10, and Surah Qaf verse 7), and this is itself one of the scientific miracles of the Quran.

The word karim (noble/generous) originally refers to anything highly valuable. Sometimes it is used for humans, sometimes for plants, and sometimes even a letter is described as karim, such as the words of the Queen of Sheba regarding Solomon's letter: إِنِّي أُلْقِيَ إِلَيَّ كِتابٌ كَرِيمٌ (An-Naml: 29).

[^1]: Usually, the root of "vision" (ru'yah) becomes transitive to an object without the preposition ila (to), and sometimes even takes two objects. If we see it here becoming transitive with ila, it is because it carries the meaning of "contemplation" (nazar), which points to a precise and non-superficial gaze.

The meaning of a "noble plant" (giyah-e karim) refers to highly beneficial plants. Of course, every plant possesses benefits that become increasingly apparent day by day with the advancement of science.

In the following verse, as a further emphasis and clarification, He says: "Indeed, in that [creation of valuable plants] is a clear sign [of the existence of Allah]" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً). Yes, paying attention to the reality that this seemingly worthless soil, with a specific composition, is the origin of various beautiful flowers, fruitful trees, and colorful fruits with completely different properties, expresses the ultimate power of Allah. However, these blind-hearted individuals are so heedless and unaware that even when they see such Divine signs, they remain in negligence, because disbelief and stubbornness have taken root in their hearts. Therefore, at the end of the verse, He says: "And most of them were not believers" (وَ ما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

This means that this lack of faith has become like a deep-seated trait within them. And what wonder is there that they do not benefit from these signs? For the receptivity of the vessel is also a primary condition for influence, just as we read regarding the Quran: هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ—"a guidance for the God-fearing" (Al-Baqarah: 2).

In the final verse under discussion, using an expression that is both a sign of threat and encouragement, both fear and hope, He says: "And indeed, your Lord—He is the All-Mighty, the All-Merciful" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ). Al-Aziz (The All-Mighty) refers to the Powerful One who is invincible; He has the ability to present great signs and is the crusher of the deniers. Yet, despite this, He is Ar-Rahim (The All-Merciful), and His vast mercy encompasses everything. A sincere return to Him in a brief moment is sufficient to turn His entire gaze of grace toward a human and draw the pen of forgiveness over their past sins.

It is possible that placing the attribute Al-Aziz before Ar-Rahim here is because if Ar-Rahim had come first, it might have suggested a sense of weakness. However, He places Al-Aziz first so it becomes clear that He is extremely Kind while possessing ultimate Power.

Ayahs

وَإِذْ نَادَىٰ رَبُّكَ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنِ ٱئْتِ ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (١٠)
When your Lord called out to Moses: [saying,] ‘Go to those wrongdoing people, (10)
قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ ۚ أَلَا يَتَّقُونَ (١١)
the people of Pharaoh. Will they not be wary [of Allah]?’ (11)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ أَن يُكَذِّبُونِ (١٢)
He said, ‘My Lord! I fear they will impugn me, (12)
وَيَضِيقُ صَدْرِى وَلَا يَنطَلِقُ لِسَانِى فَأَرْسِلْ إِلَىٰ هَـٰرُونَ (١٣)
and I will become upset and my tongue will fail me. So send for Aaron [to join me]. (13)
وَلَهُمْ عَلَىَّ ذَنۢبٌ فَأَخَافُ أَن يَقْتُلُونِ (١٤)
Besides, they have a charge against me, and I fear they will kill me.’ (14)
قَالَ كَلَّا ۖ فَٱذْهَبَا بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ ۖ إِنَّا مَعَكُم مُّسْتَمِعُونَ (١٥)
He said, ‘Certainly not! Let both of you go with Our signs: We will indeed be with you, hearing [everything]. (15)

Commentary

The Beginning of Moses’ (عليه السلام) Mission

We have stated that in this Surah, the life stories of seven great Prophets are recounted as an instructive lesson for all Muslims, especially the early Muslims.

It begins first with Moses (عليه السلام) and describes various stages of his life and his struggle with the people of Pharaoh until the moment that tyrannical and oppressive folk were drowned.

Thus far, in several Suras of the Quran (Surah Al-Baqarah, Al-Ma'idah, Al-A'raf, Yunus, Al-Isra, and Ta-Ha), mention has been made of the Children of Israel, Moses, and the Pharaohs, and discussions on this subject will also appear in some subsequent Suras.

Although these discussions may appear repetitive on the surface, a careful examination shows that in each instance, emphasis is placed on a specific part of this eventful history for a particular objective.

For example, the verses under discussion were revealed at a time when the Muslims were in a severe minority and their enemies were very strong and powerful, such that there was no balance of power between them. Here, it is necessary for Allah to recount similar stories of previous nations so they may know that the enemy's immense power and their own apparent weakness will never lead to defeat; this strengthens their morale and increases their steadfastness and persistence. Interestingly, after the story of each of these seven Prophets, the phrase: "وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ" ("And most of them were not believers. And indeed, your Lord is the Exalted in Might, the Merciful") is repeated. This is exactly the same expression we read at the beginning of this Surah regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله). This harmony is living proof of the fact that the mention of these portions of the Prophets' stories was due to the specific psychological and social conditions of the Muslims at that particular juncture of time and similar situations.

First, He says: "And remember when your Lord called Moses, saying: 'Go to the people who are wrongdoers'" (وَإِذْ نادى‌ رَبُّكَ مُوسى‌ أَنِ ائْتِ الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ‌).

"The people of Pharaoh; will they not fear [Allah]?" (قَوْمَ فِرْعَوْنَ أَ لا يَتَّقُونَ‌). It is noteworthy that the only attribute of the Pharaohs emphasized here is "oppression" (ظلم). We know that oppression has a comprehensive and broad meaning, of which polytheism (shirk) is one of the most prominent examples (إِنَّ الشِّرْكَ لَظُلْمٌ عَظِيمٌ—Luqman, verse 13). The exploitation and enslavement of the Children of Israel, with all that agony and torture, is another manifestation of it. Beyond this, through their deviant actions, they oppressed themselves before anyone else. Thus, the goal of the Prophets' invitation can be summarized as the struggle against oppression and injustice in all its dimensions!

At this moment, Moses presents his great difficulties before the Lord and requests more strength and power from Him to bear this momentous mission: "He said: 'My Lord, indeed I fear that they will deny me'" (قالَ رَبِّ إِنِّي أَخافُ أَنْ يُكَذِّبُونِ‌).

And that before I can bring my mission to completion, they might drive me from the scene with their clamor and denials, and this time the goal might not be reached.

Moses (عليه السلام) was entirely justified in saying this, because Pharaoh and his clique were so dominant over the affairs of Egypt that no one dared to oppose them, and they suppressed any voice of dissent with severity and ruthlessness.

Furthermore, "my breast is straitened [and I do not have sufficient capacity for this mission]" (وَ يَضِيقُ صَدْرِي‌).

Moreover, "my tongue is not eloquent enough" (وَ لا يَنْطَلِقُ لِسانِي‌).

For this reason, my request is: "send for Aaron [my brother] as well [so that he may be commissioned alongside me to fulfill this mission]" (فَأَرْسِلْ إِلى‌ هارُونَ‌) [1], so that with mutual support, we may carry out this great command against those headstrong oppressors.

Beyond all this, "they have a charge of a crime against me (according to their own belief)" (وَ لَهُمْ عَلَيَّ ذَنْبٌ‌). I killed one of those oppressive Pharaohs with a decisive blow when he was fighting with an oppressed man from the Children of Israel.

From this perspective, "I fear they will kill me [as retribution]" and this great mission will not be completed (فَأَخافُ أَنْ يقتلونِ‌).

In reality, Moses saw four major obstacles in the path of this great mission and asked Allah to solve them: (the problem of denial, the problem of a straitened breast, the problem of insufficient eloquence, and the problem of retribution).

Incidentally, it became clear that Moses did not fear for his own person, but rather feared being struck down before reaching the goal; therefore, he requested more strength from Allah for this struggle.

The type of means that Moses requested from Allah in this regard is a clear testament to

[1]: This sentence actually implies a hidden part, which is: "So send Gabriel to Aaron."

this fact. He requested "expansion of the breast" (a vast and open spirit), and also requested the loosening of any knot from his tongue, and the commissioning of his brother Aaron to participate in this great task, as stated more elaborately in Surah Ta-Ha: رَبِّ اشْرَحْ لِي صَدْرِي وَ يَسِّرْ لِي أَمْرِي وَ احْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِنْ لِسانِي يَفْقَهُوا قَوْلِي وَ اجْعَلْ لِي وَزِيراً مِنْ أَهْلِي هارُونَ أَخِي اشْدُدْ بِهِ أَزْرِي وَ أَشْرِكْهُ فِي أَمْرِي كَيْ نُسَبِّحَكَ كَثِيراً وَ نَذْكُرَكَ كَثِيراً: "My Lord, expand for me my breast, and ease for me my task, and untie the knot from my tongue that they may understand my speech. And appoint for me a minister from my family—Aaron, my brother. Increase through him my strength and let him share in my task, that we may exalt You much and remember You much" (Surah Ta-Ha, verses 25 to 34).

Allah granted this sincere request of Moses: "He said: 'Nay, [it is not as you fear; they will not be able to kill you, nor will your breast be straitened, nor will your tongue remain knotted and unable to speak]'" (قالَ كَلَّا). I have also granted your prayer regarding your brother and have commissioned him as well. "Go, both of you, with Our signs" (and invite Pharaoh and his misguided people toward Me) (فَاذْهَبا بِآياتِنا).

And do not think that I am far from you or that the course of your affairs is hidden from Us; rather, "Indeed, We are with you, listening" (إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ مُسْتَمِعُونَ‌).

I will never leave you alone and will assist you in difficult events. Proceed with confidence and step firmly on this path.

In this way, within three sentences, Allah gave Moses (عليه السلام) sufficient assurance and fulfilled his request. With the expression "كَلَّا" (Nay), He assured him that they would not gain the power to kill him, and that no problem would arise regarding the straitening of his breast or the lack of eloquence in his tongue. With the phrase "فَاذْهَبا بِآياتِنا", He sent his brother to help him, and finally, with the phrase "إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ مُسْتَمِعُونَ‌", He promised them that He would take them under the umbrella of His protection everywhere.

It is noteworthy that in the last sentence, the pronoun is used in the plural form, saying "إِنَّا مَعَكُمْ‌" (We are with you [all]). This expression might be an indication that in all the meetings and arenas where you face this tyrannical and rebellious group, We are present and We hear the words of all of you, and We will help you two brothers and make you victorious over them.

The notion held by some that because the word "with" (مع) implies support and assistance, it cannot include the Pharaohs, is a mistake. Rather, this word signifies the permanent presence of the Lord in all scenes; He is everywhere and always, even with the sinners and even with inanimate objects, and no place is empty of Him.

The use of the term "listening" (استماع), which implies listening with attention, is also an emphasis on this reality.

Ayahs

فَأْتِيَا فِرْعَوْنَ فَقُولَآ إِنَّا رَسُولُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦)
So approach Pharaoh and say, ‘‘We are indeed envoys of the Lord of the worlds (16)
أَنْ أَرْسِلْ مَعَنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (١٧)
that you let the Children of Israel leave with us.’’ ’ (17)
قَالَ أَلَمْ نُرَبِّكَ فِينَا وَلِيدًا وَلَبِثْتَ فِينَا مِنْ عُمُرِكَ سِنِينَ (١٨)
He [i.e. Pharaoh] said, ‘Did we not rear you as a child among us, and did you not stay with us for years of your life? (18)
وَفَعَلْتَ فَعْلَتَكَ ٱلَّتِى فَعَلْتَ وَأَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (١٩)
Then you committed that deed of yours, and you are an ingrate.’ (19)
قَالَ فَعَلْتُهَآ إِذًا وَأَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلضَّآلِّينَ (٢٠)
He said, ‘I did that when I was astray. (20)
فَفَرَرْتُ مِنكُمْ لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ فَوَهَبَ لِى رَبِّى حُكْمًا وَجَعَلَنِى مِنَ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (٢١)
So I fled from you, as I was afraid of you. Then my Lord gave me sound judgement and made me one of the apostles. (21)
وَتِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّهَا عَلَىَّ أَنْ عَبَّدتَّ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (٢٢)
That you have enslaved the Children of Israel—is that the favour with which you reproach me?’ (22)

Commentary

A Logical and Decisive Confrontation with Pharaoh

In the preceding verses, the first stage of Musa’s (عليه السلام) mission—namely, receiving revelation and the messengership, and requesting the means to achieve this great goal—came to an end.

Following that, the verses under discussion address the "second stage," which is the encounter with Pharaoh and the fateful dialogue that took place between them.

First, as an introduction, He says: "Now that everything is prepared, go to Pharaoh and say: 'Indeed, we are the messenger of the Lord of the worlds'" (فَأْتِيا فِرْعَوْنَ فَقُولا إِنَّا رَسُولُ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

The phrase "Go both of you" (فأتيا) indicates that they must make contact with him personally at any cost. The use of the word "Messenger" (رسول) in the singular form—despite the fact that both were prophets—points to the oneness and unity of their call; as if they are two souls in one body, with one program and one goal. [^1]

[^1]: Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Rasul" (رسول) is among the words applied to both "singular" and "plural," although sometimes it is pluralized as "Rusul" (رسل). Some have also considered it a verbal noun (Masdar) meaning "messengership," and we know that a verbal noun does not have dual or plural forms (in Lisan al-Arab we read: "Al-Rasul" means "Al-Risalah"). However, certainly, this word usually conveys a descriptive meaning, and therefore in many instances, it has been used in dual and plural forms. In this very story of Musa and Harun in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 47, He says: "إِنَّا رَسُولا رَبِّكَ" (Indeed, we are two messengers of your Lord).

Following the declaration of their messengership, demand the freedom of the Children of Israel and "say: 'We are commanded to ask you to send the Children of Israel with us'" (أَنْ أَرْسِلْ مَعَنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ).

Clearly, the intention was: "Remove the chains of captivity and slavery from them so they may be free and able to come with us," not that Pharaoh himself was requested to physically send them.

At this point, Pharaoh began to speak, using calculated yet malicious sentences to attempt to negate their messengership. First, he turned to Musa and said: "Did we not bring you up among us as a child?!" (قالَ أَ لَمْ نُرَبِّكَ فِينا وَلِيداً). "We took you from those surging and angry waves of the Nile that threatened your existence with destruction; we invited wet nurses for you, and exempted you from the law of executing the male children of the Israelites. You were raised in a safe and secure environment in luxury and comfort! And after that, 'you stayed among us for many years of your life'!" (وَ لَبِثْتَ فِينا مِنْ عُمُرِكَ سِنِينَ).

Then he moved to another objection against Musa, saying: "And you did that deed of yours which you did" (وَ فَعَلْتَ فَعْلَتَكَ الَّتِي فَعَلْتَ). This was an allusion to: "How can you be a prophet when you have such a record?"

And beyond all this, "you are of the ungrateful" (وَ أَنْتَ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ).

"For years you sat at our table, you ate our salt and then broke the saltshaker! With such ingratitude for favors, how can you be a prophet?"

In reality, he wanted to condemn Musa with this logic and this kind of character assassination.

The "killing" refers to the event mentioned in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 15, where Musa (عليه السلام) saw two men—one an Egyptian and the other an Israelite—fighting. He rose to support the oppressed one (the Israelite) and struck the oppressor, who died from that single blow.

After hearing Pharaoh’s malicious words, Musa (عليه السلام) proceeded to answer all three objections, but due to its importance, he prioritized the answer to Pharaoh’s second objection (or perhaps he did not consider the first objection worthy of a response, as raising someone is never a reason not to guide them to the right path if the nurturer is astray). In any case, he "said: 'I did it then, while I was of those astray'" (قالَ فَعَلْتُها إِذاً وَ أَنَا مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ). Here, there has been much discussion among commentators regarding the expression "those astray" (الضالين). On one hand, we know that a bad record for a Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), even before reaching the station of prophethood, is unacceptable because it would undermine his standing in public opinion and the purpose of the mission would remain incomplete; for this reason, the scope of the Prophets' infallibility (Ismah) includes the period before their prophethood.

On the other hand, this must be an answer that Pharaoh cannot refute.

Therefore, a group of commentators believes that "astray" (ضال) here refers to an error in the subject matter; meaning: "I did not strike that Egyptian man with the intent to kill, but rather to support the oppressed, and I did not know it would lead to his death." Thus, "astray" here means "unaware," referring to being unaware of the consequence of the action.

Others have said it means that no error occurred in killing that oppressor since he deserved it, but rather: "I did not know the consequence of this act would be such that I could not stay in Egypt, becoming a displaced person from my homeland for a time, and delaying my plans."

However, apparently, this was not an answer Musa could give to Pharaoh; it was something he could explain to his friends, not an acceptable answer for Pharaoh.

A third interpretation, which is perhaps more suitable in some respects to the status of Musa (عليه السلام) and the greatness of his character, is that Musa (عليه السلام) used a form of Tawriyah (equivocation) here. He spoke words whose outward appearance suggested: "At that time, I had not found the path of truth, then Allah showed me the path of truth and granted me the station of messengership," but inwardly he had another intention: "I did not know this act would cause so much trouble, otherwise the act itself was right and in accordance with the law of justice" (or: "On the day this incident occurred, I had lost my way and arrived at the place where this incident happened").

We know that Tawriyah means a person says something that is inwardly true, but the listener understands something else from its outward appearance. This is specific to cases where a person is in a tight spot and wishes not to lie while still maintaining appearances. [^1]

[^1]: This statement is consistent with the content of a Hadith narrated from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida (عليه السلام) regarding the interpretation of this verse in the book Uyun Akhbar al-Rida, as cited in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 48.

Then Musa adds: "So I fled from you when I feared you, then my Lord granted me wisdom and made me one of the messengers" (فَفَرَرْتُ مِنْكُمْ لَمَّا خِفْتُكُمْ فَوَهَبَ لِي رَبِّي حُكْماً وَ جَعَلَنِي مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ).

What is meant by "wisdom" (حكم) in this verse? Is it the station of prophethood itself, or the station of knowledge and awareness? There is discussion among commentators, but considering the end of this very verse, which places the station of "messengership" (رسالت) against the station of "wisdom" (حكم), it becomes clear that it is something other than messengership and prophethood.

Another witness to this is verse 79 of Surah Al-Imran, which says:

"It is not for a human that Allah should give him the Scripture and authority (wisdom) and prophethood and then he should say to the people, 'Be servants to me rather than Allah'..." (ما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُؤْتِيَهُ اللَّهُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحُكْمَ وَ النُّبُوَّةَ ثُمَّ يَقُولَ لِلنَّاسِ كُونُوا عِباداً لِي مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ).

Fundamentally, the word "Hukm" (حكم) linguistically means "to prevent for the sake of reform," and thus the "bit of a horse" is called "Hikmah" (حكمة). Subsequently, this word was applied to stating something in accordance with wisdom, and similarly, knowledge and intellect have been called "Hukm" due to this connection. It might be said that it appears from verse 14 of Surah Al-Qasas that Musa had reached the station of "wisdom and knowledge" before this incident, where it says: "And when he attained his full strength and was established, We gave him wisdom and knowledge" (وَ لَمَّا بَلَغَ أَشُدَّهُ وَ اسْتَوى‌ آتَيْناهُ حُكْماً وَ عِلْماً) (then it mentions the incident of the struggle with the Egyptian man in the following verses).

In response, we say: Knowledge and wisdom have different stages. Musa had attained one stage previously, and he attained a more perfect stage at the time of his prophethood and messengership.

Then Musa proceeded to respond to the favor Pharaoh claimed regarding his upbringing during childhood and youth. With a decisive and reproachful tone, he said: "And is this a favor of which you remind me—that you have enslaved the Children of Israel?!" (وَ تِلْكَ نِعْمَةٌ تَمُنُّها عَلَيَّ أَنْ عَبَّدْتَ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ).

It is true that the hand of fate brought me to your palace as a child and I was inevitably raised in your care—and in this was a manifestation of God's power—but look at what the cause of this event was. Why was I not raised in my father's house and in my mother's arms? Why?

Was it not because you enslaved the Children of Israel to the point that you allowed yourself to slaughter the newborn boys and keep the girls alive for bondage and service?

This boundless oppression of yours caused my mother to place me in a chest to save her infant's life and entrust me to the waves of the Nile, and the Divine Will was that that small vessel should anchor beside your palace. Yes, it was your excessive oppression that made me indebted to this "favor," deprived me of my father's pure home, and placed me in your polluted palace! With this interpretation, the connection between Musa’s response and Pharaoh’s question becomes perfectly clear.

There is also the possibility in interpreting the verse that Musa (عليه السلام) meant: "If my upbringing is a favor from you, compared to all that oppression and tyranny you inflicted upon the Children of Israel, it is a drop in the ocean. What kind of favor is this that you mention while so much oppression and tyranny exist alongside it?!" A third interpretation for Musa’s (عليه السلام) response to Pharaoh’s claim is: "If I was raised in your palace and enjoyed various blessings, do not forget that the actual builders of that palace were the slaves of my people, and the creators of all those blessings were the captives of the Children of Israel. How can you claim a favor over me for using the fruits of my people's labor?!" These three interpretations are not mutually exclusive, although the first interpretation seems clearer in some respects.

Additionally, the expression "one of the messengers" (مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ) points to the fact that: "I am not the only messenger and envoy of God; many prophets have come before me and I am one of them, yet you have forgotten them all."

Ayahs

قَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَمَا رَبُّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٢٣)
He said, ‘And what is ‘‘the Lord of all the worlds?’’ ’ (23)
قَالَ رَبُّ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُم مُّوقِنِينَ (٢٤)
He said, ‘The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them,—should you have conviction.’ (24)
قَالَ لِمَنْ حَوْلَهُۥٓ أَلَا تَسْتَمِعُونَ (٢٥)
He said to those who were around him, ‘Don’t you hear?!’ (25)
قَالَ رَبُّكُمْ وَرَبُّ ءَابَآئِكُمُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٢٦)
He said, ‘Your Lord, and the Lord of your forefathers!’ (26)
قَالَ إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ ٱلَّذِىٓ أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ (٢٧)
He said, ‘Indeed your messenger, who has been sent to you, is surely crazy!’ (27)
قَالَ رَبُّ ٱلْمَشْرِقِ وَٱلْمَغْرِبِ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَآ ۖ إِن كُنتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ (٢٨)
He said, ‘The Lord of the east and the west and whatever is between them—should you exercise your reason.’ (28)
قَالَ لَئِنِ ٱتَّخَذْتَ إِلَـٰهًا غَيْرِى لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ مِنَ ٱلْمَسْجُونِينَ (٢٩)
He said, ‘If you take up any god other than me, I will surely make you a prisoner!’ (29)

Commentary

The Accusation of Insanity and the Threat of Imprisonment

When Musa (peace be upon him) responded to Pharaoh’s words with a decisive and crushing tone, and Pharaoh found himself helpless in that regard, he changed the course of the conversation. He questioned Musa, who had claimed, "I am the messenger and envoy of the Lord of the worlds," and "Pharaoh said: 'And what is the Lord of the worlds?'" (قالَ فِرْعَوْنُ وَ ما رَبُّ الْعالَمِينَ‌).

It is highly unlikely that Pharaoh asked this to actually understand the matter; rather, it appears he raised this question out of feigned ignorance and to show contempt.

However, in any case, Musa, like all vigilant and aware debaters, had no choice but to take the matter seriously and provide a serious response. Since the Essence of God is beyond the reach of human thoughts, he reached out to His signs across the expanse of creation and spoke of the horizons: "He said: 'The Lord of the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them, if you would but be certain'" (قالَ رَبُّ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُمَا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُوقِنِينَ‌). The heavens with their grandeur, the earth with its vastness and its diverse creatures—before which you and your apparatus are nothing more than an insignificant speck—are the creation of

my Lord. Such a Creator, Director, and Organizer is worthy of worship, not a weak and insignificant being like you! It is also necessary to note that the idolaters believed that every entity in this world had its own "lord" (rubb), and they viewed the world as a combination of scattered systems. However, the words of Musa (peace be upon him) point to the fact that this single system governing the entire universe is proof that it has a "Single Lord."

The phrase "if you would but be certain" (إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُوقِنِينَ‌) may be an allusion by Musa to Pharaoh and his companions that: "I know your goal in asking this is not to perceive the truth; however, if you were seekers of truth and possessed intellect and consciousness, this argument I have made is sufficient. Open your eyes for a moment and reflect upon these vast heavens, the expansive earth, and its signs, so that you may know what is to be known and correct your worldview."

Yet, Pharaoh did not wake from the sleep of negligence despite this firm explanation from the great heavenly teacher. He continued his mockery and ridicule, following the age-old method of arrogant tyrants: "He said to those around him: 'Do you not hear [what he says]?'" (قالَ لِمَنْ حَوْلَهُ أَ لا تَسْتَمِعُونَ‌). It is clear what kind of people surrounded Pharaoh: individuals of his own kind, a group of the wealthy and powerful, and collaborators in oppression and injustice.

Ibn Abbas states that those around him there were five hundred people who were considered the elite of his folk. [^1] His goal was to ensure that this logical and heart-reaching speech of Musa would not leave

[^1]: Tafsir Abu al-Futuh Razi, under the verse being discussed.

the slightest impression on the dark hearts of this group, and to present it as a hollow speech whose meaning is incomprehensible.

But again, Musa (peace be upon him) continued his logical and calculated words without any fear or apprehension and "said: '[He is] your Lord and the Lord of your first forefathers'" (قالَ رَبُّكُمْ وَ رَبُّ آبائِكُمُ الْأَوَّلِينَ‌). In truth, Musa, who had started with "signs in the horizons" in the first stage, pointed to "signs within the self" in the second stage. He addressed the secrets of creation within the existence of humans themselves and the effects of Divine upbringing and the Lordship of the Creator in the human soul and body, so that these oblivious, arrogant people might at least think about themselves and gain some self-knowledge, and subsequently, knowledge of their God.

But Pharaoh continued his stubbornness and went beyond the stage of mockery and ridicule, attributing insanity and madness to Musa and "said: 'Indeed, your messenger who has been sent to you is surely mad!'" (قالَ إِنَّ رَسُولَكُمُ الَّذِي أُرْسِلَ إِلَيْكُمْ لَمَجْنُونٌ‌). This is the same accusation that all tyrants of history leveled against Divine reformers.

It is interesting that this arrogant deceiver was not even willing to say "our messenger" or "sent to us," but rather said "your messenger" who has been "sent to you." This is because even the expression "your messenger" was intended as mockery—a mockery coupled with a sense of superiority, implying "I am too high for a messenger to come to invite me." His goal in attributing madness to Musa (peace be upon him) was to neutralize the effect of his powerful logic on the minds of those present.

However, this unjust accusation had no effect on the lofty spirit of Musa (peace be upon him), and he continued the main line of Monotheism (Tawhid) through the signs of God in the expanse of creation, in the horizons and the souls, and "said: '[He is] the Lord of the East and the West and whatever is between them, if you would but use your reason'" (قالَ رَبُّ الْمَشْرِقِ وَ الْمَغْرِبِ وَ ما بَيْنَهُما إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ‌).

If you have an apparent rule over a small territory called Egypt, the real sovereignty of my Lord encompasses the entire East and West of the world and everything between them, and His signs shine everywhere upon the forehead of all beings. Fundamentally, this very rising and setting of the sun in the East and West, and the system governing it, is itself a sign of His greatness. But the flaw here is that you do not exercise reason and are not accustomed to thinking at all. (Note that the phrase "if you would but use your reason" points to the fact that if you had a practice of reasoning in your life, past or present, you would have perceived this truth).

In fact, Musa (peace be upon him) responded to the accusation of madness in a beautiful way: "I am not mad; the mad and mindless one is he who does not see all these signs of the Lord." As the poet says: "Every person who does not reflect upon all these wondrous designs upon the doors and walls of existence is himself but a design upon a wall!" It is true that Musa (peace be upon him) first referred to the management of the heavens and the earth, but since the heavens are very high and the earth is very mysterious, in the final stage he pointed to something that no one has the power to deny and which humans deal with every day: the system of the rising and setting of the sun and the precise program within it, which no one can claim to be the organizer of.

The expression "whatever is between them" (ما بَيْنَهُما) refers to the unity and connection between the East and the West, just as this expression was used regarding the heavens and the earth. Similarly, in "your Lord and the Lord of your first forefathers," it expresses the connection and unity of

generations.

This powerful and invincible logic made Pharaoh extremely angry, and he finally resorted to the weapon that all illogical men of power resort to upon defeat and failure, and "said: 'If you take a god other than me, I will surely place you among the prisoners'" (قالَ لئِنِ اتَّخَذْتَ إِلهَاً غَيْرِي لَأَجْعَلَنَّكَ مِنَ الْمَسْجُونِينَ‌). "I do not understand these words of yours; I only know that one great god exists, and that is me! And whoever says otherwise is condemned to death or a death-dealing prison!"

Some commentators believe that the "Alif and Lam" (the definite article) in "al-Masjunin" (the prisoners) is for "ahd" (referring to something known), pointing to a specific prison where anyone thrown into it would remain forever until their corpse was brought out. [^1] In fact, with this sharp speech and tyrannical threat, Pharaoh wanted to silence Musa (peace be upon him), because the continuation of these discussions would lead to the awakening of the people, and for tyrants, nothing is more dangerous than the awakening and alertness of the people!

[^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Fakhr Razi, and Ruh al-Ma'ani, under the verse being discussed.

Ayahs

قَالَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكَ بِشَىْءٍ مُّبِينٍ (٣٠)
He said, ‘What if I bring you something [as an] unmistakable [proof]?’ (30)
قَالَ فَأْتِ بِهِۦٓ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (٣١)
He said, ‘Then bring it, should you be truthful.’ (31)
فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ ثُعْبَانٌ مُّبِينٌ (٣٢)
Thereat he threw down his staff, and behold, it was a manifest python. (32)
وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُۥ فَإِذَا هِىَ بَيْضَآءُ لِلنَّـٰظِرِينَ (٣٣)
Then he drew out his hand, and behold, it was white and bright to the onlookers. (33)
قَالَ لِلْمَلَإِ حَوْلَهُۥٓ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسَـٰحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ (٣٤)
He said to the elite [who stood] around him, ‘This is indeed an expert magician (34)
يُرِيدُ أَن يُخْرِجَكُم مِّنْ أَرْضِكُم بِسِحْرِهِۦ فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ (٣٥)
who seeks to expel you from your land with his magic. So what do you advise?’ (35)
قَالُوٓا۟ أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ وَٱبْعَثْ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ (٣٦)
They said, ‘Put him and his brother off for a while, and send heralds to the cities (36)
يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ (٣٧)
to bring you every expert magician.’ (37)

Commentary

Your Country is in Danger, Arise!

In the preceding verses, we saw how Musa (peace be upon him) maintained his logical superiority over Pharaoh, demonstrating to those present the extent to which his creed was rooted in reason and intellect, while Pharaoh’s claims were flimsy and hollow—alternating between mockery, accusations of madness, and finally, relying on power and threats of imprisonment and death.

At this point, the scene shifts, and Musa (peace be upon him) must also adopt a new approach so that Pharaoh is rendered helpless and incapacitated in this arena as well.

Musa must also rely on power—a Divine power originating from a striking miracle. He turned to Pharaoh and said: "Even if I bring you a clear sign of my mission, will you still imprison me?" (قَالَ أَوَلَوْ جِئْتُكَ بِشَيْءٍ مُبِينٍ).

Pharaoh found himself in a severe deadlock here, because Musa (peace be upon him) made a veiled reference to an extraordinary program, capturing the attention of those present. If Pharaoh were to ignore his words, everyone would object, saying he must allow Musa (peace be upon him) to present his significant matter. If he possesses the ability, it will become clear that he cannot be opposed; otherwise, his exaggeration will be exposed. In any case, Musa's (peace be upon him) statement could not be easily dismissed. Inevitably, "[Pharaoh] said: 'Bring it then, if you are of the truthful!'" (قَالَ فَأْتِ بِهِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ).

"At this moment, Musa (peace be upon him) threw down the staff he held in his hand, and (by the command of the Lord) it became a manifest, massive serpent" (فَأَلْقَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِيَ ثُعْبَانٌ مُبِينٌ).

"Then he drew out his hand from his garment, and suddenly it was white and radiant to the beholders!" (وَنَزَعَ يَدَهُ فَإِذَا هِيَ بَيْضَاءُ لِلنَّاظِرِينَ). In reality, these two great miracles—one being a manifestation of awe and the other a manifestation of hope—the first is suited for the station of warning (indhar), and the second for glad tidings (bisharat). One represents Divine punishment, and the other is light and a sign of mercy, for a miracle must be harmonious with the Prophet's call.

"Thu'ban" means a massive snake, which is referred to as a "dragon" (azhdaha) in Persian.

Raghib in Al-Mufradat suggests that this word might be derived from the root "tha'b," meaning the flow of water, because the movement of this animal resembles streams that move in a zigzagging, winding fashion.

The description "mubin" (manifest/clear) may refer to the fact that the staff had truly transformed into a massive serpent, and there was no optical illusion, sleight of hand, or act like the magic of sorcerers involved.

It is also necessary to note that here the term "thu'ban" is used, while in verse 10 of Surah Al-Naml and verse 31 of Surah Al-Qasas, the term "jann" (small snakes that move with speed and agility) is used, and in Surah Taha verse 20, the term "hayyah" (meaning snake, derived from the root for life) is employed.

This variation in terminology might seem questioning at first glance, but in reality, it is to express one of two points:

It may refer to the different states of that serpent: initially, the "staff" turned into a small, slender snake, which gradually grew and transformed into a dragon.

Or, each of these three words refers to one of the characteristics of that serpent: "thu'ban" refers to its greatness, "jann" refers to its speed and agility, and "hayyah" refers to its being alive.

Pharaoh was deeply shaken by witnessing this scene and fell into a profound state of terror. However, to preserve his satanic power—which was severely endangered by the appearance of Musa (peace be upon him)—and also to maintain the faith of his entourage and boost their morale, he sought to justify Musa's miracles. First, "he said to the chiefs around him: 'Indeed, this is a learned and skilled sorcerer!'" (قَالَ لِلْمَلَإِ حَوْلَهُ إِنَّ هَٰذَا لَسَاحِرٌ عَلِيمٌ). The very person he called "mad" just moments ago, he now refers to as "learned" ('alim)! Such is the way of tyrants: they sometimes change faces several times in a single session, grasping at a new pretext every moment to reach their goal.

Pharaoh thought that since magic was prevalent at that time, this accusation and label would stick to Musa better than anything else after showing these miracles.

Then, to mobilize the crowd against him, he continued: "He wants to drive you out of your land with his magic!" (يُرِيدُ أَنْ يُخْرِجَكُمْ مِنْ أَرْضِكُمْ بِسِحْرِهِ). "What do you advise and what do you command?" (فَمَاذَا تَأْمُرُونَ).

This is the same Pharaoh who previously considered all of Egypt his undisputed property, saying: A laysa li mulku Misr: "Is not the kingdom and ownership of this land of Egypt mine?" Now that he sees the pillars of his throne shaking, he completely forgets his absolute ownership of this land and considers it the property of the people, saying: "Your land is in danger; devise a plan!" The Pharaoh who, until an hour ago, was not willing to listen to anyone and was always the absolute commander and undisputed authority, is now so helpless that he asks his entourage, "What do you command?" A very desperate consultation from a position of weakness!

From verse 110 of Surah Al-A'raf, it appears that the entourage also began consulting among themselves, and they were so panicked that they lost the power of thought, each turning to the other and saying: "What do you command?" Yes, such is the tradition of tyrants in every age and time: when they are in control of affairs, they consider everything their own and everyone their slaves, understanding nothing but the logic of despotism.

But when they see the pillars of their throne of injustice shaking and their government in danger, they temporarily step down from the throne of despotism and appeal to the people and their opinions. They call the country the people's country and the land and soil theirs, respecting their views. However, once the storm subsides, it is the same old story.

In our own era, we saw the remnants of past monarchs: how, when the world went according to their desires, they considered the entire country their private estate, even ordering those who did not wish to join their party to leave the country, saying God's earth is vast and go wherever you want—here, it is only the program I dictate and nothing else! But we saw how much they bowed before the people when the storm of revolution blew, even repenting for their past sins and requesting forgiveness. Yet, it was of no use against a people who had known them well for many years.

After consultations, the entourage finally "said to Pharaoh: 'Give Musa and his brother a respite and do not hasten in their matter, and send officials to all the cities of Egypt for mobilization'" (قَالُوا أَرْجِهْ وَأَخَاهُ وَابْعَثْ فِي الْمَدَائِنِ حَاشِرِينَ) [1].

"So that they may bring to you every skilled and veteran sorcerer" (يَأْتُوكَ بِكُلِّ سَحَّارٍ عَلِيمٍ). In reality, Pharaoh's entourage were either deceived or consciously accepted his accusation against Musa, framing the plan such that he is a "sorcerer," and against a sorcerer, one must appeal to "sahhar"—meaning more skilled sorcerers! They said that fortunately, in the vast country of Egypt, masters of the art of magic are many; if Musa is a sorcerer, we will place "skilled sorcerers" against him, and we will gather so many sorcerers expert in the tricks of magic that they will expose Musa's secret!

"Hashirin" is from the root "hashr," meaning to mobilize a group of people toward a battlefield or the like. Thus, the officials were to mobilize the sorcerers at any cost possible to combat Musa (peace be upon him).

[1]: "Arjih" is from the root "irja’," meaning to delay and not to hasten in judgment. The pronoun at the end refers to Musa. Originally it was "arji’hu," and the hamza was omitted for ease of pronunciation.

Ayahs

فَجُمِعَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ لِمِيقَـٰتِ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (٣٨)
So the magicians were gathered for the tryst of a known day, (38)
وَقِيلَ لِلنَّاسِ هَلْ أَنتُم مُّجْتَمِعُونَ (٣٩)
and the people were told: ‘Will you all gather?!’ (39)
لَعَلَّنَا نَتَّبِعُ ٱلسَّحَرَةَ إِن كَانُوا۟ هُمُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (٤٠)
‘Maybe we will follow the magicians, if they are victors!’ (40)
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ قَالُوا۟ لِفِرْعَوْنَ أَئِنَّ لَنَا لَأَجْرًا إِن كُنَّا نَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبِينَ (٤١)
So when the magicians came, they said to Pharaoh, ‘Shall we have a reward if we were to be the victors?’ (41)
قَالَ نَعَمْ وَإِنَّكُمْ إِذًا لَّمِنَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبِينَ (٤٢)
He said, ‘Of course; and you will be among members of my inner circle.’ (42)

Commentary

The Sorcerers Gathered from Everywhere

In these verses, another scene of this eventful story is depicted:

Following the suggestion of Pharaoh's inner circle, a group of elite officials was dispatched to various cities in Egypt to search for skilled sorcerers in every corner. "Finally, the sorcerers were gathered for the appointment of a known day" (فَجُمِعَ السَّحَرَةُ لِمِيقاتِ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ‌).

In other words, they prepared them in advance for such a day so that they could be dispatched to the field of combat at the appointed time.

The meaning of "a known day" (يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ‌), as understood from the verses of Surah Al-A'raf, was one of the famous festival days of the Egyptians, which Musa عليه السلام had designated for the contest. His objective was for the people to have a greater opportunity to be present at the scene, as He was confident in His victory and wanted the power of Divine signs and the weakness of Pharaoh and his assistants to become manifest to everyone, so that the light of faith might shine in the hearts of a larger group.

The people were also invited to attend this field of combat: "And it was said to the people: 'Will you also gather?'" (وَ قِيلَ لِلنَّاسِ هَلْ أَنْتُمْ مُجْتَمِعُونَ‌). This expression shows that Pharaoh's officials were working very calculatedly in this regard. They knew that if they forced the people to attend, they might show a negative reaction, as every human being is naturally averse to compulsion. Therefore, they said: "If you are inclined, attend this gathering," and certainly, this manner of speaking drew a larger number of people to that assembly.

And it was said to the people: "The goal is that if the sorcerers are victorious—since their victory is the victory of our gods—we may follow them," and make the scene so intense and heated that the enemy of our gods is driven out of the field forever! (لَعَلَّنا نَتَّبِعُ السَّحَرَةَ إِنْ كانُوا هُمُ الْغالِبِينَ‌).

It is clear that the presence of more spectators who have a connection and bond with one side of the contest serves both to encourage them and boost their morale, and causes them to exert their utmost effort. Furthermore, at the moment of victory, they have the ability to create such an uproar that the opponent is marginalized forever, and they can also instill fear in the opposing side from the very beginning of the contest.

Yes, Pharaoh's officials wished to bring the people to the scene with these hopes, and Musa also asked God for the presence of such a crowd so that He could implement His purpose in the most excellent way.

These were all on one side; on the other side, "when the sorcerers came to Pharaoh and saw him in a severe predicament, they thought of taking the greatest advantage and obtaining significant privileges from him. They said to Pharaoh: 'Will there be a considerable reward for us if we are the victors?'" (فَلَمَّا جاءَ السَّحَرَةُ قالُوا لِفِرْعَوْنَ أَ إِنَّ لَنا لَأَجْراً إِنْ كُنَّا نَحْنُ الْغالِبِينَ‌).

Pharaoh, who was severely trapped and helpless in this deadlock, was ready to give them the highest privileges. He immediately "said: 'Yes, I will give whatever you want; moreover, in that case, you will be among those near to me!'" (قالَ نَعَمْ وَ إِنَّكُمْ إِذاً لَمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ‌). In fact, Pharaoh said to them: "What do you want? Whether it is wealth or status, I will place both at your disposal!"

This expression shows to what extent proximity to Pharaoh was important in that environment and society, such that he mentions it as a great reward. In truth, there is no reward higher than for a person to become close to his desired power.

If the misguided considered proximity to Pharaoh as the highest reward, the aware God-worshippers do not consider any reward higher than proximity to the Lord. They would not trade even Paradise with all its blessings for a single manifestation of His Pure Essence.

For this very reason, the martyrs in the way of "Allah," who must receive the highest rewards for their great sacrifice, attain the reward of Divine proximity according to the testimony of the Quran, and the expression "with their Lord" (عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ‌) is a clear witness to this reality.

And also for this reason, every pure-hearted believer, when performing worship, seeks only one thing: "proximity to Allah" (قربة الى الله).

Ayahs

قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰٓ أَلْقُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُم مُّلْقُونَ (٤٣)
Moses said to them, ‘Throw down whatever you have to throw!’ (43)
فَأَلْقَوْا۟ حِبَالَهُمْ وَعِصِيَّهُمْ وَقَالُوا۟ بِعِزَّةِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ ٱلْغَـٰلِبُونَ (٤٤)
So they threw down their sticks and ropes, and said, ‘By the might of Pharaoh, we shall surely be victorious!’ (44)
فَأَلْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِىَ تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ (٤٥)
Thereat Moses threw down his staff, and behold, it was swallowing what they had faked. (45)
فَأُلْقِىَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ سَـٰجِدِينَ (٤٦)
Thereat the magicians fell down prostrating. (46)
قَالُوٓا۟ ءَامَنَّا بِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (٤٧)
They said, ‘We believe in the Lord of all the worlds, (47)
رَبِّ مُوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ (٤٨)
the Lord of Moses and Aaron.’ (48)
قَالَ ءَامَنتُمْ لَهُۥ قَبْلَ أَنْ ءَاذَنَ لَكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ ٱلَّذِى عَلَّمَكُمُ ٱلسِّحْرَ فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ ۚ لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُم مِّنْ خِلَـٰفٍ وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٤٩)
He [i.e. Pharaoh] said, ‘Do you profess faith in Him before I should permit you? He is indeed your chief who has taught you magic! Soon you will know! I will cut off your hands and feet from opposite sides, and I will crucify you all.’ (49)
قَالُوا۟ لَا ضَيْرَ ۖ إِنَّآ إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنقَلِبُونَ (٥٠)
They said, ‘[There is] no harm [in that]! Indeed, we shall return to our Lord. (50)
إِنَّا نَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لَنَا رَبُّنَا خَطَـٰيَـٰنَآ أَن كُنَّآ أَوَّلَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٥١)
Indeed we hope our Lord will forgive us our offences for being the first to believe.’ (51)

Commentary

The Light of Faith Began to Shine in the Hearts of the Sorcerers

When the sorcerers made their agreements and pacts with Pharaoh, he promised them rewards and proximity to his court, encouraging them and making them feel secure. Consequently, they went to prepare the preliminaries for the task. During the time they had, they gathered many ropes and staffs, which they had apparently hollowed out and filled with a specific chemical substance (such as mercury) that becomes light and volatile when exposed to the heat of the sun.

Finally, the promised day arrived, and a vast multitude of people gathered at the scene to witness this historic struggle. Pharaoh and his entourage on one side, the sorcerers on another, and Moses and his brother Aaron on the third side, were all present there.

However, as is the custom of the Quran, it omits these preliminaries—which become clear through the context of future discussions—and enters directly into the core of the narrative. Here, depicting this fateful scene, it says:

"Moses said to them: Throw what you are going to throw, and bring forth whatever you have to the field" (قَالَ لَهُمْ مُوسَىٰ أَلْقُوا مَا أَنْتُمْ مُلْقُونَ).

It is understood from verse 115 of Surah Al-A'raf that Moses said this when the sorcerers asked him whether he would take the lead and throw first, or should they.

This proposal by Moses عليه السلام, which stemmed from his confidence in victory and was evidence of his composure in the face of the vast multitude of enemies and the staunch supporters of Pharaoh, dealt the first blow to the sorcerers. It showed that Moses possessed a unique psychological tranquility and was reliant upon and heartened by another Power.

The sorcerers, who were drowned in pride and arrogance, having employed their maximum capability and being hopeful of their victory, "threw their ropes and their staffs and said: By the might of Pharaoh, we shall certainly be the victors!" (فَأَلْقَوْا حِبَالَهُمْ وَعِصِيَّهُمْ وَقَالُوا بِعِزَّةِ فِرْعَوْنَ إِنَّا لَنَحْنُ الْغَالِبُونَ) [^1]. Indeed, like all fawning flatterers, they began with the name of Pharaoh and relied upon his hollow power.

At this moment, as the Quran explains elsewhere, the specific staffs and ropes began to move in the form of small and large snakes (Surah Ta-Ha, verse 66). They had specifically chosen staffs as part of their magical tools

[^1]: "Hibal" is the plural of "Habl" (rhyming with Tabl) meaning rope, and "Isiyy" is the plural of "Asa" (staff).

to compete, in their imagination, with the staff of Moses, and they added ropes to further prove their superiority! At this moment, a shout of joy rose from the people, and a spark of hope began to shine in the eyes of Pharaoh and his entourage, such that they were bursting with joy and were moved to delight and cheer by witnessing this pleasurable scene!

But Moses did not allow this situation to continue for long. He stepped forward "and threw his staff; suddenly it became a huge serpent and began to swiftly swallow the false tools of the sorcerers! It gulped them down one after another into its throat!" (فَأَلْقَىٰ مُوسَىٰ عَصَاهُ فَإِذَا هِيَ تَلْقَفُ مَا يَأْفِكُونَ) [^1]. Here, at first, a fleeting silence reigned over the people; mouths remained open in wonder, eyes stopped moving as if they were frozen there. But soon, this silence was replaced by terrifying screams; a group fled, another group waited to see how the matter would end, and some were shouting aimlessly, while the sorcerers' mouths remained open in astonishment.

At this point, everything changed. The sorcerers, who until that moment were on the path of devilry, cooperating with Pharaoh and fighting against Moses عليه السلام, suddenly came to their senses. Since they were aware of all the intricacies and techniques of magic, they became certain that this

[^1]: "Talqaf" is from the root "Laqf" (rhyming with Saqf) meaning to take something quickly, whether with the hand or the mouth; and it is clear that here it means taking with the mouth. "Yafikun" is from the root "Ifk" (rhyming with Kidhb) meaning a lie, and here it refers to the false tools which they had passed off as small and large snakes.

matter was definitely not magic; it was a great divine miracle. "Suddenly, they all fell down in prostration" (فَأُلْقِيَ السَّحَرَةُ سَاجِدِينَ).

It is interesting that the Quran uses the expression "Ulqiya" (were cast down), which implies they were "thrown down." This indicates that they were so affected by the attraction of Moses' miracle that it was as if they fell to the ground and prostrated involuntarily.

And along with this action, which was clear evidence of their faith, they also said with their tongues: "We have believed in the Lord of the worlds!" (قَالُوا آمَنَّا بِرَبِّ الْعَالَمِينَ).

And so that no ambiguity or doubt remained, and so Pharaoh could not interpret this statement in any other way, they added: "The Lord of Moses and Aaron" (رَبِّ مُوسَىٰ وَهَارُونَ). This shows that although Moses performed the act of throwing the staff and the dialogue with the sorcerers, his brother Aaron stood beside him, ready to provide any support for his brother.

This extraordinary transformation that occurred in the spirit of the sorcerers—stepping from absolute darkness into a dazzling light in a short moment, and turning their backs on all the benefits they expected from Pharaoh, and even more, putting their own lives at risk—was all because they possessed knowledge and science. In the light of that knowledge, they were able to distinguish truth from falsehood and cling to the Truth.

They did not traverse the remainder of the path with the feet of "reason," for they had mounted the swift steed of "love." The scent of the Flower had so intoxicated them that they had lost their self-restraint. As we shall see, for this very reason, they showed the most courageous steadfastness in the face of Pharaoh's severest threats!

In a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read:

"There is no heart except that it is between two fingers of the fingers of the All-Merciful; if He wills, He keeps it upright, and if He wills, He causes it to deviate." [^1] (Naturally, God's will in these two stages stems from the readiness of individuals, and this granting or withholding of success is due to the different states of the hearts and is not without reason).

At this moment, Pharaoh, who on one hand had completely lost his morale and on the other hand saw all his power and existence in danger—and specifically knew what a deep impact the sorcerers' conversion would have on the people's morale, potentially causing a vast group to follow them in prostration—resorted to what he thought was a new initiative. He turned to the sorcerers and said: "Did you believe in him before I gave you permission?" (قَالَ آمَنْتُمْ لَهُ قَبْلَ أَنْ آذَنَ لَكُمْ) [^2]. He, who for many years had sat upon the throne of tyranny, not only expected that people should not perform any action without his permission, but his expectation was that the hearts, minds, and thoughts of the people should also be under his control and permission; that they should not think unless he commanded, and not decide until he allowed!

[^1]: Tafsir Fi Zilal, Vol. 6, p. 208.

[^2]: Here and in Surah Ta-Ha, verse 71, the expression "Amantum Lahu" is used, while in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 123, "Amantum Bihi" is mentioned. As some linguists have stated, when faith (Iman) is made transitive with "Lam," it conveys the meaning of submission/humility, and when it is made transitive with "Ba," it conveys the meaning of confirmation/belief.

And such is the way of tyrants.

This arrogant rebel was not even willing to utter the name of the Lord or the name of Moses; rather, he sufficed with the pronoun "Lahu" (in him/for him), which here is intended for belittlement.

But Pharaoh was not content with even this, and he uttered two more sentences to both consolidate his position in his own imagination and to block the awakened thoughts of the people and put them back to sleep:

First, he accused the sorcerers that this was a prior collusion and conspiracy formed between them and Moses—a conspiracy against all the people of Egypt! He said: "He is surely your chief and master who taught you magic, and you have all learned magic from his school!" (إِنَّهُ لَكَبِيرُكُمُ الَّذِي عَلَّمَكُمُ السِّحْرَ).

You have created this staged scene by prior arrangement to mislead the great nation of Egypt and bring them under the dominance of your own rule! You want to displace the original owners of this country from their city and land and put slaves in their place.

But I will not allow you to succeed in this conspiracy; I will stifle this plot in its infancy! "Soon you shall know; I will punish you in such a way that it becomes a lesson for everyone. I will cut off your hands and feet on opposite sides (right hand and left foot, or left hand and right foot) and I will crucify you all without exception!" (فَلَسَوْفَ تَعْلَمُونَ لَأُقَطِّعَنَّ أَيْدِيَكُمْ وَأَرْجُلَكُمْ مِنْ خِلَافٍ وَلَأُصَلِّبَنَّكُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ).

Meaning, I will not only kill you all, but a death accompanied by agony and torture, and that too in public and upon high palm trees. For cutting hands and feet on opposite sides causes a person to likely die more slowly, increasing the agony and torture.

And such is the method of oppressive and tyrannical rulers in every age and time: first, they accuse the divine reformers of conspiring against the people, and after using the weapon of slander, they employ the weapon of the sword, so that the position of the truth-seekers and their popular support is first weakened, and then they are removed from their path.

But Pharaoh had miscalculated here. For the sorcerers of a moment ago, and the believers of this moment, had their hearts so illuminated by the light of faith and heated by the fire of God's love that they answered this threat of Pharaoh in the presence of the crowd in a very decisive manner, thwarting his satanic plan. "They said: No harm! And no loss will reach us from this act; do whatever you wish, for we are returning to our Lord" (قَالُوا لَا ضَيْرَ ۖ إِنَّا إِلَىٰ رَبِّنَا مُنْقَلِبُونَ).

By this act, you not only take nothing away from us, but you bring us to the meeting with the True Beloved and the Real Deity. On the day when these threats affected us, we did not know ourselves, we were not acquainted with our God, and having lost the path of truth, we were wandering in the desert of life. But today, we have found our lost one; do whatever you can!

Then they added: We have committed sins in the past, and in this scene, we became the leaders of the struggle against the true Prophet of God, Moses عليه السلام, and were the first in contending against the truth. But "we hope that our Lord will forgive us our faults, because we were the first of the believers" (إِنَّا نَطْمَعُ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ لَنَا رَبُّنَا خَطَايَانَا أَنْ كُنَّا أَوَّلَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

Today we fear nothing—neither your threats, nor struggling in blood atop the high branches of palm trees!

If we have any fear, it is only of our past sins, and we hope that too will be removed under the shadow of faith and hope in the grace of the Truth.

What power is this that when it appears in the human heart, the greatest powers seem small to him, he becomes resistant to the harshest tortures, and becomes generous in sacrificing his life?

This is the power of faith.
