This is the flame of the glowing lamp of love, which makes the nectar of martyrdom in the way of God sweeter than honey in the human throat, and makes union with the Beloved the highest goal.

This was the very power that the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله utilized, with which he nurtured the first Muslims and rapidly brought a backward nation to the peak of glory—Muslims whose history became a source of wonder for the world.

In any case, this scene proved very costly for Pharaoh and his apparatus. Although according to some narrations he carried out his threats and martyred the believing sorcerers, this act not only failed to extinguish the people's emotions—which had been stirred in favor of Moses عليه السلام—but rather fanned them.

Everywhere, there was talk of the newly appeared Prophet, and everywhere, the first martyred believers were discussed. A group believed through this means, such that some of Pharaoh's close companions and even his wife joined these ranks.

A question arises here: how did the repentant and believing sorcerers call themselves the "first of the believers"?

Did they mean they were the first believers in that specific scene?

Or the first believers from among Pharaoh's supporters?

Or the first believers to drink the draught of martyrdom?

All of these are possible and, at the same time, not contradictory to one another.

These interpretations apply if we believe that others from the Children of Israel or others had believed in Moses before them. However, if we say that after his appointment, he was commissioned to directly contact Pharaoh himself and deal the first blow to his structure, it is not unlikely that this group was truly the very first believers, and there is no need for another interpretation.

Ayahs

۞ وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبَادِىٓ إِنَّكُم مُّتَّبَعُونَ (٥٢)
Then We revealed to Moses, [saying],‘Set out with My servants at night, for you will be pursued.’ (52)
فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ فِى ٱلْمَدَآئِنِ حَـٰشِرِينَ (٥٣)
Then Pharaoh sent heralds to the cities, (53)
إِنَّ هَـٰٓؤُلَآءِ لَشِرْذِمَةٌ قَلِيلُونَ (٥٤)
[proclaiming:] ‘These are indeed a small band. (54)
وَإِنَّهُمْ لَنَا لَغَآئِظُونَ (٥٥)
They have aroused our wrath, (55)
وَإِنَّا لَجَمِيعٌ حَـٰذِرُونَ (٥٦)
and we are alert and fully prepared.’ (56)
فَأَخْرَجْنَـٰهُم مِّن جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (٥٧)
So We took them out of gardens and springs, (57)
وَكُنُوزٍ وَمَقَامٍ كَرِيمٍ (٥٨)
and [made them leave behind] treasures and stately homes. (58)
كَذَٰلِكَ وَأَوْرَثْنَـٰهَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (٥٩)
So it was; and We bequeathed them to the Children of Israel. (59)

Commentary

We Drove Them Out of Their Treasures and Magnificent Palaces!

In the preceding verses, we saw that ultimately Moses (عليه السلام) emerged victorious from that decisive arena. Although Pharaoh and his folk did not believe in him, this event had several important effects, each of which was considered a significant victory:

  1. The Children of Israel became believers in and encouraged by their leader and guide, gathering around him with one heart and soul. After years of misery, wretchedness, and displacement, they saw among them a heavenly Prophet who was both the guarantor of their guidance and the leader of their revolution, freedom, and victory.

  2. Moses (عليه السلام) established a place for himself among the people of Egypt and the Copts; a group became inclined toward him, or at least feared opposing him, and the sound of Moses' call echoed throughout all of Egypt.

  3. Most importantly, Pharaoh found within himself no power to harass a man who held such a staff in his hand and possessed such an eloquent tongue in his mouth—neither in terms of public opinion nor in terms of fear for his own life.

The combination of these factors provided a favorable ground for Moses (عليه السلام) to remain among those people, engage in invitation and preaching, and complete the proof (Hujjah) upon them.

Many years passed in this manner, and Moses (عليه السلام) showed them other miracles—which we mentioned in Surah Al-A'raf under verses 130 to 135—alongside his logic and eloquence. Allah even afflicted the people of Egypt with years of famine and drought so that those worthy of awakening might wake up (for more details on this, see Tafsir Nemouneh, Volume 6, page 313 onwards).

When Moses (عليه السلام) had completed the proof upon them, and the ranks of the believers and deniers became distinct, the command for the Children of Israel to migrate was given to Moses (عليه السلام). The verses under discussion depict this scene.

First, it says: "And We inspired Moses, saying: 'Set out with My servants by night, for surely you will be pursued'" (وَ أَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ مُوسى‌ أَنْ أَسْرِ بِعِبادِي إِنَّكُمْ مُتَّبَعُونَ‌).

This is a Divine plan: you shall move by night, and they will also become aware and pursue you, and whatever must happen in the midst of this shall happen.

The expression "My servants" (عِبادِي), despite the preceding word "We inspired" (أَوْحَيْنا) being in the plural form, is used to express Allah's ultimate love for the faithful servants.

Moses (عليه السلام) executed this command and, away from the eyes of the enemies, mobilized the Children of Israel and ordered the march. He specifically chose the night for this task by Allah's command so that his plan would be more calculated.

However, it is obvious that the movement of such a massive group is not something that can be kept hidden for long. Soon, Pharaoh's spies reported the matter to him, and as the Quran says: "Then Pharaoh sent heralds to the cities to gather forces" (فَأَرْسَلَ فِرْعَوْنُ فِي الْمَدائِنِ حاشِرِينَ‌).

Of course, under the conditions of that time, it required a considerable amount of time for Pharaoh's message to reach all the cities of Egypt. However, it is natural that this news reached nearby cities quickly, and ready forces immediately moved to form the vanguard and the strike force, while other forces gradually joined them.

Furthermore, to prepare the psychological ground of the people for this general mobilization, he ordered it to be announced: "These are but a small band" (small in number compared to the Pharaohs and small in terms of power) (إِنَّ هؤُلاءِ لَشِرْذِمَةٌ قَلِيلُونَ‌).

Therefore, in the struggle against this group, with all the power we possess, there is no room for any concern; we are the winners.

"Shirdhimah" (شِرْذِمَةٌ) originally means a small group or the remains of something, and tattered clothes are called "sharadhim." Thus, in addition to the meaning of being few, the concept of being scattered is also embedded in it. It is as if Pharaoh, with this expression, wanted to depict the lack of cohesion among the Children of Israel in terms of military personnel as well.

Then he added: How much patience should we have? And to what extent should we tolerate these rebellious slaves?! "And surely they have enraged us" (وَ إِنَّهُمْ لَنا لَغائِظُونَ‌).

Tomorrow, who will irrigate the fields of Egypt? Who will repair our houses? Who will lift the heavy burdens from the ground in this vast country? Who will be our servants?

Additionally, "We are fearful of the conspiracy of this group (whether they stay here or leave), and we must have full readiness and necessary vigilance to confront them" (وَ إِنَّا لَجَمِيعٌ حاذِرُونَ‌).

Some commentators have interpreted "hadhirun" (حاذِرُونَ) from the root "hadhar" (حذر) meaning fear of their conspiracy, while others interpreted it as vigilance, alertness, and readiness in terms of force and weapons. However, these two interpretations are not contradictory; it is possible that the Pharaohs were both fearful and prepared for confrontation.

Then the Quran proceeds to mention the outcome of the Pharaohs' actions and briefly describes the downfall of their government and the leadership of the Children of Israel, saying: "So We expelled them from gardens and springs" (فَأَخْرَجْناهُمْ مِنْ جَنَّاتٍ وَ عُيُونٍ‌).

"And from treasures and beautiful palaces and comfortable dwellings" (وَ كُنُوزٍ وَ مَقامٍ كَرِيمٍ‌).

Yes, "Thus it was, and We gave them as an inheritance to the Children of Israel" (كَذلِكَ وَ أَوْرَثْناها بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ‌).

There is discussion among commentators regarding the interpretation of "noble station" (مَقامٍ كَرِيمٍ). Some have considered it to mean magnificent palaces and valuable dwellings; some, gatherings of joy and delight; some, the assemblies of rulers and governors surrounded by obedient officials; and some have interpreted it as the pulpits from which orators delivered speeches (pulpits where propaganda and speeches were made in favor of Pharaoh and his government and apparatus).

Of course, the first meaning seems most appropriate of all, although these meanings do not contradict each other, and it is possible that all are included in the concept of the verse: their palaces were taken from them, as were their positions of government and power, and their festive gatherings.

Key Points

1. Did the Children of Israel Rule in Egypt?

Based on the expression in the above verses where Allah says: "We made the Children of Israel inherit them," a group of commentators believe that they returned to Egypt, took the reins of government, and ruled over that land for a period [^1]. Certainly, the literal appearance of the above verses is consistent with this interpretation.

Meanwhile, others believe that after the destruction of the Pharaohs, they headed toward the holy lands, but after a while, they returned to Egypt and formed a government there [^2]. The chapters of the current Torah related to this part are consistent with this interpretation.

Others have suggested the possibility that the Children of Israel split into two groups: one group remained in Egypt and ruled, and another group set out with Moses (عليه السلام) toward the holy lands.

It has also been suggested that the meaning of the Children of Israel becoming heirs is that they ruled over the vast land of Egypt after Moses (عليه السلام) and during the time of Solomon.

However, considering that Moses (عليه السلام) was a great revolutionary Prophet, it seems very unlikely that he would completely abandon such a land—whose pillars of government had collapsed and was entirely at his disposal—and head toward the deserts without making any decision for it, especially since hundreds of thousands of the Children of Israel had lived there for many years and were familiar with the issues of that environment.

Therefore, it is one of two cases: either all the Children of Israel returned to Egypt and formed a government, or a group of them remained there by the command of Moses (عليه السلام) and implemented this program. Otherwise, the expulsion of the Pharaohs and making the Children of Israel their heirs, as mentioned in the verses, would not have a clear meaning.

2. The Order of the Verses

In the subsequent verses, the Quran describes how the Pharaohs were drowned. This raises the question of why the Quran first mentions the expulsion of the Pharaohs from their palaces, estates, and properties and the inheritance of the Children of Israel, and then the manner of the Pharaohs' drowning, while the natural order is otherwise.

This may be a case of "summary" followed by "detail"; that is, the matter is first stated concisely and then explained in detail in the following verses.

It could also be a case of mentioning the result first and then explaining the preceding events.

(Reflect on this).

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan and Qurtubi under the verses discussed; Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani also cites this as a notable interpretation.

[^2]: Ruh al-Ma'ani under the verses discussed.

Ayahs

فَأَتْبَعُوهُم مُّشْرِقِينَ (٦٠)
Then they pursued them at sunrise. (60)
فَلَمَّا تَرَٰٓءَا ٱلْجَمْعَانِ قَالَ أَصْحَـٰبُ مُوسَىٰٓ إِنَّا لَمُدْرَكُونَ (٦١)
When the two hosts sighted each other, the companions of Moses said, ‘Indeed we have been caught up.’ (61)
قَالَ كَلَّآ ۖ إِنَّ مَعِىَ رَبِّى سَيَهْدِينِ (٦٢)
He said, ‘Certainly not! Indeed my Lord is with me. He will guide me.’ (62)
فَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰٓ أَنِ ٱضْرِب بِّعَصَاكَ ٱلْبَحْرَ ۖ فَٱنفَلَقَ فَكَانَ كُلُّ فِرْقٍ كَٱلطَّوْدِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٦٣)
Thereupon We revealed to Moses: ‘Strike the sea with your staff!’ Whereupon it parted, and each part was as if it were a great mountain. (63)
وَأَزْلَفْنَا ثَمَّ ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (٦٤)
There, We brought the others near, (64)
وَأَنجَيْنَا مُوسَىٰ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥٓ أَجْمَعِينَ (٦٥)
and We delivered Moses and all those who were with him. (65)
ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (٦٦)
Then We drowned the rest. (66)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (٦٧)
There is indeed a sign in that, but most of them do not have faith. (67)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (٦٨)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (68)

Commentary

The Painful End of the Pharaohs!

In these verses, the final scene of this portion of the story of Moses and Pharaoh is presented: the manner of the destruction of the Pharaohs and the victory and salvation of the Children of Israel.

As we read in the previous verses, Pharaoh dispatched his officials to the cities of Egypt and prepared sufficient troops and forces. Some commentators have written that he sent six hundred thousand men as the vanguard of the army and followed them himself with one million more! They moved swiftly through the night in pursuit and reached the army of Moses at sunrise, as the first verse under discussion says: "So they pursued them at sunrise" (فَأَتْبَعُوهُمْ مُشْرِقِينَ) [1]. "When the two groups saw each other, the companions of Moses said: 'Indeed, we are surely overtaken by the Pharaohs, and there is no way of escape!'" (فَلَمَّا تَراءَا الْجَمْعانِ قالَ أَصْحابُ مُوسى‌ إِنَّا لَمُدْرَكُونَ).

Before us lies the sea and the roaring waves of water, and behind us a sea of bloodthirsty soldiers with full equipment—a multitude deeply enraged against us, who have proven their bloodthirstiness by killing our innocent children for many years. Pharaoh himself is a sufficiently headstrong, tyrannical, and bloodthirsty man. Therefore, they will quickly surround us all and put us to the sword, or capture us and return us to torture. All evidence indicated that this was indeed the case.

At this moment, painful times passed for the Children of Israel—moments whose bitterness is indescribable. Perhaps a large number wavered in their faith and severely lost their morale.

However, Moses عليه السلام remained calm and confident, knowing that Allah's promises regarding the salvation of the Children of Israel and the destruction of the rebellious people were infallible. Thus, with a world of certainty and trust, he turned to the terrified crowd of the Children of Israel and "said: 'By no means! Indeed, my Lord is with me; He will soon guide me'" (قالَ كَلَّا إِنَّ مَعِي رَبِّي سَيَهْدِينِ).

This expression might refer to the same promise Allah gave when commissioning Moses and Aaron, saying: "Indeed, I am with you both; I hear and I see" (Surah Ta-Ha, Verse 46).

He knows that Allah is with him everywhere. Specifically, the emphasis on the name "Rabb" (the Lord, Owner, and Reformer) shows he knew he was not traversing this path by his own feet, but by the grace of the All-Powerful and Merciful Lord.

At this moment, when some perhaps heard Moses' words with disbelief and were still waiting for the final moments of their lives, the final command was issued, as the Quran says: "Then We inspired Moses: 'Strike the sea with your staff!'" (فَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى‌ مُوسى‌ أَنِ اضْرِبْ بِعَصاكَ الْبَحْرَ). The same staff that was a sign of warning one day became a sign of mercy and salvation the next.

Moses عليه السلام did so and struck the sea with the staff. Here, a wondrous scene appeared that brought a spark of joy to the eyes and hearts of the Children of Israel: "Suddenly the sea split, the waters were divided, and each part became like a massive mountain!" and paths appeared between them (فَانْفَلَقَ فَكانَ كُلُّ فِرْقٍ كَالطَّوْدِ الْعَظِيمِ).

"In falaqa" (انفلق) is derived from the root "falq" (فلق), meaning to be split, and "firq" (فرق) is from the root "farq" (فرق), meaning to be separated. In other words (as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat), the difference between "falq" and "farq" is that the former refers to the splitting and the latter to the separation; hence, "firqah" (فرقة) and "firq" (فرق) are used for a piece or a group that is separated from the rest.

"Tawd" (طود) means a massive mountain, and its re-description as "Azim" (Great/Massive) in the above verse is an emphasis on this meaning.

In any case, the God whose command is effective over all things—if the waters rebel, it is by His command, and if the storms move, it is by His order; the same God for whom the design of existence is but a trace of His palace, and water, wind, and earth are His wanderers—gave such a command to the waves of the sea. The waves swiftly accepted and piled upon one another, paths appeared among them, and each group of the Children of Israel set out on a path.

Pharaoh and his people, who were stunned and bewildered by seeing this scene and such a clear and manifest miracle, still did not dismount from the steed of arrogance. They continued to pursue Moses and the Children of Israel and moved toward their final destiny, as the Quran says: "And there We brought the others near" (وَ أَزْلَفْنا ثَمَّ الْآخَرِينَ). Thus, the Pharaohs also entered the sea paths, still arrogantly running after their former slaves who had risen in rebellion, unaware that the final moments of their lives had arrived and the decree of punishment would soon be issued.

The next verse says: "And We saved Moses and all those who were with him" (وَ أَنْجَيْنا مُوسى‌ وَ مَنْ مَعَهُ أَجْمَعِينَ).

Exactly when the last person of the Children of Israel emerged from the sea and the last person of the Pharaohs entered the sea, We commanded the waters to return to their original state. The roaring waves suddenly collapsed and merged, carrying Pharaoh and his army away like pieces of straw, crushing and destroying them.

The Quran expresses this event in a short phrase, saying: "Then We drowned the others" (ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا الْآخَرِينَ).

And thus, everything ended in a single moment. The captive slaves were freed, and the arrogant tyrants were caught and destroyed. History turned a page; a dazzling civilization built upon the ruins of the homes of the oppressed was wiped from the face of the earth. The era of those arrogant ones ended, and the oppressed became the heirs to their land and government.

Yes, "Indeed, in that is a clear sign and a great lesson, but most of them did not believe." It is as if eyes were closed, ears were deaf, and hearts were fallen into sleep (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً وَ ما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ). Since the Pharaohs did not believe upon seeing those wondrous scenes, do not be surprised by these polytheistic people, and do not be distressed by their lack of faith, for history remembers many such scenes.

The expression "most" (أكثر) indicates that a group of the Pharaohs did embrace the religion of Moses and joined his companions—not only Asiya, the wife of Pharaoh, and the faithful friend of Moses mentioned in the Quran as the "Believer of the Family of Pharaoh," but also another group like the repentant sorcerers joined him.

The last verse under discussion, in a short and meaningful sentence, refers to the infinite power and mercy of God, saying: "And indeed, your Lord—He is the All-Mighty, the All-Merciful" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ).

It is from His "Mighty" (عزت) that whenever He wills, He issues the command for the destruction of rebellious nations. To destroy a tyrannical people, He does not need to dispatch an army of angels from heaven; He commands the very water that is the source of their life to be the cause of their death, and the same Nile River that was the source of wealth and power for the Pharaohs becomes their graveyard!

And it is from His "Mercy" (رحمت) that He never hurries in this matter; rather, He gives years of respite, sends miracles, and establishes the proof. It is also from His mercy that He delivers these oppressed slaves from the clutches of those bullying and forceful masters.

Key Points

1. The Crossing Point of the Children of Israel:

In the Holy Quran, it is repeated several times that Moses led the Children of Israel across the "Bahr" (sea) by Allah's command [1], and in a few instances, the term "Yamm" (water/sea) is used [2]. Now the question is: what is meant by "Bahr" and "Yamm" here? Does it refer to the vast and great Nile River, from which all the prosperity of the land of Egypt originated, or does it refer to the Red Sea (also known as the Sea of Kulzum)?

From the current Torah and also the words of some commentators, it appears to refer to the "Red Sea." However, we have evidence indicating that it refers to the great Nile River, because "Bahr" in the language—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat—originally means abundant and wide water, and "Yamm" also conveys this same meaning. Therefore, applying these two words to the "Nile" presents no obstacle. As for the evidence supporting this view:

  1. The residence of the Pharaohs, which was the center of the developed cities of Egypt, must have been a point not far from the Nile River. If we take the current location of the Pyramids or their vicinity as the benchmark, the Children of Israel had to cross the Nile first to reach the Holy Land, because this region is located west of the Nile and to reach the Holy Land they had to go east (note carefully).

  2. The distance between the developed areas of Egypt—which are naturally near the Nile—and the Red Sea is so great that it seems very unlikely the Children of Israel could traverse it in one night or half a night (from the previous verses it became clear that they left the land of the Pharaohs at night, presumably in the middle of the night, and Pharaoh's army reached them at sunrise).

  3. To leave the land of Egypt and reach the Holy Lands, there is no need to cross the Red Sea, because before the digging of the Suez Canal, there was a significant dry strip of land there—unless we resort to the hypothesis that in very ancient times the Red Sea was connected to the Mediterranean Sea and there was no dry land there, a hypothesis that is by no means proven.

  4. In the story of casting Moses into the water, the Quran uses the term "Yamm" (Ta-Ha: 39), and as we said, regarding the drowning of the Pharaohs, it also uses "Yamm." Given that both are in the same story, and even in the same Surah (Surah Ta-Ha), and both are mentioned absolutely, it seems they are the same. Considering that Moses' mother certainly did not cast him into the sea but, according to history and common evidence, entrusted him to the Nile, it follows that the drowning of the Pharaohs was in the Nile (note carefully).

2. The Manner of the Salvation of the Children of Israel and the Drowning of the Pharaohs:

Some commentators who are reluctant to accept miracles insist on justifying the event of the drowning of the Pharaohs and the salvation of the Children of Israel mentioned in the previous verses in a way that fits with natural and ordinary causes! Thus, they have sometimes said that this matter is applicable to mobile bridges common today [1]. Others have said that Moses عليه السلام was familiar with the paths and could cross through the isthmuses that existed in the "Sea of Suf" (Gulf of Suez) and enter the "Sinai Peninsula," and the splitting of the sea mentioned in the verses refers to this [2]. Others have perhaps strengthened the possibility that Moses reached that point exactly at the end of the low tide and was able to cross the dry spots, but immediately the high tide began and the Pharaohs were engulfed in the waves and perished!

But the truth is that none of these possibilities are consistent with the apparent meaning of the Quranic verses, if not their explicit text. By accepting the issue of miracles, which has been mentioned many times in the Quran's description of the prophets—especially the story of this very staff of Moses—there is no necessity for such justifications. What is the obstacle to the waters of the Nile, by the command of Allah who governs the law of causality in the universe, gathering and accumulating under a mysterious attraction after the striking of the staff, such that a traversable path appears among them, and after a while, this attraction is neutralized and the waters return to their original natural state? This is not an exception to the law of causality, but an admission of the influence of extraordinary causes that are unknown to us—with the limited knowledge we possess.

3. All-Mighty yet All-Merciful:

This point is also worthy of attention: the last verse under discussion, which is a kind of conclusion from the entirety of the work of Moses and Pharaoh and the victory of the army of truth and the destruction of the army of falsehood, describes Allah as "Mighty" (Aziz) and "Merciful" (Rahim). The first refers to the invincibility of His power, and the second conveys the vastness of His mercy toward all servants. Specifically, "Mighty" precedes "Merciful" so that the misconception does not arise that His mercy stems from a position of weakness; rather, He is Merciful while being All-Mighty.

Of course, some commentators believe that the description of Might refers to the defeat of the enemies and the description of Mercy refers to the victory of His friends. However, there is no obstacle to both attributes encompassing both groups, for all benefit from His mercy—even the sinners—and all are fearful of His power—even the righteous!

Commentary

This is the God I Worship:

As we mentioned at the beginning of this Surah, Allah recounts the life stories of seven great prophets and their struggles to guide straying nations. This serves both as a source of consolation for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the few believers of those days, and as a warning to all enemies of the truth and the arrogant.

Therefore, following the cautionary tale of Moses and Pharaoh, He addresses the inspiring story of Abraham (عليه السلام) and his struggles against the polytheists. This account begins with Abraham's dialogue with his uncle, Azar, and his misguided people [^1]. First, He says: "And recite to them the news of Abraham" (وَ اتْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ نَبَأَ إِبْراهِيمَ).

From all the reports concerning this great prophet, He emphasizes this part: "When he said to his father and his people, 'What do you worship?'" (إِذْ قالَ لِأَبِيهِ وَ قَوْمِهِ ما تَعْبُدُونَ). Certainly, Abraham knew what they worshipped; his goal was to draw them into conversation and make them confess to their own actions. Furthermore, the use of the word "ma" (what thing) expresses a form of contempt.

They immediately replied: "They said, 'We worship idols and remain devoted to them'" (قالُوا نَعْبُدُ أَصْناماً فَنَظَلُّ لَها عاكِفِينَ). This expression shows that not only did they feel no shame for their actions, but they were quite proud of them. After the phrase "na'budu asnaman" (we worship idols), which was sufficient to state their purpose, they added: "fanazallu laha 'akifin" (we rub our foreheads on their threshold all day long).

The word "nazallu" is usually applied to actions performed during the day, and its use in the present/future tense (mudari') indicates continuity and persistence.

"Akif" is derived from the root "ukuf," which means paying attention to something and remaining constant in its presence with respect; it adds further emphasis to the previous meaning.

"Asnam" is the plural of "sanam," meaning a statue made of gold, silver, wood, or the like, which they worshipped, imagining it to be a manifestation of holy beings or sacred entities.

In any case, upon hearing this, Abraham (عليه السلام) placed them under a barrage of intense objections and confronted them with a logical dead-end using two crushing sentences. "He said, 'Do they hear you when you call?'" (قالَ هَلْ يَسْمَعُونَكُمْ إِذْ تَدْعُونَ).

"Or do they benefit you or harm you?" (أَوْ يَنْفَعُونَكُمْ أَوْ يَضُرُّونَ). The minimum requirement for a deity is that He hears the call of His worshipper and hastens to help in times of trouble, or at the very least, there should be a fear of disobeying His command.

However, in these idols, nothing can be seen to indicate they possess the slightest perception or consciousness, or the smallest influence on human destiny. They are merely worthless metals, stones, or wood to which superstition and the power of imagination and fancy have granted such a status.

But the fanatical idolaters, faced with this logical question, resorted to their old and repetitive answer and "said, 'These issues are not the point; the important thing is that we found our forefathers doing the same!'" (قالُوا بَلْ وَجَدْنا آباءَنا كَذلِكَ يَفْعَلُونَ). This response, which reflects their blind imitation of their ignorant ancestors, was the only answer they could give to Abraham (عليه السلام). It is an answer whose proof of falsehood is inherent within it; no rational person allows themselves to follow others with closed eyes and ears, especially since the experiences of later generations are usually greater than those of the past, and there is no reason for blind imitation of them.

The expression "kadhalika yaf'alun" (they used to do so) is a further emphasis on their imitation—meaning, whatever they did, we do, whether it be worshipping idols or anything else!

At this point, Abraham directed the sharp edge of his attack toward the idols and "said, 'Have you then considered what you have been worshipping?'" (قالَ أَ فَرَأَيْتُمْ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ).

"You and your ancient forefathers?" (أَنْتُمْ وَ آباؤُكُمُ الْأَقْدَمُونَ).

"Indeed, they are enemies to me, except the Lord of the worlds" (فَإِنَّهُمْ عَدُوٌّ لِي إِلَّا رَبَّ الْعالَمِينَ). Yes, they are all enemies to me, and I am an irreconcilable enemy to them.

It is noteworthy that Abraham says, "They are enemies to me," even though the implication is that he also has enmity toward them. This expression might be because the worship of idols is the cause of misery, misguidance, and punishment in this world and the hereafter for man.

This is considered equivalent to their enmity. Furthermore, it is understood from several verses of the Quran that idols will disown their worshippers on the Day of Resurrection and rise in enmity against them; by Allah's command, they will speak and express their loathing [^2]. The exception of "Rabb al-'Alamin" (the Lord of the worlds), even though He was not included among their deities (known as a "disconnected exception" in linguistics), is for the purpose of emphasizing pure monotheism. There is also the possibility that among them were those who worshipped God in addition to the idols; to account for this, Abraham excludes the Lord of the worlds.

The use of the pronoun "hum" (they), which is usually for rational beings, in reference to the idols is for the same reason mentioned above.

Then Abraham (عليه السلام) proceeds to describe the Lord of the worlds and mention His spiritual and material blessings, so that by comparison with the idols—who neither hear the prayers of worshippers nor possess benefit or harm—the matter becomes perfectly clear. He begins first with the blessing of creation and guidance, saying: "He who created me, and it is He who guides me" (الَّذِي خَلَقَنِي فَهُوَ يَهْدِينِ).

[^1]: We have repeatedly stated that the word "ab" in the Arabic language and in the Holy Quran is sometimes applied to a father and sometimes to an uncle, and here it carries the second meaning (for more explanation, refer to Volume 5, page 303 onwards). [^2]: For more explanation in this regard, refer to Volume 13 of Tafsir Nemouneh under verse 82 of Surah Maryam.

He has guided me both in the realm of creation (takwin), providing me with the means of material and spiritual life, and in the realm of legislation (tashri'), sending me revelation and a Heavenly Book.

The mention of the word "fa" (then/so) after creation indicates that guidance is not separate from creation; it goes hand in hand with it everywhere. The phrase "yahdin" (He guides me), which appears in the present/future tense, is clear evidence of the continuity of guidance and man's need for Him throughout his entire life.

It is as if Abraham (عليه السلام), with these words, is expressing the reality that from the moment of my creation, I have always been with Him and am with Him in every state; I feel His presence in my life. He is the Friend who has placed a cord around my neck and leads me wherever He desires!

After stating the first stage of Lordship (Rububiyyah), namely guidance after creation, he turns to material blessings and says: "And it is He who feeds me and gives me drink" (وَ الَّذِي هُوَ يُطْعِمُنِي وَ يَسْقِينِ). Yes, I see all blessings as coming from Him; my skin and flesh, my water and food, are all from His bounties.

Not only in my state of health am I subject to His blessings, but "And when I am ill, it is He who cures me" (وَ إِذا مَرِضْتُ فَهُوَ يَشْفِينِ). Although illness also sometimes comes from His side, out of etiquette in speech, he attributes it to himself.

Then he steps beyond the stage of worldly life and addresses the eternal life in the Hereafter, to make it clear that I sit at the table of His bounty everywhere—not just in this world, but in the Hereafter as well. He says: "And who will cause me to die and then bring me to life" (وَ الَّذِي يُمِيتُنِي ثُمَّ يُحْيِينِ).

Yes, both my death is from Him and the return to life is from His side.

And when I enter the field of Resurrection, my eyes of hope are fixed upon Him because "And who I hope will forgive me my sin on the Day of Recompense" (وَ الَّذِي أَطْمَعُ أَنْ يَغْفِرَ لِي خَطِيئَتِي يَوْمَ الدِّينِ). Undoubtedly, the prophets are infallible (ma'sum) and have no sins to be forgiven. However, as we have said in the past, sometimes the "good deeds of the righteous are considered the sins of those drawn near (to Allah)," and in their high station, even performing a good deed can sometimes be subject to questioning because it prevented a better deed; hence it is called "leaving the better" (tark al-awla).

He never relies on his own good deeds, for these are nothing next to Divine Generosity and are not worth mentioning in the face of His blessings; rather, his only reliance is on Allah's grace. This is the final stage of total devotion to Allah (inqita' ilallah).

In short: to identify the true Deity, Abraham first goes to the "Creatorship" of the Lord, then clarifies His station of Lordship (Rububiyyah) in all stages.

First is the stage of guidance, then the stage of "material blessings"—including creating conditions and removing obstacles—and finally the stage of "eternal life" in the other world, where His Lordship manifests in the form of bestowing gifts and forgiving sins. In this way, he strikes a line of cancellation through the superstitious notion of multiple gods and lords of types, and bows his head in submission at the threshold of the Lord.

Commentary

The Prolific Supplications of Ibrahim (عليه السلام)

Here begin the supplications of Ibrahim and his requests from the presence of Allah. It is as if, after inviting that misguided people toward the Lord and explaining the manifestations of His Lordship in the universe, he suddenly detaches himself from them and turns his face toward the threshold of God. He asks Him for whatever he desires to show the idol-worshipping people that whatever they seek for this world and the Hereafter must be sought from Him; furthermore, this is another emphasis on the absolute Lordship of Allah.

The first request he makes from His presence is: "My Lord! Grant me knowledge, wisdom, and the ability to perceive the truth, and join me with the righteous" (رَبِّ هَبْ لِي حُكْماً وَ أَلْحِقْنِي بِالصَّالِحِينَ).

Here, he first asks for the station of "Hukm" (judgment/authority) and then for "joining the righteous."

"Hukm" and "Hikmah" (wisdom) are from the same root. As Raghib has stated in Al-Mufradat, wisdom is reaching the truth through knowledge and awareness, and the recognition of beings and virtuous deeds. In other words, it is the knowledge of values and criteria by which a human can recognize the truth wherever it may be and distinguish falsehood in whatever garment it appears. This is what some philosophers have referred to as the "perfection of the theoretical faculty."

This is the same reality that Luqman received from Allah: وَ لَقَدْ آتَيْنا لُقْمانَ الْحِكْمَةَ (Luqman: 12), and it is mentioned as "abundant good" in verse 269 of Surah Al-Baqarah: وَ مَنْ يُؤْتَ الْحِكْمَةَ فَقَدْ أُوتِيَ خَيْراً كَثِيراً. It appears that "Hukm" carries a concept superior to "Hikmah," meaning awareness coupled with the readiness for implementation; in other words, the power of correct judgment that is free from ego-worship and error.

Indeed, Ibrahim (عليه السلام) first and foremost asks Allah for "deep and correct understanding" coupled with authority, because no practical program is possible without such a foundation.

Following that, he asks Allah to be joined with the righteous, which refers to the practical aspects and so-called "practical wisdom," in contrast to the previous request which pertained to "theoretical wisdom."

Undoubtedly, Ibrahim (عليه السلام) possessed both the station of "Hukm" and was among the "righteous." So why does he make such a request from Allah?

The answer is that neither wisdom nor righteousness has a fixed limit. He requests to reach higher and loftier levels of knowledge and action day by day; even a Prophet of the Ulul-'Azm (Arch-Prophets) is not content with his current state in these aspects! Furthermore, he knows that all of this comes from Allah, and at every moment there is the possibility of slipping or the withdrawal of these blessings. Therefore, he seeks their continuation along with perfection from Allah, just as we—even though we are, God willing, walking upon the straight path of faith—seek guidance to the "Straight Path" from Allah every day in our prayers, requesting the continuation of this path and its perfection.

After these two requests, he makes another important plea with this expression: "O Allah! Grant me a mention of truth and a good reputation among the future nations" (وَ اجْعَلْ لِي لِسانَ صِدْقٍ فِي الْآخِرِينَ). Make it such that my memory remains in hearts and my path and program continue among those who come after; let me be an "Uswah" (exemplar) and a model to be followed, and the founder of a school through which they learn Your way and move in Your direction.

Allah also granted this prayer of Ibrahim, as the Quran says: وَ جَعَلْنا لَهُمْ لِسانَ صِدْقٍ عَلِيًّا: "And We granted them a high and lofty mention of truth" (Maryam: 50).

It is not unlikely that this request includes the very thing Ibrahim asked of Allah after building the Kaaba, saying: رَبَّنا وَ ابْعَثْ فِيهِمْ رَسُولًا مِنْهُمْ يَتْلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ آياتِكَ وَ يُعَلِّمُهُمُ الْكِتابَ وَ الْحِكْمَةَ وَ يُزَكِّيهِمْ: "Our Lord, and send among them a messenger from themselves who will recite to them Your verses and teach them the Book and wisdom and purify them and cause them to grow" (Al-Baqarah: 129).

And we know that this request was fulfilled with the appearance of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), and the good mention of Ibrahim continued through this great Ummah.

Then, he lifts his vision from this world and turns toward the eternal abode of the Hereafter, making a fourth supplication: "O Allah! Make me among the inheritors of the Garden of Bliss" (وَ اجْعَلْنِي مِنْ وَرَثَةِ جَنَّةِ النَّعِيمِ). A Paradise where spiritual and material blessings overflow—blessings that have neither decline nor weariness; blessings that are not even perceivable to us, the prisoners of this world, having neither crossed our minds, nor been seen by our eyes, nor heard by our ears!

We have previously mentioned that the term "inheritance" regarding Paradise is either because inheritance means attaining a blessing without toil and trouble, and certainly all those heavenly blessings compared to our insignificant acts of obedience are a gift without toil; or it is because, according to narrations, every human has a home in Paradise and a home in Hell—when one becomes an inhabitant of Hell, their home in Paradise passes to others.

In the fifth supplication, he turns his attention toward his misguided uncle, and according to the promise he had previously given him that he would seek forgiveness for him, he says: "O Allah! Forgive my father (uncle), for he was among the misguided" (وَ اغْفِرْ لِأَبِي إِنَّهُ كانَ مِنَ الضَّالِّينَ). Ibrahim had previously given this promise according to the explicit text of verse 114 of Surah At-Tawbah: "وَ ما كانَ اسْتِغْفارُ إِبْراهِيمَ لِأَبِيهِ إِلَّا عَنْ مَوْعِدَةٍ وَعَدَها إِيَّاهُ." His goal was to find a way into his heart and draw him toward faith; thus, he made such a promise and fulfilled it. According to a narration from Ibn Abbas, he repeatedly sought forgiveness for "Azar." However, when Azar died in a state of disbelief and his enmity toward the true religion became certain, Ibrahim ceased seeking forgiveness.

As we read in the continuation of the same verse mentioned above: فَلَمَّا تَبَيَّنَ لَهُ أَنَّهُ عَدُوٌّ لِلَّهِ تَبَرَّأَ مِنْهُ:

"But when it became apparent to him that he was an enemy to Allah, he disassociated himself from him." [1]

Finally, he offers his sixth and last supplication, which is also concerning the Last Day, to the presence of Allah: "O Allah! Do not disgrace me on the Day they are resurrected" (وَ لا تُخْزِنِي يَوْمَ يُبْعَثُونَ). "La tukhzini" is from the root "khizy" which, as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, means "spiritual brokenness" (and shame), which either comes from the person himself appearing as extreme bashfulness, or is imposed upon the person by another.

This expression from Ibrahim (عليه السلام), besides being a lesson and a model for others, is a sign of the ultimate sense of responsibility and reliance upon the grace of the Lord.

[1]: For further explanation, please refer to Volume 8 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 160, under verse 114 of Surah At-Tawbah.

Commentary

The Dispute Between Deities and Misguided Worshipers!

In the final verse of the previous discussion, a brief reference was made to the Day of Resurrection and the issue of the Hereafter. In the verses under discussion, a comprehensive depiction of the nature of the Day of Resurrection is presented through several verses. It describes the most important "commodity" that finds a buyer in that market, as well as the fate of the believers, the disbelievers, the misguided, and the hosts of Iblis. The apparent context of the verses indicates that this description and explanation are part of the speech of Ibrahim عليه السلام and a continuation of his final supplication. Most commentators have also stated this, although some have suggested the possibility that these verses are entirely the Word of Allah, following Ibrahim's words to explain and complete them; however, this possibility is weak.

In any case, it first states: "The Day of Resurrection is a day when neither wealth nor sons will avail" (يَوْمَ لا يَنْفَعُ مالٌ وَ لا بَنُونَ).

In reality, these two major capitals of worldly life—wealth and human resources—will not yield the slightest result for their owners there. By extension, other worldly capitals that rank below these two will certainly be of no benefit.

It is obvious that "wealth and sons" here does not refer to wealth and children that were utilized in the path of seeking Allah's pleasure; rather, the emphasis is on the material aspects of the matter. The point is that material assets will not solve any problem on that day. However, if they are placed in the path of obeying the Lord's command, they are no longer merely material assets; they take on a Divine color and the "Dye of Allah" (صبغة الله) and are considered "the enduring good deeds" (الباقيات الصالحات).

Then, as an exception to this statement, it adds: "Except him who comes to Allah with a sound heart (free from any kind of polytheism, disbelief, and the pollution of sin)" (إِلَّا مَنْ أَتَى اللَّهَ بِقَلْبٍ سَلِيمٍ).

Thus, the only lifesaving capital on the Day of Judgment is a "sound heart" (Qalb Salim). What a comprehensive and beautiful expression! It is an expression that encompasses both pure faith and sincere intention, as well as every kind of righteous deed; for such a pure heart will produce nothing but pure actions. In other words, just as the human heart and soul influence human actions, his actions also have a vast reflection on the heart and soul, coloring them with their own hue—whether Merciful (Rahmani) or Satanic.

Then, describing Paradise and Hell, it says: "At that time, Paradise shall be brought near to the righteous" (وَ أُزْلِفَتِ الْجَنَّةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ) [1].

"And Hell shall be laid open for the misguided" (وَ بُرِّزَتِ الْجَحِيمُ لِلْغاوِينَ). This actually occurs before they enter Paradise and Hell, where each of these two groups sees the vista of their abode from up close: the believers become joyful and the misguided become terrified. This is the first stage of their reward and punishment.

It is interesting that it does not say: "The righteous are brought near to Paradise," but rather says "Paradise is brought near to them," which is an indication of their very exalted and precious status.

[1]: "Uzlifat" is derived from the root "Zulfa" (on the pattern of Kubra), meaning proximity and nearness.

It is also noteworthy that the term "Ghawin" (the misguided) is the same term used in the story of Iblis after he was driven from Allah's presence, where Allah says: إِنَّ عِبادِي لَيْسَ لَكَ عَلَيْهِمْ سُلْطانٌ إِلَّا مَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْغاوِينَ: "Indeed, My servants—you have no authority over them, except those who follow you of the misguided" (Surah Al-Hijr, Verse 42).

Then, it addresses the reproachful and rebuking dialogues that take place with this misguided group at that time, saying: "And it will be said to them: Where are those you used to worship?" (وَ قِيلَ لَهُمْ أَيْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْبُدُونَ).

"Deities other than Allah" (مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ). "Can they help you against these hardships and difficulties you now face?" (هَلْ يَنْصُرُونَكُمْ).

"Or can they call someone to help you, or can anyone even come to their own aid?" (أَوْ يَنْتَصِرُونَ) [1]. But they have no answer to this question, and no one expects one from them.

"At this moment, all the deities are gathered and cast into Hell along with their misguided worshipers" (فَكُبْكِبُوا فِيها هُمْ وَ الْغاوُونَ).

[1]: "Yantasirun" may refer to seeking help for oneself, for another, or both, as we read in the following verses that both the deities and the worshipers are cast into the fire of Hell.

According to some commentators, they will be thrown one on top of the other!

"And also the hosts of Iblis, all together" (وَ جُنُودُ إِبْلِيسَ أَجْمَعُونَ). In reality, these three groups—the idols, the idol-worshipers, and the hosts of Satan who were the brokers of this sin and deviation—are all gathered in Hell, but in such a way that they are cast into it one after another.

Since "Kubkibu" is originally from the root "Kabb," and "Kabb" means throwing something face-down into a pit, its repetition (Kubkub) signifies the repetition of this fall. This indicates that when they fall into Hell, they are like a stone thrown from a height into a valley; first hitting one point, then another, until reaching the bottom of the valley [1].

But the matter does not end here; rather, it portrays a scene of dispute and argument among these three groups of Hell-dwellers: "They will say while they are disputing therein" (قالُوا وَ هُمْ فِيها يَخْتَصِمُونَ).

Yes, the misguided worshipers will say: "By Allah, we were indeed in manifest error" (تَاللَّهِ إِنْ كُنَّا لَفِي ضَلالٍ مُبِينٍ) 2.

[1]: In modern Persian, "Kabkaba" refers to a group of riders or the collective sound of the footsteps of beasts and humans, and is a metaphor for pomp, glory, and majesty (Moin Dictionary). It is not unlikely that this expression is derived from "Kubkubatun" (with damma on both Kafs), which in Arabic means a group of humans or beasts, and is sometimes paired in Persian with "Dabdaba," which also means the sound of a crowd's footsteps or the sound of drums.

"Because we equated you false deities with the Lord of the Worlds!" (إِذْ نُسَوِّيكُمْ بِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ) [1].

"But none misled us except the criminals" (وَ ما أَضَلَّنا إِلَّا الْمُجْرِمُونَ). Those same criminals who were the leaders of our society and, to protect their own interests, led us down this path and made us wretched.

It is also possible that "the criminals" refers to the devils or their misguided ancestors who led them to this path.

But alas, today "we have no intercessors" (فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ).

"Nor a close, warm friend" who could help us (وَ لا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ). In short, neither do the deities intercede for us, as we imagined in the world, nor do friends have the power to help us. It is noteworthy that "intercessors" (Shafi'in) in the previous verse appears in the plural, while "friend" (Sadiq) appears in the singular. This difference might be because this group of misguided people sees with their own eyes that believers who had slips are benefiting from the intercession of a group of intercessors such as prophets, successors, and angels, and the intercession of some righteous friends; they too wish they had an intercessor and a friend.

[1]: "Idh" here may be used in a temporal (zarfiyyah) or causal (ta'liliyyah) sense.

Furthermore, "Sadiq" (friend) and "Aduww" (enemy), as some commentators have specified, can be applied to both singular and plural.

But they soon realize the reality that neither regret avails there, nor is it a place for action and compensation. Therefore, they wish to return to the world and say: "If only we had another chance to return, we would be among the believers!" (فَلَوْ أَنَّ لَنا كَرَّةً فَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ).

It is true that they have found faith there on that day, but this is a type of forced faith. The faith that is effective and constructive is that which is voluntary and in this world—faith that becomes a source of guidance and righteous deeds.

However, in any case, this wish also solves no problem, and Divine Law does not allow anyone to return, and they themselves know this truth, as indicated by the word "Law" [1].

Finally, at the end of this section of Ibrahim's story, his dialogues with the misguided people, his supplications before Allah, and his descriptions of the state of the Day of Resurrection, Allah repeats those two soul-shaking verses as a conclusion for all servants—the same verses that appeared at the end of the story of Musa and Pharaoh and will appear at the end of the stories of other prophets in this same Surah: "Indeed, in this is a great sign of Allah's majesty and power, and of the painful end of the misguided and the victory of the believers, but most of them were not believers."

[1]: "Law" is a conditional particle usually used in cases where the condition is impossible.

(إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً وَ ما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

"And indeed, your Lord is the Almighty, the Merciful" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ). The repetition of these sentences is an effective consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and the few believers of that day, as well as for the believing minorities in every age and time, so they do not fear the misguided majority and remain heartened by Divine Might and Mercy. It is also a threat to the misguided and an indication that if they are given a respite, it is not out of weakness but because of Mercy.

Key Points

1- The "Sound Heart" is the Only Saving Capital

In the midst of Ibrahim's عليه السلام words in the above verses, we read that in describing the Resurrection, he says nothing is of use there except a "sound heart" (Qalb Salim).

"Salim," which comes from the root of "Salamah" (health/safety), has a clear meaning: a heart that is far from any kind of disease and moral or ideological deviation.

Is it not that the Quran says regarding the hypocrites: "فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ مَرَضٌ فَزادَهُمُ اللَّهُ مَرَضاً": "In their hearts is a disease, so Allah has increased their disease because of their persistence!" (Surah Al-Baqarah, Verse 10).

In several meaningful Hadiths, the "sound heart" is well-defined:

1- In a Hadith from Imam Al-Sadiq عليه السلام regarding the verse under discussion, we read: "Every heart in which there is polytheism or doubt is fallen and worthless" [1].

2- On the other hand, we know that intense material attachments and world-worship lead a person to every deviation and sin, because: "Love of the world is the root of every sin" 2. Therefore, a sound heart is a heart that is empty of "love of the world," just as Imam Al-Sadiq عليه السلام said in another Hadith regarding this same verse: "It is the heart that is safe from the love of the world" [3]. Considering Verse 197 of Surah Al-Baqarah: "وَ تَزَوَّدُوا فَإِنَّ خَيْرَ الزَّادِ التَّقْوى‌": "And take provisions, but indeed, the best provision for that world is God-wariness," it becomes clear that a sound heart is one that, due to its health, becomes the source of Divine Taqwa.

3- The final word is that a sound heart is a heart in which there is nothing but Allah, as Imam Al-Sadiq عليه السلام said in response to a question about this verse: "The sound heart is the one that meets its Lord while there is no one in it besides Him" [4]. It goes without saying that "heart" in these contexts refers to the human soul and spirit.

In Islamic narrations regarding the heart, its health, the ailments that afflict it, and the way to combat these ailments, much has been said. From all of them, this Islamic logic is perfectly clear: that Islam, before anything else, gives importance to the intellectual, ideological, and moral foundation,

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses discussed. 2: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 70, p. 239. [3]: Tafsir al-Safi, under this same verse. [4]: Al-Kafi, as quoted in Al-Safi under the verse discussed.

because all of a person's practical programs are a reflection of it.

Just as the health of the physical heart is the factor for the health of the body, and its disease causes the disease of all limbs—since the nourishment of all body cells is carried out by the blood sent to all parts of the body's territory with the help of the heart—likewise, the health and corruption of a person's life programs are a manifestation and reflection of the health and corruption of belief and ethics.

We conclude this discussion with a saying from Imam Al-Sadiq عليه السلام where he said: "Hearts are of four types: A heart in which there is hypocrisy and faith. A heart that is overturned. A heart that is sealed, so no truth enters it. And a heart that is luminous and empty (of other than Allah)."

Then he added: "The luminous heart is the heart of the believer; whenever Allah grants him a blessing, he gives thanks, and whenever a calamity reaches him, he practices patience and endurance. But the overturned heart is the heart of the polytheists, just as Allah has said: أَ فَمَنْ يَمْشِي مُكِبًّا عَلى‌ وَجْهِهِ أَهْدى‌ أَمَّنْ يَمْشِي سَوِيًّا عَلى‌ صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ: 'Is he who walks prone on his face better guided or he who walks upright on a straight path?' As for the heart in which there is faith and hypocrisy, it is the heart of those who are indifferent toward truth and falsehood; if they are in an environment of truth, they follow the truth, and if they are in an environment of falsehood, they incline toward falsehood. And as for the sealed heart, it is the heart of the hypocrites" [1].

[1]: "Usul al-Kafi," Vol. 2, p. 309—Chapter on the Darkness of the Hypocrite's Heart (with slight paraphrasing).

2- In several narrations from Imam Al-Baqir and Imam Al-Sadiq عليهما السلام regarding the verse فَكُبْكِبُوا فِيها هُمْ وَ الْغاوُونَ, we read: "These are a people who described justice with their tongues but then opposed it in practice for something else" [1]. This shows to what extent speech without action is ugly and reprehensible, such that it casts the speaker painfully into the fire of Hell. They are those who are both misguided and misguiding; their words call people toward the truth while their actions call to falsehood—rather, their actions demonstrate their lack of faith in their own words.

Additionally, it should be noted that "Ghawun," derived from the root "Ghayy," does not mean just any kind of misguidance, but rather, as Raghib says in "Al-Mufradat," it is that type of ignorance and misguidance that stems from corruption of belief.

3- Regarding the verse فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ وَ لا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ: (We have neither intercessors nor a warm friend), several narrations have been quoted, and in some, it is explicitly stated: "The intercessors are the Imams, and the friend is from among the believers" 2. In another Hadith from Jabir ibn Abdullah, we read that he heard the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله saying: "A man in Paradise will say: 'What happened to my friend so-and-so?' while his friend is in Hell. Then Allah will say: 'Bring out his friend for him to Paradise.' Then those remaining in the Fire will say: فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ وَ لا صَدِيقٍ حَمِيمٍ: 'Woe to us, for we have neither an intercessor nor a kind friend!'" [1].

It is obvious that neither is intercession without criteria and standards, nor is the request regarding their friend arbitrary. There must be a kind of spiritual bond and connection between the intercessor and the one being interceded for so that this goal may be realized (we have provided a detailed explanation of this subject in the discussion on intercession in the first volume of Tafsir Nemouneh under Verse 48 of Surah Al-Baqarah).

[1]: These narrations were quoted by the author of Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn from "Usul al-Kafi," "Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim," and "Mahasin al-Barqi." 2: "Mahasin al-Barqi" as quoted in Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn under the verse discussed. [1]: "Majma' al-Bayan" under the verse discussed.

كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٠٥)

إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ نُوحٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٠٦)
