Commentary

O Noah! Why Have the Lowly Gathered Around You?

After concluding the account of Ibrahim (peace be upon him) and his dialogues with his misguided people, the Quran mentions the story of the people of Noah as another instructive narrative. Within a few verses, it recounts their stubbornness, persistence, and insolence, alongside their painful end.

First, It says: "The people of Noah denied the messengers" (كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ نُوحٍ الْمُرْسَلِينَ) [1].

[^1]: The feminine form "كَذَّبَتْ" is used because "قوم" (people) carries the meaning of "جماعة" (group), and "group" is grammatically feminine. Some have also stated that "قوم" is inherently a feminine word because its diminutive form is "قويمة" (the first explanation was provided by Tabarsi in Majma' al-Bayan and the second by Fakhr Razi in his Tafsir). However, Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani states that the word "قوم" is used as both masculine and feminine.

It is clear that the people of Noah only denied Noah (peace be upon him). However, since the call of all prophets was one in terms of fundamental principles, denying Noah was considered a denial of all the messengers. Therefore, He says the people of Noah denied the "messengers" (mursalin).

There is also the possibility that the people of Noah fundamentally rejected all religions and creeds, denying all divine prophets whether they appeared before or after Noah.

Then, referring to this stage of his life—which resembles the stages previously mentioned regarding Ibrahim and Musa—It says: "Remember when their brother Noah said to them: 'Will you not fear Allah?'" (إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ نُوحٌ أَ لا تَتَّقُونَ). The expression "brother" is a term that signifies the ultimate bond of affection based on equality and parity. That is, Noah, without seeking any superiority over them, invited them to piety with the utmost sincerity and cordiality.

The use of the term "brotherhood," which appears not only regarding Noah but also concerning many other prophets (such as Hud, Salih, and Lut), inspires all leaders of the path of truth that they must observe the utmost love and sincerity in their invitation, coupled with an avoidance of any seeking of superiority, so that estranged hearts may be attracted to the religion of truth and feel no burden.

After the invitation to piety—which is the essence of all guidance and salvation—he adds: "Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger" (إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ).

"So fear Allah and obey me," for my obedience is the obedience of Allah (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ).

This expression indicates that Noah (عليه السلام) had a long history of trustworthiness among his people, and they knew him by this lofty attribute. Therefore, he says: For this reason, I am trustworthy in delivering the divine message, and you shall see no betrayal from me.

Placing "piety" (taqwa) before "obedience" is because as long as there is no faith or belief in "Allah" and fear of Him, obedience to the command of His prophet will not take place.

Once again, Noah (عليه السلام) resorts to another proof of his truthfulness—a proof that silences the tongues of excuse-seekers. He says: "I do not ask you for any reward for this [message]" (وَ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ).

"My reward is only with the Lord of the worlds" (إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى‌ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

It is clear that divine motives are usually evidence of the sincerity of the claimant to prophethood, whereas material motives clearly show that the goal is profit-seeking. The Arabs of that era were particularly familiar with this issue regarding soothsayers and similar individuals.

Following this sentence, he repeats the same statement he made after emphasizing his messengership and trustworthiness: "So fear Allah and obey me" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ).

However, when the stubborn polytheists and headstrong arrogant ones found the paths of excuse-seeking closed to them, they clung to this issue: "They said, 'Shall we believe in you while the lowest [of the people] follow you?'" (قالُوا أَ نُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَ اتَّبَعَكَ الْأَرْذَلُونَ).

They argued that the value of a leader should be recognized by his followers—as the saying goes, a shrine is known by its pilgrims. "When we look at your followers, a handful of 'nobodies,' anonymous, poor, destitute, and barefoot people with weak and insignificant trades have surrounded you. In such a state, how do you expect prominent wealthy men and renowned nobles to bow their heads in submission at your threshold?! Our water will never flow in the same stream as this crowd; we have never sat at the same table nor gathered under one roof. What an unreasonable expectation you have!"

It is true they were correct in the assessment that a leader should be known through his followers, but their great mistake was that they had lost the true concept and criteria of personality. They had set wealth, property, clothing, houses, and beautiful, expensive mounts as the criteria for measuring value, while they were oblivious to purity, piety, truth-seeking, and the high qualities of humanity which were abundant among the low-income classes and very scarce among the nobles.

A class-based spirit in its worst form dominated their thinking; for this reason, they considered the destitute class as "arazil" (اراذل)! (Arazil is the plural of ardhal, which is the plural of radhl, meaning lowly and insignificant). Incidentally, if they had stepped out of the prison of a class-based society, they could have easily understood that the faith of this group was itself the best evidence for the truthfulness and authenticity of this prophet's call.

But Noah (عليه السلام) immediately disarmed them here and "said: 'And what knowledge have I of what they used to do?'" (قالَ وَ ما عِلْمِي بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ).

Whatever their past was, it is gone. What matters is today, that they have answered the call of the divine leader, embarked on the path of self-improvement, and placed their hearts at the disposal of the Truth.

If they did good or bad deeds in the past, "Their account is only with my Lord, if you could but perceive" and had understanding and discernment (إِنْ حسابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلى‌ رَبِّي لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ). From this statement, it is also inferred that they wanted to accuse this group of believers of having a bad moral or practical record in addition to the issue of poverty. This is while corruption and pollution are usually found to a greater degree in the affluent classes; they are the ones who have all kinds of means of corruption at their disposal, are intoxicated by status and wealth, and rarely serve Allah! But Noah (عليه السلام), without getting into a confrontation with them on this issue, says: "I know nothing bad of them, and even if it were as you say, their account is with Allah!"

"What is my duty is that I spread my wings for all truth-seekers; 'I am not one to drive away the believers'" (وَ ما أَنَا بِطارِدِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ). This sentence is, in fact, a response to the implicit request of these arrogant wealthy men who had asked Noah to drive away and expel this group so that they might attend his presence!

"My only duty is to warn the people; 'I am only a clear warner'" (إِنْ أَنَا إِلَّا نَذِيرٌ مُبِينٌ).

Whoever hears this warning of mine and returns from the path of deviation to the straight path is my follower, whoever they may be and in whatever material condition or social circumstances.

It is noteworthy that this objection was not only raised against Noah (عليه السلام), who was the first of the Ulu al-'Azm (Arch-Prophets), but also against the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), who is the Seal of the Prophets, as well as other prophets. With the dark glasses they wore, they saw the sight of these "white-clad" ones as dark and always demanded their expulsion and rejection. They fundamentally did not approve of a God or prophets who had such servants and followers! But how beautifully the Quran says to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) in Surah Al-Kahf: "And keep yourself patient [by being] with those who call upon their Lord in the morning and the evening, seeking His countenance. And let not your eyes pass beyond them, desiring adornments of the worldly life, and do not obey one whose heart We have made heedless of Our remembrance and who has followed his desire and whose affair is ever [in] neglect" (Surah Al-Kahf, 18:28).

Even in our own era, this objection is raised against the leaders of the path of truth: that the largest number of your supporters consist of the "oppressed" (mustad'afin) and the barefoot.

They intend to cast a flaw with these words, while unknowingly they are offering praise and commendation, and putting a seal of approval on the authenticity of the school of thought.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ يَـٰنُوحُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَرْجُومِينَ (١١٦)
They said, ‘Noah, if you do not desist, you will certainly be stoned [to death].’ (116)
قَالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِى كَذَّبُونِ (١١٧)
He said, ‘My Lord! Indeed my people have impugned me. (117)
فَٱفْتَحْ بَيْنِى وَبَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحًا وَنَجِّنِى وَمَن مَّعِىَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (١١٨)
So judge conclusively between me and them, and deliver me and the faithful who are with me.’ (118)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَمَن مَّعَهُۥ فِى ٱلْفُلْكِ ٱلْمَشْحُونِ (١١٩)
Thereupon We delivered him and those who were with him in the laden ark. (119)
ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنَا بَعْدُ ٱلْبَاقِينَ (١٢٠)
Then We drowned the rest. (120)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٢١)
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (121)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٢٢)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (122)

Commentary

The Salvation of Noah and the Drowning of the Arrogant Polytheists

The reaction of this misguided and stubborn people toward Noah was the same as that of all oppressors throughout history, which was relying on power, force, and threats of destruction. They said:

"O Noah, enough! If you do not desist from these words and stop making the atmosphere of our society bitter and dark with your talk, you will certainly be stoned!" (قالُوا لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ يا نُوحُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمَرْجُومِينَ‌).

The expression "مِنَ الْمَرْجُومِينَ‌" (from those who are stoned) indicates that stoning was a precedent among them for dealing with opponents. In reality, they were saying to Noah: "If you continue these talks and this invitation toward monotheism and your religion, the same fate will befall you as befell our other opponents, which is stoning—one of the worst types of killing." [^1]

Noah, seeing that this continuous and long-term invitation, despite its clear logic and all his patience and endurance, had no effect except on a small number of people, finally took his complaint to the threshold of Allah. While describing his situation, he requested separation and salvation from the clutches of these illogical oppressors. "He said: My Lord! Indeed, my people have denied me" (قالَ رَبِّ إِنَّ قَوْمِي كَذَّبُونِ‌).

[^1]: "Rajm" (رجم) is originally from "Rijam" (on the weight of Kitab), the plural of "Rajmah" (on the weight of Luqmah), meaning a piece of stone placed on a grave or stones that idolaters circle around. "Rajm" also means striking with stones to the point of death, and sometimes it has been used to mean killing in any form or type, because their killings were carried out with stones.

It is true that Allah is aware of all things, but Noah mentions this while presenting his complaint and as a preamble to his subsequent request.

It is noteworthy that Noah does not express distress over the hardships that befell him personally; rather, he is only distressed that they have denied him and refused to accept the Divine message.

Then he says: Now that no path remains for the guidance of this group, "open a separation between me and them" and judge between us Yourself (فَافْتَحْ بَيْنِي وَ بَيْنَهُمْ فَتْحاً). "Fath" (فتح) originally—as the lexicographers have stated—means opening and removing a closure. This is of two types: sometimes it has a physical aspect, like "Fath al-Bab" (opening the door), and sometimes it has a spiritual aspect, like "Fath al-Hamm" (opening/relieving grief and removing sorrow), "Fath al-Mustaghlaq min al-Ulum" meaning unlocking the secrets of knowledge, and "Fath al-Qadiyyah" meaning judging and ending a dispute or lawsuit.

Then he adds: "And save me and the believers who are with me" (وَ نَجِّنِي وَ مَنْ مَعِيَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

At this point, Divine mercy came to Noah's aid and His painful punishment sought out the deniers, as He says: "So We saved him and those with him in the laden ship [filled with humans and various animals]" (فَأَنْجَيْناهُ وَ مَنْ مَعَهُ فِي الْفُلْكِ الْمَشْحُونِ‌).

"Then We drowned the rest thereafter" (ثُمَّ أَغْرَقْنا بَعْدُ الْباقِينَ‌).

"Mashhun" (مشحون) is derived from the root "Shahn" (on the weight of Sahn), meaning to fill, and sometimes it has also come to mean equipping. "Shahna'" (شحناء) refers to an enmity that fills a person's entire being. The meaning here is that the ship was full of people and all necessary provisions, lacking nothing. That is, after the ship was prepared in every respect and ready for departure, Allah sent the flood so that Noah and the other passengers would not face any hardship, and this itself was one of the Divine blessings upon them.

At the end of this account, He says the same thing He stated at the end of the stories of Moses عليه السلام and Abraham عليه السلام, saying: "Indeed, in the story of Noah, his persistent and continuous invitation, his patience and endurance, and finally the drowning and destruction of his opponents, there is a sign for everyone" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً). "Though most of them were not believers" (وَ ما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ‌).

Therefore, O Prophet, do not be distressed by the turning away and stubbornness of the polytheists of your people; show steadfastness, for your fate and that of your companions is the fate of Noah and his companions, and the end of the misguided is the same ill-fated end of those who were drowned.

And know that "Indeed, your Lord is the All-Mighty, the Especially Merciful" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ‌). His mercy requires that He give them sufficient opportunity and respite and complete the proof (Hujjah) upon them, and His Might ensures that He will ultimately make you victorious and face them with defeat.

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ عَادٌ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٢٣)
[The people of] ‘Ad impugned the apostles, (123)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٢٤)
when Hud, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (124)
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٢٥)
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (125)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٢٦)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (126)
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٢٧)
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (127)
أَتَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ ءَايَةً تَعْبَثُونَ (١٢٨)
Do you build futile a sign on every prominence? (128)
وَتَتَّخِذُونَ مَصَانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ (١٢٩)
You set up structures as if you will be immortal, (129)
وَإِذَا بَطَشْتُم بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ (١٣٠)
and when you seize [someone for punishment], you seize [him] like tyrants. (130)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٣١)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (131)
وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَمَدَّكُم بِمَا تَعْلَمُونَ (١٣٢)
Be wary of Him who has provided you with whatever you know, (132)
أَمَدَّكُم بِأَنْعَـٰمٍ وَبَنِينَ (١٣٣)
and aided you with sons and with cattle, (133)
وَجَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (١٣٤)
gardens and springs. (134)
إِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٣٥)
Indeed I fear for you the punishment of a tremendous day.’ (135)

Commentary

The Crimes and Reckless Actions of the People of ‘Ad

Now it is the turn of the people of "‘Ad" and their Prophet "Hud." Within eighteen verses, the Quran describes a portion of their lives, their destiny, and the lessons of admonition hidden therein.

The people of ‘Ad, as we have previously mentioned, were a population living in the land of "Al-Ahqaf" in the region of "Hadramaut," part of "Yemen" in the southern Arabian Peninsula.

"The rebellious people of ‘Ad—as the Quran states—denied the messengers" (كَذَّبَتْ عادٌ الْمُرْسَلِينَ) [^1]. Although they only denied Hud, because Hud’s call was the call of all prophets, they had, in reality, denied all the prophets.

After this brief mention, the text goes into detail, saying: "When their brother Hud said to them: 'Will you not fear Allah?'" (إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ هُودٌ أَ لا تَتَّقُونَ). He invited them to monotheism and piety with the utmost compassion and kindness, like a "brother," and for this reason, the word "brother" (akh) is applied to him.

Then he added: "Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger" (إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ). The history of my life among you is a witness to this truth: that I have never walked the path of treachery and have possessed nothing but truth and sincerity.

He emphasizes again: since this is the case and you are well aware of it, "So fear Allah and obey me" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ), for obeying me is obeying Allah.

And if you think I harbor desires for wealth or that these [calls] are a prelude to attaining riches and status, know that "I do not ask you for any reward for it" (وَ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ).

"My reward is only upon the Lord of the worlds" (إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى‌ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

All blessings and bounties are from Him, and if I desire anything, I ask it of Him, for He is the Lord of us all.

In this section of the story of Hud and the people of ‘Ad, the Quran emphasizes four parts in sequence: First, it specifies the content of Hud’s call, which was monotheism and piety, as we read in the preceding verses. Then, it turns to criticizing their deviations and wrongdoings, reminding them of three issues. Addressing them in the form of a rhetorical question, it says: "Do you build a monument on every high place for amusement?!" (أَ تَبْنُونَ بِكُلِّ رِيعٍ آيَةً تَعْبَثُونَ).

The word "ri‘" originally means a high place, and "ta‘bathun" is derived from "‘abath," meaning an action in which no correct goal is pursued. Considering the word "ayah," which means a sign or monument, it becomes clear that these affluent and wealthy people built structures on the high points of mountains and hills (such as towers and the like) for ostentation and boasting over others. These served no sound purpose other than to attract the attention of others and to flaunt their power and strength.

The view of some commentators—that these were huts built on heights as centers for vanity, play, and debauchery (as is common among the tyrants of our age)—seems unlikely, as it does not align well with the words "ayah" and "‘abath."

Some commentators have also suggested the possibility that the people of ‘Ad built these structures overlooking roads to mock and ridicule travelers from above. However, of these three interpretations, the first seems the most accurate.

Once again criticizing them, he says: "And you take for yourselves palaces (or strongholds) as if you might live forever" (وَ تَتَّخِذُونَ مَصانِعَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَخْلُدُونَ). "Masani‘" is the plural of "masna‘," meaning a place or a magnificent and sturdy building.

Hud does not object to them having suitable houses; rather, he says that you have become so drowned in the world and engaged in excessive luxury and disproportionate fortification of palaces and castles that you have forgotten the Hereafter. You have perceived the world not as a transit point, but as an eternal home. Indeed, such buildings that cause negligence and breed pride are certainly blameworthy.

In a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read that one day he was passing by a road and his eyes fell upon a dome and a palace overlooking the path. He asked: "What is this?"

The companions replied: "It belongs to one of the Ansar." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله paused for a moment. The owner arrived and offered greetings, but the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله turned his face away from him...

The Ansari man discussed this with his companions, saying: "By Allah, I see the Messenger of Allah’s view toward me as unpleasant. I do not know what has happened regarding me or what I have done!" They said: "The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw that magnificent building of yours and became upset."

The Ansari man went and leveled that dome and palace to the ground. One day the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله came and did not see it. He asked: "What happened to the building that was here?" They explained the situation. He said:

"Every building that is built is a burden (wabal) upon its owner on the Day of Resurrection, except for that which is indispensable!" [^2] From this narration and similar ones, the Islamic perspective becomes perfectly clear: it opposes "Taghuti" and distracting buildings accompanied by extravagance and excess. It does not permit Muslims to act like arrogant and godless oppressors—especially in environments where the deprived and those in need of housing are numerous—by constructing such buildings.

Interestingly, the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله did not resort to force to achieve this humane goal, nor did He ever order the destruction of such a building. Rather, He achieved His goal through a subtle moral reaction—turning His face away and showing indifference.

Then he turns to another criticism regarding the ruthlessness of the people of ‘Ad during disputes and conflicts, saying: "And when you strike, you strike as tyrants" (وَ إِذا بَطَشْتُمْ بَطَشْتُمْ جَبَّارِينَ). Someone might do something that warrants punishment, but you must never step beyond the path of truth and justice, nor impose the heaviest penalty for the smallest crime, nor shed blood in anger and fall upon people with the sword. This is the conduct of the tyrants, oppressors, and rebels of the age.

Raghib in "Al-Mufradat" says: "Batsh" means seizing something with power and superiority.

In truth, Hud reproaches these world-worshippers through three avenues:

First, for the monuments they built on heights out of selfishness and ostentation to boast over others.

Second, for buildings like the sturdy palaces of tyrants, which are signs of long-term worldly desires and negligence of the fact that the world is a "place of passage, not a place of permanence."

And finally, he criticizes their excess during punishment. The common factor among these three matters is the sense of seeking superiority and the love of permanence. This shows that the love of the world had so dominated them that they had left the path of servitude and were drowned in world-worship, even progressing to the point of claiming divinity. These points once again prove the truth that:

"Love of the world is the root of every sin." [^3]

After stating these three criticisms, he invites them to piety once more, saying: "So fear Allah and obey me" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ).

Then we reach the third part of Hud’s discourse, which is the description of Divine blessings upon the servants, in order to stimulate their sense of gratitude so that they might return to Allah.

In this regard, he uses the method of "brevity followed by detail," which is very effective for making discussions heart-felt. First, he addresses them, saying: "And fear Him who provided you with that which you know" and placed them at your disposal continuously and systematically (وَ اتَّقُوا الَّذِي أَمَدَّكُمْ بِما تَعْلَمُونَ). ^4

Then, after this brief statement, he proceeds to elaborate, saying: "He provided you with cattle and sons" (أَمَدَّكُمْ بِأَنْعامٍ وَ بَنِينَ).

On one hand, He provided you with material wealth—a significant part of which, especially in that era, was cattle and livestock—and on the other hand, sufficient human resources capable of protecting, maintaining, and nurturing it.

This expression is repeated in various verses of the Quran: when listing material blessings, it first mentions "wealth" (amwal) and then "human resources" (sons) who are the guardians and nurturers of wealth. This appears to be a natural order, not necessarily implying that wealth is of greater importance.

In Surah Al-Isra, verse 6, we read: "And We aided you with wealth and sons and made you more numerous in manpower."

Then he adds: "And gardens and springs" (وَ جَنَّاتٍ وَ عُيُونٍ). In this way, We made your life fruitful in terms of human resources, agriculture, gardening, livestock, and means of transport, such that you would not feel the slightest deficiency or discomfort in your life.

But how is it that you forgot the Giver of all these blessings? You sat at His table day and night yet did not recognize Him.

Then he moves to the final stage of his words, threatening and warning them of Divine punishment, saying: If you are ungrateful, "Indeed, I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day" (إِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ).

A day when you will see with your own eyes the result of all that oppression, pride, arrogance, passion-worship, and alienation from the Lord.

Usually, "Yawm ‘Adhim" (a Great/Terrible Day) in the Quran refers to the Day of Resurrection, which is great in every respect. However, in Quranic verses, it is sometimes also applied to the harsh and terrifying days that have passed over nations. Just as we read in this same Surah regarding the story of Shu‘ayb, after their stubbornness and persistence against the truth, Allah punished them with a painful penalty (a thunderbolt that descended from a cloud), and then refers to this day as a "Great Day": "So the punishment of the day of the shadow seized them. Indeed, it was the punishment of a terrible day."

Therefore, in the verse under discussion, "Yawm ‘Adhim" may also refer to the day when the arrogant ones of the people of ‘Ad were caught in the painful punishment of the crushing storm. A witness to this meaning is the description of their punishment a few verses later.

It may also refer to the punishment of the Day of Resurrection, or both punishments, as the history of both days is "great."

[^1]: Since "‘Ad" formed a "group" (jama‘at) and a "tribe" (qabilah), the verb "kadhdhabat" appears with the feminine "ta," as these two words are grammatically feminine. [^2]: "Majma‘ al-Bayan," under the verse discussed. [^3]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, under the verse discussed.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْنَآ أَوَعَظْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَكُن مِّنَ ٱلْوَٰعِظِينَ (١٣٦)
They said, ‘It is the same to us whether you exhort us or not. (136)
إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّا خُلُقُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٣٧)
These are nothing but the traditions of the ancients, (137)
وَمَا نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ (١٣٨)
and we will not be punished.’ (138)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَهْلَكْنَـٰهُمْ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٣٩)
So they impugned him, whereupon We destroyed them. There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (139)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٤٠)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (140)

Commentary

Do Not Warn Us, For It Has No Effect on Us!

In the preceding verses, we witnessed the profound dialogues of the compassionate Prophet, Hud عليه السلام, with the rebellious people of 'Ad. Now, let us examine the irrational and malicious responses of that people. The Quran states: "They said: Do not exhaust yourself; it is all the same to us whether you preach and advise or whether you are not among the preachers; it will not have the slightest effect on our hearts!" (قالُوا سَواءٌ عَلَيْنا أَ وَعَظْتَ أَمْ لَمْ تَكُنْ مِنَ الْواعِظِينَ).

Furthermore, they claimed that what you criticize us for is not subject to criticism: "This is nothing but the custom and character of the ancients" (إِنْ هذا إِلَّا خُلُقُ الْأَوَّلِينَ).

And contrary to what you say, "We shall never be punished, neither in this world nor in the next!" (وَ ما نَحْنُ بِمُعَذَّبِينَ).

Khuluq (with the vowel damma on the kha and lam) means habit, method, and character, as this word appears in the singular form meaning temperament and moral habit. In this context, it refers to the actions they were committing, such as idolatry, building sturdy and beautiful palaces, showing off by constructing towers on high points, and also being violent in punishment and retribution. That is to say: what we are doing is the same as what our predecessors did, and it cannot be a matter worthy of criticism.

Some have interpreted it to mean falsehood and lies, meaning: your words regarding God and the Resurrection are false claims that have been said before (but this would be the case if it were read as khalq [rhyming with halq], yet the famous recitation is not so).

Following this statement, the Quran describes the painful fate of this people as follows: "So they denied him, and We destroyed them" (فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَهْلَكْناهُمْ).

And at the end of this account, it repeats the same two content-rich and lesson-bearing sentences that were stated at the end of the stories of Noah, Abraham, and Moses.

It says: "Indeed, in this story is a clear sign and token of the power of Allah, the steadfastness of the Prophets, and the ominous end that befell the rebellious and the tyrants; yet even so, most of them did not believe" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً وَ ما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

"And indeed, your Lord is the Almighty, the Invincible, and the Merciful, the Compassionate" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ).

He shows forbearance to a sufficient degree, grants opportunity, and presents clear proofs for the guidance of the astray; however, at the time of punishment, He seizes so firmly that no room for escape remains for anyone!

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٤١)
[The people of] Thamud impugned the apostles (141)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ صَـٰلِحٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٤٢)
when Salih, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (142)
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٤٣)
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (143)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٤٤)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (144)
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٤٥)
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (145)
أَتُتْرَكُونَ فِى مَا هَـٰهُنَآ ءَامِنِينَ (١٤٦)
Will you be left secure in that which is here (146)
فِى جَنَّـٰتٍ وَعُيُونٍ (١٤٧)
—amid gardens and springs, (147)
وَزُرُوعٍ وَنَخْلٍ طَلْعُهَا هَضِيمٌ (١٤٨)
farms and date palms with dainty spathes (148)
وَتَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ ٱلْجِبَالِ بُيُوتًا فَـٰرِهِينَ (١٤٩)
—while you skillfully hew out houses from the mountains? (149)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٥٠)
So be wary of Allah and obey me, (150)
وَلَا تُطِيعُوٓا۟ أَمْرَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (١٥١)
and do not obey the dictates of the transgressors, (151)
ٱلَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ (١٥٢)
who cause corruption in the land and do not bring about reform.’ (152)

Commentary

Do Not Obey the Corrupt Extravagant Ones

The fifth section of the stories of the Prophets mentioned in this Surah is the concise and brief account of the people of "Thamud" and their Prophet "Salih" (عليه السلام), who lived in a land called "Wadi al-Qura" between "Medina" and "Sham" (Syria). They enjoyed a luxurious life, but due to rebellion and defiance, they were destroyed in such a manner that no trace of them remains today.

The beginning of this story is completely similar to the stories of the "People of 'Ad" and "Noah," showing how history repeats itself. It says: "The people of Thamud denied the messengers of Allah" (كَذَّبَتْ ثَمُودُ الْمُرْسَلِينَ).

This is because all messengers had a single call, and denying their Prophet Salih was, in reality, a denial of all the messengers.

After mentioning this summary, it proceeds to the details, saying: "When their brother Salih, who was concerned for them, said: 'Will you not fear Allah?'" (إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ صالِحٌ أَ لا تَتَّقُونَ). He, who was a kind guide and leader like a brother, sought no superiority or material interests. For this reason, the Quran refers to him as "their brother" (أخوهم). Like all divine Prophets, he began his call with piety (Taqwa) and a sense of responsibility.

Then, to introduce himself, he says: "Indeed, I am a trustworthy messenger to you," and my past record among you is a clear witness to this claim (إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ).

"So fear Allah and obey me," for nothing matters to me except the pleasure of Allah and your goodness and prosperity (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ).

And for this reason, "I do not ask you for any reward for this call," and I have no expectations from you (وَ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ). I work for another, and my reward is with Him; yes, "My reward is only upon the Lord of the worlds" (إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى‌ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

This was the first part of Salih's story, which is summarized in the presentation of his call and the declaration of his mission.

Then, in another section, he points to the sensitive and criticizable aspects of their lives, convicting them in a trial of conscience.

He says: "Do you imagine that you will be left forever in total security amidst these luxuries and blessings?" (أَ تُتْرَكُونَ فِي ما هاهُنا آمِنِينَ).

Do you think that this material and heedless life is eternal, and that the hand of death, vengeance, and punishment will not seize you?

Then, using the method of summary and detail, he explains his previous closed statement as follows: "Do you think [you will remain] in these gardens and springs..." (فِي جَنَّاتٍ وَ عُيُونٍ).

"In these crops and palm trees whose fruits are sweet, fresh, and ripe," will you remain forever? (وَ زُرُوعٍ وَ نَخْلٍ طَلْعُها هَضِيمٌ) 1

Then, referring to their sturdy and luxurious houses, he says: "And you carve houses out of the mountains with great skill [and vanity]" (وَ تَنْحِتُونَ مِنَ الْجِبالِ بُيُوتاً فارِهِينَ). "Farih" (فاره) from the root "Farah" (فره) originally means joy accompanied by heedlessness and egoism. Sometimes it also means skill in performing a task. Although both meanings are compatible with the above verse, considering the reproach and blame from Prophet Salih (عليه السلام), the first meaning seems more appropriate.

From the sum of these verses, compared to the attributes mentioned in previous verses regarding the people of 'Ad, it can be inferred that the people of 'Ad relied more on "egoism," "status-seeking," and "showing off," while the people of Thamud were more enslaved by their stomachs and a luxurious, pampered life. However, both shared a grim fate because they trampled upon the call of the Prophets to emerge from the prison of "self-worship" and reach "Allah-worship," and thus they received the punishment for their deeds.

After mentioning these criticisms, Prophet Salih (عليه السلام) moves to the third part of his speech and warns them: "So fear Allah and obey me" (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ).

"And do not obey the command of the extravagant (Musrifin)" (وَ لا تُطِيعُوا أَمْرَ الْمُسْرِفِينَ).

"Those who spread corruption in the land and never reform" (الَّذِينَ يُفْسِدُونَ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَ لا يُصْلِحُونَ).

Key Point: The Relationship Between Extravagance and Corruption on Earth

We know that extravagance (Israf) is the transgression of the limits of the laws of creation and the laws of legislation. It is also clear that in any sound system, any transgression of limits leads to corruption and disintegration. In other words, the source of corruption is extravagance, and the result of extravagance is corruption.

Of course, it should be noted that "Israf" has a broad meaning. Sometimes it is in simple matters like eating and drinking (as mentioned in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 31).

Sometimes it is in taking revenge and retribution beyond the limit (as mentioned in Surah Al-Isra, verse 33).

Sometimes it is in spending and charity beyond measure (as mentioned in Surah Al-Furqan, verse 67).

Sometimes it is in judging and passing verdicts that lead to falsehood and lies (as in Surah Ghafir, verse 28, where "extravagant" and "liar" are placed together).

Sometimes it is in beliefs that lead to doubt and hesitation (as in Surah Ghafir, verse 34, where "extravagant" and "doubter" appear together).

Sometimes it means seeking superiority, arrogance, and exploitation (as we read about Pharaoh in Surah Al-Dukhan, verse 31: "Indeed, he was haughty among the extravagant").

And finally, sometimes it means any kind of sin (as we read in Surah Az-Zumar, verse 53: "Say, 'O My servants who have transgressed against themselves [by sinning], do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed, Allah forgives all sins'").

Given what we have said, the relationship between extravagance and corruption becomes clearly evident.

According to "Allameh Tabataba'i" in "Al-Mizan," the universe is a single fabric of order and goodness, and even with the contradictions occasionally seen among its parts, it possesses great composition, harmony, and coordination. This system flows toward righteous goals and defines a path for each of its components. Now, if one of these components deviates from its orbit and takes the path of corruption, a conflict arises between it and the other components of this world. If they can return this disordered, extravagant part to its original orbit, all the better; otherwise, they destroy it so that the system may continue its course!

Man, who is one of the components of this universe, is not exempt from this general law. If he moves in his orbit based on his primordial nature (Fitra) and is in harmony with the system of existence, he reaches the blissful goal destined for him. But if he exceeds his limit and steps onto the path of "corruption on earth," first Allah disciplines him and warns him with painful and wrathful events, as we read in Surah Ar-Rum, verse 41: "Corruption has appeared throughout the land and sea by [reason of] what the hands of people have earned so He may let them taste part of [the consequence of] what they have done that perhaps they will return [to righteousness]."

But if even this is not effective and corruption takes root in the depths of his soul, Allah cleanses the face of the earth from the filth of such people's existence with the punishment of "extirpation" (Istisal) 1. From here, it becomes clear why in the above verses, Allah has mentioned "extravagance" in harmony with "corruption on earth" and the lack of reform.

1: Al-Mizan, Vol. 15, p. 333.

Ayahs

قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسَحَّرِينَ (١٥٣)
They said, ‘Indeed you are one of the bewitched. (153)
مَآ أَنتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا فَأْتِ بِـَٔايَةٍ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (١٥٤)
You are just a human being like us. So bring us a sign, should you be truthful.’ (154)
قَالَ هَـٰذِهِۦ نَاقَةٌ لَّهَا شِرْبٌ وَلَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (١٥٥)
He said, ‘This is a she-camel; she shall drink and you shall drink on known days. (155)
وَلَا تَمَسُّوهَا بِسُوٓءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٥٦)
Do not cause her any harm, for then you shall be seized by the punishment of a terrible day.’ (156)
فَعَقَرُوهَا فَأَصْبَحُوا۟ نَـٰدِمِينَ (١٥٧)
But they hamstrung her, whereupon they became regretful. (157)
فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٥٨)
So the punishment seized them. There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (158)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٥٩)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (159)

Commentary

The Obstinacy and Stubbornness of the People of Salih

In the preceding verses, you heard the reasoned and benevolent logic of Salih (peace be upon him) addressed to his misguided people; now, in these verses, hear the logic of the people in response to him.

They said: "O Salih, you are under the spell of sorcery and have lost your mind, which is why you speak such incoherent words!" (قالُوا إِنَّما أَنْتَ مِنَ الْمُسَحَّرِينَ).

Furthermore, they argued: "You are nothing but a human being like us," and no intellect permits us to obey a human being just like ourselves! (ما أَنْتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا). "If you are telling the truth, bring a sign and a miracle so that we may believe in you" (فَأْتِ بِآيَةٍ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ).

The word "مسحر" (musahhar) is derived from the root "سحر" (sihr), meaning someone who has been bewitched. They believed that sorcerers could sometimes disable a person's intellect and consciousness through magic. They leveled this accusation not only against Prophet Salih (peace be upon him) but also against a group of other prophets, and even against the Prophet of Islam (peace be upon him and his family), as we read in Surah Al-Furqan, verse 8: إِنْ تَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا رَجُلًا مَسْحُوراً: "The wrongdoers said: 'You are only following a man who has lost his mind due to being bewitched!'"

Indeed, they saw "intellect" as conforming to one's environment, following the trends of the day, and adapting oneself to all forms of corruption. If a divine and revolutionary man rose up to shatter corrupt beliefs and systems, according to this logic, they would call him mad, possessed, or bewitched.

Some commentators have suggested other possibilities for the meaning of "مسحرين" which are not very suitable, so we have bypassed them.

In any case, this rebellious group requested a miracle not out of a quest for truth, but as a pretext; nevertheless, the proof had to be completed against them. Therefore, by Allah's command, Salih "said: 'This is a she-camel; she shall have a share of the village's water, and you shall have a share on a designated day'" (قالَ هذِهِ ناقَةٌ لَها شِرْبٌ وَ لَكُمْ شِرْبُ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ).

"ناقة" (naqah) means a female camel. The Quran speaks concisely about the characteristics of this camel, which had a miraculous nature, and does not list its specific features. However, we know it was not an ordinary or typical camel. According to a group of commentators, this she-camel emerged miraculously from the heart of a mountain. One of its characteristics was that it would reserve and drink the settlement's water for one entire day, as alluded to in the above verse and in Surah Al-Qamar, verse 28.

Of course, other characteristics have been mentioned in some narrations regarding it. [^1]

[^1]: For further explanation in this regard, refer to Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 155, under the commentary of Surah Hud, verse 61.

In any case, Salih was commanded to announce to them that they must leave this extraordinary and supernatural camel—which was a sign of Allah's infinite power—alone. He commanded: "Do not touch her with any harm, for if you do, the punishment of a Great Day will seize you" (وَ لا تَمَسُّوها بِسُوءٍ فَيَأْخُذَكُمْ عَذابُ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ).

Naturally, the rebellious group, who were unwilling to allow the deceived masses to wake up and viewed the people's awareness as a threat to their interests, plotted to destroy the she-camel. "Finally, they attacked her and, with one or several blows, hamstrung her, and then they became regretful of what they had done," because they felt the divine punishment was only a few steps away (فَعَقَرُوها فَأَصْبَحُوا نادِمِينَ) [^1].

When their transgression exceeded all bounds and they practically demonstrated that they were not ready to accept the truth, Allah's will was set to cleanse the earth of their presence: "At this moment, the divine punishment seized them" (فَأَخَذَهُمُ الْعَذابُ).

As briefly mentioned in Surah Al-A'raf, verse 78, and Surah Hud, verse 67, first a severe earthquake shook their land. When they woke up and sat on their knees, the event gave them no respite; a lethal thunderbolt accompanied by the earthquake brought the walls down upon them, and in that same state, they lost their lives amidst an extraordinary terror.

[^1]: "عقروها" is from the root "عقر" (aqr), which originally means the root or foundation of something. It is also used to mean slaughtering, or hamstringing an animal (cutting its lower legs and bringing it to the ground).

At the end of this account, the Quran says the same thing it stated at the end of the stories of the people of Hud, the people of Noah, and the people of Abraham. It says: "In this story of the people of Salih, and all the steadfastness and endurance of this great Prophet and that eloquent logic, and also the stubbornness, obstinacy, and opposition of those dark-faced ones against the awakening miracle and the grim fate they suffered, there is a sign and a lesson; yet most of them did not believe" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً وَ ما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

Indeed, no one can overcome the power of Allah, just as this great power does not prevent His mercy toward His friends or even toward His enemies. "And indeed, your Lord is the Almighty, the Merciful" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ) [^1].

[^1]: According to narrations, the one who killed the she-camel of Thamud was only one person, yet this act is mentioned in the Quran in the "plural form." This is because the others were in agreement and harmony with him and were satisfied with his deed. From here, a window opens to a fundamental principle: that intellectual and ideological bonds tie the fates of individuals together. For a more detailed discussion on this subject, see Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 158 (under the commentary of Surah Hud, verse 65).

Ayahs

كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ ٱلْمُرْسَلِينَ (١٦٠)
The people of Lot impugned the apostles (160)
إِذْ قَالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ لُوطٌ أَلَا تَتَّقُونَ (١٦١)
when Lot, their brother, said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (161)
إِنِّى لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ (١٦٢)
Indeed I am a trusted apostle [sent] to you. (162)
فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُونِ (١٦٣)
So be wary of Allah and obey me. (163)
وَمَآ أَسْـَٔلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ ۖ إِنْ أَجْرِىَ إِلَّا عَلَىٰ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦٤)
I do not ask you any reward for it; my reward lies only with the Lord of all the worlds. (164)
أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلذُّكْرَانَ مِنَ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٦٥)
What! Of all people do you come to males, (165)
وَتَذَرُونَ مَا خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُم مِّنْ أَزْوَٰجِكُم ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ عَادُونَ (١٦٦)
abandoning your wives your Lord has created for you? Indeed, you are a transgressing lot.’ (166)

Commentary

A Disgraceful and Transgressive People! (The People of Lut)

The sixth prophet whose life and the story of his misguided people are partially recounted in this Surah is Prophet Lut عليه السلام. Although he lived during the era of Ibrahim عليه السلام, the account of his life is not mentioned immediately after Ibrahim's. This is because the Quran is not a book of history intended to relate events in chronological order; rather, it focuses on aspects of education and human development, which necessitates different arrangements. The life of Lut and the story of his people share a greater harmony with the lives of the prophets mentioned recently.

First, it says: "The people of Lut denied the messengers" (كَذَّبَتْ قَوْمُ لُوطٍ الْمُرْسَلِينَ).

As we have previously noted, the mention of "messengers" (الْمُرْسَلِينَ) in the plural form is either due to the unity of the prophets' call—where denying one is considered a denial of them all—or because they truly did not believe in any of the preceding prophets.

Then, it refers to the call of Prophet Lut, which is consistent with the nature of the calls of other past prophets, saying: "When their brother Lut said to them: 'Will you not fear Allah?'" (إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ أَخُوهُمْ لُوطٌ أَ لا تَتَّقُونَ).

The tone of his speech and his deep, extraordinary compassion showed that he spoke like a brother. Then he added: "Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger" (إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ).

Have you ever seen any betrayal from me until now? From now on, too, I will certainly maintain trustworthiness regarding the Divine revelation and in delivering the message of your Lord.

"Now that it is so: fear Allah, and obey me," for I am the leader on the path to your salvation (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ).

Do not think that this call is a means for livelihood or pursues a material goal; no, "I do not ask you for any reward for it; my reward is only from the Lord of the worlds" (وَ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى‌ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

Then he begins to criticize their indecent acts and some of their moral deviations. Since the most significant point of their deviation was sexual deviation and homosexuality, he emphasized this issue and said: "Do you approach males among the worlds?" (أَ تَأْتُونَ الذُّكْرانَ مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ). Meaning, despite the fact that Allah has created so many individuals of the opposite sex for you, and you could have a pure and peaceful life through correct and healthy marriage with them, you have abandoned this pure and natural blessing of Allah and defiled yourselves with such a low and disgraceful act!

There is also a possibility in the interpretation of the verse that "among the worlds" (مِنَ الْعالَمِينَ) serves as a qualifier for the people themselves; meaning, you alone among the inhabitants of the worlds have taken up this deviation. This aligns with some historical accounts stating that the first people to commit homosexuality on a wide scale were the people of Lut [1]. However—

[1]: Regarding the cause of the deviation of these disgraceful people, a story is mentioned in histories which we provided in Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 197 (under verse 81 of Surah Hud).

The first interpretation is more suitable for the following verse.

Then he added: "And leave those whom your Lord has created for you as your wives?" (وَ تَذَرُونَ ما خَلَقَ لَكُمْ رَبُّكُمْ مِنْ أَزْواجِكُمْ).

"Rather, you are a transgressing people!" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ عادُونَ).

Never has a natural need, whether spiritual or physical, drawn you to this deviant act; rather, it is transgression and rebellion that has stained your reputation with such disgrace.

Your action is like a person who leaves fragrant fruits and nourishing, healthy, and natural foods to go after poisoned, contaminated, and deadly food. This is not a natural need; this is transgression and rebellion.

Key Points

1- Homosexuality: A Shameful Deviation

In the Holy Quran, in Surahs Al-A'raf, Hud, Al-Hijr, Al-Anbiya, An-Naml, and Al-Ankabut, there are references to the situation of the people of Lut and their heinous sin. However, in each case, the expression differs from the others. In reality, each of these expressions points to one of the sinister dimensions of this disgraceful act:

In Al-A'raf, we read that Lut says to them: "Rather, you are a people committing excess" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ مُسْرِفُونَ) (Al-A'raf: 81).

In Surah Al-Anbiya, we read: "And We saved him from the city that was doing wicked deeds. Indeed, they were a people of evil, defiantly disobedient" (وَ نَجَّيْناهُ مِنَ الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي كانَتْ تَعْمَلُ الْخَبائِثَ إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَوْمَ سَوْءٍ فاسِقِينَ) (Al-Anbiya: 74).

And in the verse under discussion, we read that Lut says to them: "Rather, you are a transgressing people" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ عادُونَ).

In Surah An-Naml, it says: "Rather, you are a people behaving ignorantly" (بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ) (An-Naml: 55).

And in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 29, it is stated from the tongue of Lut as he says to them: "Indeed, you approach men and obstruct the way" (إِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجالَ وَ تَقْطَعُونَ السَّبِيلَ)—meaning you cut off the path of nature and human procreation [1]. In this way, this ugly act is presented under the titles of "excess" (israf), "wickedness" (khaba'ith), "disobedience" (fisq), "transgression" (udwan), "ignorance" (jahl), and "obstructing the way" (qat' al-sabil).

It is called "excess" because they forgot the system of creation in this matter and exceeded the limit; "transgression" is applied to it for the same reason.

"Wickedness" (khabith) refers to a contaminated act or thing that a healthy human nature finds repulsive, and what act is more repulsive than this?

"Disobedience" (fisq) means exiting the obedience of the Lord and stripping away human character, which is an inevitable consequence of this act.

"Ignorance" (jahl) is due to being unaware of the sinister consequences this act has for the individual and society.

And finally, "obstructing the way" (qat' al-sabil) is the sinister result of this act which, if spread, leads to the termination of the human race, as the attraction to the same sex gradually replaces the natural attraction to the opposite sex.

2- The Sinister Consequences of Homosexuality

Although in the ninth volume of Tafsir Nemouneh (in the commentary on verses 81-83 of Surah Hud), we had a detailed discussion regarding the harms of this act, due to the importance of the subject, it is necessary to add more points to it.

In a hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "The scent of Paradise will not reach the one who is the passive partner in homosexuality!" [1]

In another hadith from Ali عليه السلام, we read that he "introduced sodomy as being on the verge of disbelief" [2].

In a hadith from Imam Ali ibn Musa al-Rida عليه السلام regarding the philosophy of the prohibition of homosexuality, it is stated: "The reason for the prohibition of males for males and females for females is because of how females are fashioned and how males are naturally disposed, and because in males approaching males and females approaching females, there is the cutting off of progeny, the corruption of social order, and the ruin of the world." [3]

This issue is so ugly and disgraceful from the perspective of Islam that in the chapters of Islamic penal codes (Hudud), its punishment is undoubtedly considered to be execution. Even for those who perform lower stages of homosexual acts, severe punishments are mentioned. For instance, in a hadith from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever kisses a boy out of lust, Allah will bridle him on the Day of Resurrection with a bridle of fire!" [1]

The punishment for one who commits such an act is mentioned as being from thirty to ninety-nine lashes.

In any case, there is no doubt that sexual deviation is among the most dangerous deviations that can appear in human societies, as it casts its sinister shadow over all moral issues and draws man toward emotional deviation (we have provided a detailed discussion on this in Volume 9, page 194 onwards, under verse 81 of Surah Hud).

[1]: Some have interpreted the phrase "obstructing the way" (تَقْطَعُونَ السَّبِيلَ) to mean that the people of Lut were also thieves and highway robbers. [1] and [2]: Bihar al-Anwar, New Edition, Vol. 79, p. 67. [3]: Previous source, p. 64. [1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 79, p. 72.

Ayahs

قَالُوا۟ لَئِن لَّمْ تَنتَهِ يَـٰلُوطُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلْمُخْرَجِينَ (١٦٧)
They said, ‘Lot, if you do not desist, you will surely be banished.’ (167)
قَالَ إِنِّى لِعَمَلِكُم مِّنَ ٱلْقَالِينَ (١٦٨)
He said, ‘Indeed I detest your conduct.’ (168)
رَبِّ نَجِّنِى وَأَهْلِى مِمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ (١٦٩)
‘My Lord! Deliver me and my family from what they do.’ (169)
فَنَجَّيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ أَجْمَعِينَ (١٧٠)
So We delivered him and all his family, (170)
إِلَّا عَجُوزًا فِى ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (١٧١)
except an old woman who remained behind. (171)
ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا ٱلْـَٔاخَرِينَ (١٧٢)
Then We destroyed [all] the rest, (172)
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَسَآءَ مَطَرُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (١٧٣)
and rained down upon them a rain [of stones]. Evil was the rain of those who were warned! (173)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٧٤)
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (174)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٧٥)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (175)

Commentary

The Fate of the People of Lut

The people of Lut, who were intoxicated by the wine of lust and pride, instead of accepting the counsels of this divine leader with heart and soul and rescuing themselves from the quagmire in which they were submerged, rose up to oppose him. They "said: O Lut! Enough, be silent; if you do not desist, you will surely be among those expelled [from this city]" (قالُوا لَئِنْ لَمْ تَنْتَهِ يا لُوطُ لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُخْرَجِينَ).

Your words disturb our thoughts and disrupt our peace; we are not even willing to hear such talk, and if you continue in this manner, your minimum punishment will be exile from this land.

In another part of the Quranic verses, we read that they put this threat on the verge of execution and ordered: "Drive out the family of Lut from your city, for they are people who keep themselves pure [and do not commit sin]!" (أَخْرِجُوهُمْ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ إِنَّهُمْ أُناسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ).

The state of a misguided and polluted people reaches a point where purity and piety become the greatest flaw among them, while impurity and pollution become a source of pride! This is the grim fate of a society that rushes headlong toward corruption.

From the phrase "لَتَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْمُخْرَجِينَ" (you will surely be among those expelled), it can be inferred that this corrupt population had previously driven out a group of pure individuals whom they saw as obstacles to their foul deeds. They threatened Lut that if he continued his path, he too would suffer the same fate.

In some commentaries, it is explicitly stated that they used to expel chaste individuals in the worst possible manner. [^1]

However, Lut, without paying any heed to their threats, continued his words and "said: Indeed, I am of those who detest your deeds" (قالَ إِنِّي لِعَمَلِكُمْ مِنَ الْقالِينَ). That is to say: I will continue my protests; do whatever is in your power, for I do not fear these threats in the way of Allah and in the struggle against abominations! The expression "من القالين" (of those who detest) again indicates that another group of believers had joined their voices with Lut and had begun to protest vehemently, even though the rebellious people eventually removed them from the scene.

"قالين" is the plural of "قال" from the root "قلى" (rhyming with halq and shirk), meaning an intense enmity that affects the depths of a person's heart and soul. This expression clarifies the intensity of Lut's loathing toward their actions.

It is interesting that Lut عليه السلام says: "I am an enemy to your deeds," meaning I have no personal enmity or private grudge against you as individuals; rather, it is your action that is shameful. If you cast away these deeds, I will be your sincere friend.

Ultimately, all those counsels and advice had no effect. Corruption turned their entire society into a foul-smelling swamp, and the completion of the proof (itmam al-hujjah) was sufficient. The mission of Lut عليه السلام reached its end. It is here that he must save himself and those who believed in his call from this polluted region, so that the deadly divine punishment may crush the shameful people.

In the position of supplication and request before the threshold of Allah, he pleaded: "My Lord! Save me and my family from what they do" (رَبِّ نَجِّنِي وَ أَهْلِي مِمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ).

Although some have suggested that "family" (ahl) refers to all those who believed in him, verse 36 of Surah Adh-Dhariyat says: "We found therein only one household of Muslims" (فَما وَجَدْنا فِيها غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ).

However, as we previously noted, some expressions in the verses under discussion indicate that a group had believed in him earlier and had been exiled from that land.

Furthermore, from what has been said, the reality becomes clear that Lut's prayer for his family was not based on emotional aspects or kinship ties, but rather because of their faith.

Allah answered this prayer, as He says: "So We saved him and his family, all of them" (فَنَجَّيْناهُ وَ أَهْلَهُ أَجْمَعِينَ).

"Except an old woman who remained among those who stayed behind" (إِلَّا عَجُوزاً فِي الْغابِرِينَ). [^1]

[^1]: "غابر" (ghabir) is from the root "غبور" (ghubur), meaning the remainder of something. Whenever a population moves from a point and someone remains, they are called "غابر". For this reason, the remaining dust is called "غبار" (ghubar), and "غبره" (ghubrah) refers to the milk remaining in an animal's udder.

This old woman was none other than the wife of Lut, who, in terms of belief and religion, was in harmony with that misguided people and never believed in Lut. Ultimately, she suffered the same fate, the details of which are found in Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 178 onwards.

Yes, Allah saved Lut and the few believers with him. By Allah's command, they departed from the land of the polluted by night, leaving those drowned in corruption and shame to themselves. At the break of dawn, the command for divine punishment arrived. A terrifying earthquake overtook their land; their prosperous cities, beautiful palaces, and luxurious yet shame-filled lives were completely overturned, as the Quran states here in one short sentence: "Then We destroyed the others" (ثُمَّ دَمَّرْنَا الْآخَرِينَ).

"And We rained upon them a rain" (But what a rain! A rain of stones that even erased their ruins from sight!) (وَ أَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْهِمْ مَطَراً). "And evil was the rain of those who were warned" (فَساءَ مَطَرُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ).

Ordinary rains are life-giving and reviving, but this rain was terrifying, destructive, and devastating.

From verse 82 of Surah Hud, it is understood that first the cities of the people of Lut were overturned, then a rain of dense pebbles descended upon them. As we said in the commentary of that verse, the rain of stones was perhaps so that their traces would also be erased, leaving a mound of stone and earth in place of those prosperous cities.

Were these stones torn from the deserts by a great storm and dropped upon them? Or were they wandering celestial stones that fell there by divine command?

Or, as some say, was there a dormant volcano nearby that erupted by Allah's command and showered them with a rain of stones? It is not exactly known. What is certain is that this deadly rain left no trace of life in that polluted land.

(A detailed account of the story of the people of Lut is found in Volume 9 of Tafsir Nemouneh, pages 178 to 198, and in Volume 11, from pages 104 to 118, with its various points).

Again, at the end of this story, we reach the same two sentences that appear at the end of similar stories in this Surah regarding five other great Prophets. He says: "Indeed, in the story of this oppressive and shameful people and their grim and deadly end, there is a sign [and a lesson]" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً).

"But most of them were not believers" (وَ ما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

What sign could be clearer than this, which acquaints you with important and fate-determining issues without the need for personal experience?

Yes, the history of those who passed is a lesson and a sign for those who come after. It is not even an "experience" in the sense that in an experience, a person must endure losses to gain results; rather, here, the results accrue to us from the losses of others.

"And indeed, your Lord—He is the All-Mighty, the All-Merciful" (وَ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ). What mercy is greater than this: that He does not immediately punish such polluted nations and gives them sufficient respite for guidance and reconsideration?

And also, what mercy is greater than this: that His punishment does not burn the "dry and the wet" together; even if there is only one believing family among thousands upon thousands of polluted families, He saves them.

And what might and power is higher than this: that in the blink of an eye, He so overturns the land of the polluted that no trace of it remains? He commissions the earth, which was the cradle of their comfort, to be the agent of their death, and turns the life-giving rain into a rain of death!

when Shu‘ayb said to them, ‘Will you not be wary [of Allah]? (177)
