Commentary

Shu'ayb and the "People of the Wood" (Ashab al-Aykah)

This is the seventh and final installment in the series of stories of the prophets mentioned in this Surah, and it is the story of the great Prophet of Allah, "Shu'ayb," and his rebellious people.

This Prophet lived in the land of "Madyan" (a city in the south of the Levant) and "Aykah" (rhyming with Laylah), a well-known settlement near Madyan.

Verse 79 of Surah Al-Hijr bears witness to the fact that the land of Aykah was located on the route taken by the people of Hijaz toward the Levant (Sham).

First, it says: "The People of the Wood (Aykah) denied the messengers" (كَذَّبَ أَصْحابُ الْأَيْكَةِ الْمُرْسَلِينَ).

Not only was Shu'ayb—the Prophet sent specifically to them—denied by them, but all other prophets were also denied by them due to the unity of the prophets' call; or perhaps they fundamentally did not accept any of the heavenly religions.

"Aykah" originally means a place with tangled trees, which we refer to in Persian as a "thicket" (bisheh). The land located near Madyan was named "Aykah" because of its abundant water and many trees. Evidence suggests they lived a life of luxury and possessed great wealth, and perhaps for this very reason, they were drowned in pride and negligence!

Then, elaborating on this summary, it says: "When Shu'ayb said to them: 'Will you not fear [Allah]?'" (إِذْ قالَ لَهُمْ شُعَيْبٌ أَ لا تَتَّقُونَ). In truth, Shu'ayb's call began from the same point as that of other prophets: the call to Taqwa (piety and God-wariness), which is the root and essence of all reform programs and moral and social transformations.

It is noteworthy that in this story, the expression "their brother" (akhuhum), which appeared in the stories of Salih, Hud, Nuh, and Lut, is not seen. This is perhaps because Shu'ayb was originally from Madyan and only had kinship ties with the people there, not with the people of Aykah. Therefore, in Surah Hud, verse 84, when speaking only of "Madyan," this expression is used: "And to Madyan [We sent] their brother Shu'ayb" (وَ إِلى‌ مَدْيَنَ أَخاهُمْ شُعَيْباً). However, since the verse under discussion speaks of the "People of Aykah," and they had no kinship with Shu'ayb, this expression was not mentioned.

He then added: "Indeed, I am to you a trustworthy messenger" (إِنِّي لَكُمْ رَسُولٌ أَمِينٌ).

"So fear Allah and obey me" (for my obedience is His obedience) (فَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَ أَطِيعُونِ).

Know also that: "I do not ask you for any reward for this [call]; my reward is only from the Lord of the worlds" (وَ ما أَسْئَلُكُمْ عَلَيْهِ مِنْ أَجْرٍ إِنْ أَجْرِيَ إِلَّا عَلى‌ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ).

These are the same unified and carefully calculated statements that appeared at the beginning of the calls of other prophets: the call to Taqwa, emphasizing a history of trustworthiness among the people, and stressing that this divine call has only a spiritual motive with no material expectations from anyone, so that excuse-seekers and pessimists cannot use it as a means to flee.

Shu'ayb, like the other prophets whose history has been partially recounted in this Surah, after his general call to Taqwa and obedience to Allah's command, pointed his finger at the moral and social deviations of that environment in the second part of his teachings. He criticized them, and since the most significant deviation of these affluent people was economic disorder, blatant injustice, the violation of rights, and exploitation, he emphasized these issues more than anything else. First, he says: "Give full measure" (do not give short measure).

(أَوْفُوا الْكَيْلَ).

"And do not be of those who cause loss" (وَ لا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُخْسِرِينَ).

"And weigh with an even and correct balance" (وَ زِنُوا بِالْقِسْطاسِ الْمُسْتَقِيمِ) [1]

[1]: "Qistas" (rhyming with miqyas) means a scale. Some consider it a Roman word and others Arabic. Some believe Qistas refers to a large scale and Mizan to a small scale, and that Qistas is a scale with a pointer (tongue), which is why it shows weight accurately.

"And do not deprive people of their due, and do not disparage the goods and possessions of the people" (وَ لا تَبْخَسُوا النَّاسَ أَشْياءَهُمْ).

"And do not commit evil in the earth, spreading corruption" (وَ لا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ).

In these last three verses, Shu'ayb gives "five commands" in short and calculated phrases to this misguided people. Some commentators have imagined that these are mostly reinforcements of one another, while careful scrutiny shows that these five commands actually refer to five fundamental and distinct matters, or in other words, four commands and one general summary.

To clarify this difference, it is necessary to note the fact that the people of Shu'ayb (the people of Aykah and Madyan) were located in a sensitive commercial region on the route of caravans traveling from Hijaz to the Levant, and from the Levant to Hijaz and other regions.

We know that these caravans develop many needs in the middle of their journey, and sometimes the people of the cities along the route take maximum advantage of these needs. They buy the caravans' goods at the lowest price and sell their own goods at the highest price (note that at that time, many transactions were conducted as barter).

Sometimes they find a thousand faults with the goods they buy, while they praise the goods they sell in return a hundredfold. When weighing and measuring, they measure their own goods precisely and sometimes give short measure, but they weigh others' goods with indifference or sometimes take more than the required amount. Since the other party is in need regardless, they are forced to submit to all these issues! Aside from the caravans on the route, the local people who were weaker and had lower incomes and were forced to trade their goods with the powerful capitalists fared no better.

The price of the commodity—whether what they bought or what they sold—was determined by the will of those wealthy individuals. The measure and weight were also always in their hands, and these poor, oppressed people had to be as submissive as "a corpse in the hands of a washer."

With what we have said in mind, we return to the various expressions in the above verses:

In one instance, he commands them to fulfill the right of the measure (kayl), and in another, to weigh with a correct balance (qistas). We know that the measurement of goods is either by volume (kayl) or by weight (wazn). He specifically points to each of these separately to place more emphasis on the command that they should not engage in short-changing in any case.

Furthermore, short-changing has various methods: sometimes the scale and measure are correct but the right is not fulfilled, and sometimes the scale is broken and the measure is incorrect or fraudulent. The above verses refer to all of these.

After clarifying these two expressions, we turn to "la tabkhasu," which is derived from the root bakhs. It originally means to unjustly diminish the rights of people, and sometimes it means fraud and trickery that leads to the loss of others' rights. Therefore, the above sentence has a broad meaning that includes any kind of adulteration, fraud, deception, and trickery in transactions, and any kind of trampling on the rights of others.

As for the phrase "وَ لا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُخْسِرِينَ" (and do not be of those who cause loss), considering that mukhshir means someone who exposes a person or thing to loss, it also has a broad meaning that, in addition to short-measuring, encompasses any factor that causes loss and detriment to the party in a transaction.

In this way, all forms of exploitation, injustice, and wrongdoing in transactions, and any kind of harmful fraud and effort—whether in quantity or quality—are included in the above commands.

And since economic disorders become the source of the disintegration of the social system, at the end of these commands, as a summary, he says: "وَ لا تَعْثَوْا فِي الْأَرْضِ مُفْسِدِينَ" (And do not commit evil in the earth, spreading corruption). That is, do not corrupt the earth, do not lead societies to ruin, and end all forms of exploitation, unjust profiteering, and the violation of others' rights.

These were not only effective instructions for the wealthy and oppressive society of Shu'ayb's time but are effective and problem-solving for every age and time, leading to economic justice.

Then, in his final command in this part of his speech, Shu'ayb once again calls them to Taqwa and says: "And fear Him who created you and the former generations" (وَ اتَّقُوا الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ وَ الْجِبِلَّةَ الْأَوَّلِينَ). You are not the only people and population to have walked upon this earth; before you, your fathers and other nations came and went. Do not forget their past and your own future.

"Jibillah" is from jabal, meaning mountain. It is used for a large group of people who are as great as a mountain; some have mentioned the number as ten thousand.

Also, the nature and primordial disposition (fitrah) of man is called jibillah because it is unchangeable, like a mountain that cannot be moved.

The above expression may also refer to the fact that what I have said regarding the abandonment of oppression and corruption, the fulfillment of people's rights, and the observance of justice has been within the primordial nature of humans from the first day, and I have come to revive your pure nature.

But unfortunately, the words of this compassionate and awakening Prophet did not affect them, and we will see their bitter and ugly response to this logical discourse in the coming verses.

Ayahs

قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّمَآ أَنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسَحَّرِينَ (١٨٥)
They said, ‘Indeed you are one of the bewitched. (185)
وَمَآ أَنتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُنَا وَإِن نَّظُنُّكَ لَمِنَ ٱلْكَـٰذِبِينَ (١٨٦)
You are just a human being like us, and we indeed consider you to be a liar. (186)
فَأَسْقِطْ عَلَيْنَا كِسَفًا مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ إِن كُنتَ مِنَ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ (١٨٧)
Make a fragment of the sky falls upon us, should you be truthful.’ (187)
قَالَ رَبِّىٓ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ (١٨٨)
He said, ‘My Lord knows best what you are doing.’ (188)
فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمْ عَذَابُ يَوْمِ ٱلظُّلَّةِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ (١٨٩)
So they impugned him, and then they were overtaken by the punishment of the day of the overshadowing cloud. It was indeed the punishment of a terrible day. (189)
إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً ۖ وَمَا كَانَ أَكْثَرُهُم مُّؤْمِنِينَ (١٩٠)
There is indeed a sign in that; but most of them do not have faith. (190)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ (١٩١)
Indeed your Lord is the All-mighty, the All-merciful. (191)

Commentary

The Fate of This Stubborn People!

The oppressive and tyrannical group, finding themselves without an argument against the logical words of Shu'ayb, unleashed a flood of accusations and lies against him in order to continue their self-indulgence.

First, they applied the same perennial label that criminals and tyrants have always used against the prophets, saying: "You are only one of those bewitched!" (قالُوا إِنَّما أَنْتَ مِنَ الْمُسَحَّرِينَ) [1]. They implied: "Not a single logical word is found in your speech, and do you imagine that with such talk you can prevent us from our freedom of action regarding our wealth?!"

Furthermore, they said: "You are but a human being like us; what expectation do you have that we should follow you? What virtue or superiority do you possess over us?" (وَ ما أَنْتَ إِلَّا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُنا). "Our only thought concerning you is that you are a liar!" (وَ إِنْ نَظُنُّكَ لَمِنَ الْكاذِبِينَ).

After uttering these contradictory statements—sometimes calling him a liar and an opportunistic man who seeks superiority over them through these means, and sometimes calling him insane—their final word was: "Very well, if you are truthful, then cause fragments of the sky to fall upon us" (فَأَسْقِطْ عَلَيْنا كِسَفاً مِنَ السَّماءِ إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الصَّادِقِينَ). "Afflict us with the very punishment you threaten us with, so you may know we do not fear these threats!"

"Kisaf" (rhyming with padar) is the plural of kisfah (rhyming with qit'ah), meaning a piece or fragment. The "fragments of the sky" refer to pieces of stone falling from the heavens.

In this manner, they reached the peak of shamelessness and insolence, manifesting their disbelief and denial in the worst possible form.

In the face of these discordant words, ugly expressions, and the demand for divine punishment, the only response Shu'ayb gave was: "He said: My Lord is best aware of what you do" (قالَ رَبِّي أَعْلَمُ بِما تَعْمَلُونَ). This was an indication that "this matter is beyond my control; the falling of heavenly stones and other punishments have not been entrusted to me as you demand. He knows your deeds and is aware of the extent of your desert. Whenever He sees you deserving of punishment, and when warnings and advice no longer avail and the proof has been sufficiently established, He will send down the torment and uproot you."

This expression, and similar ones found in other stories of the prophets, clearly shows that they deferred everything to the permission and command of Allah and never claimed to possess anything of their own.

However, the time eventually arrived to cleanse the face of the earth of these defiled people.

As the Quran states in the following verse: "So they denied him, and then the punishment of the 'Day of the Overshadowing Cloud' seized them!" (فَكَذَّبُوهُ فَأَخَذَهُمْ عَذابُ يَوْمِ الظُّلَّةِ).

"And indeed, it was the punishment of a great day" (إِنَّهُ كانَ عَذابُ يَوْمٍ عَظِيمٍ).

"Zullah" originally means a piece of cloud that casts a shadow. Many commentators have narrated under this verse that for seven days, a scorching heat engulfed their land, and absolutely no breeze blew. Suddenly, a piece of cloud appeared in the sky and a breeze began to blow. They rushed out of their houses and took refuge under the shadow of the cloud due to their intense distress.

At that moment, a deadly thunderbolt rose from the cloud—a thunderbolt with a deafening sound—followed by fire raining down upon them, and an earthquake shook the ground, and they were all destroyed.

We know that a "thunderbolt" (lightning strike), which results from the exchange of powerful electricity between the cloud and the earth, possesses both a terrifying sound and a massive fiery spark, and is sometimes accompanied by a severe tremor at the site of the strike. Thus, the various descriptions regarding the punishment of the people of Shu'ayb in different Surahs of the Quran all return to a single reality: in Surah Al-A'raf (verse 91) it is described as "Rajfah" (earthquake), in Surah Hud (verse 94) as "Sayhah" (mighty blast), and in the verses under discussion as "the punishment of the Day of the Overshadowing Cloud."

Although some commentators like Al-Qurtubi and Fakhr al-Razi have suggested the possibility that the "Companions of the Wood" (Aykah) and "Madyan" were two separate groups, each receiving a distinct punishment, a careful examination of the relevant verses reveals that this possibility is not very significant.

At the end of this story, the Quran states the same conclusion found at the end of the previous six stories of the great prophets:

It says: "Indeed, in the story of the people of the land of Aykah, the compassionate invitation of their prophet Shu'ayb, their stubbornness, persistence, and denials, and finally the destruction of this oppressive people by the deadly thunderbolt, there is a sign and a lesson" (إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآيَةً).

"But most of them did not believe" (وَ ما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ).

Despite this, the Compassionate and Merciful Allah gave them sufficient respite to come to their senses and reform themselves. When they became deserving of punishment, He seized them with His overwhelming power. Indeed, "Your Lord is the Almighty, the Merciful" (وَ إِنْ رَبَّكَ لَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الرَّحِيمُ).

Key Points

1. Complete Harmony in the Invitation of the Prophets

At the conclusion of the stories of these seven great prophets, which form a complete cycle of educational lessons, it is necessary to note that while the stories of these prophets appear in other Surahs of the Quran, they are nowhere presented in this specific form—where the beginning of all their invitations is harmonious, and the end of all of them is likewise expressed harmoniously.

In five parts of these stories, the content of the invitation is "Taqwa" (piety/God-consciousness), followed by an emphasis on the prophet's trustworthiness and his refusal to demand any wage or reward.

After that, the most significant deviations prevalent in those environments are criticized in a friendly and logical tone.

Then, the ugly and offensive reaction of the deviant nations is presented, and finally, their painful punishment—which took a different form in each case—is sent down.

At the end of all seven stories, reference is made to them being a "sign" and a "lesson," and to the lack of faith among the majority of these misguided peoples.

And again, at the end of all these, emphasis is placed on the "Power" and "Mercy" of Allah.

This harmony, above all, reflects "Tawhid" (Monotheism) in the invitation of the prophets, showing they possessed a single program with a unified beginning and end. They were all teachers of the classes of human development. Although the content of these classes had to change with the passage of time and the progress of human society, the principles, foundations, and results were all the same. Furthermore, this served as a consolation for the Prophet (peace be upon him and his family) and the few early believers—and for believers in every age—that they should not fear the multitude of opponents or the majority of a misguided people, and should remain 100% hopeful in the outcome of their work.

It is also a warning and an admonition for tyrants, oppressors, and the misguided in every age not to consider divine punishment far from themselves—punishments such as earthquakes, thunderbolts, deadly storms, volcanoes, the cleaving of the earth, and torrential rains, against which modern humans are just as helpless as those of the past! For modern man, despite all his power and industrial progress, remains extremely weak, vulnerable, and helpless against storms, floods, lightning, and earthquakes.

All of this is because the goal of Quranic stories is the growth and perfection of human beings; the goal is light and illumination in the souls and the control of rebellious desires; ultimately, the goal is the struggle against oppression, tyranny, and deviation.

2. The Beginning of Every Invitation was Taqwa

It is noteworthy that a significant portion of these same stories of the prophets appears in Surah Hud and Surah Al-A'raf, but there they usually begin with the call to Tawhid and the oneness of God, starting with the phrase: "O my people, worship Allah; you have no deity other than Him" (يا قَوْمِ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ ما لَكُمْ مِنْ إِلهٍ غَيْرُهُ).

However, in this Surah (Ash-Shu'ara), as you observed, the first call was to Taqwa (أَ لا تَتَّقُونَ). In reality, both lead to the same result, for unless there is at least a minimum level of Taqwa—meaning truth-seeking and a quest for reality—in a person, neither the call to Tawhid nor anything else will be effective. Thus, we read at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah: "This is the Book about which there is no doubt, a guidance for the God-fearing (Muttaqin)" (ذلِكَ الْكِتابُ لا رَيْبَ فِيهِ هُدىً لِلْمُتَّقِينَ).

Of course, Taqwa has levels, and each level is a foundation for the next.

Again, this difference is seen between the Surah under discussion (Ash-Shu'ara) and Surah Al-A'raf and Surah Hud: there, the emphasis was mostly on the struggle against idolatry, and other issues were overshadowed. But here, the emphasis is on moral and social deviations such as boasting and seeking superiority, extravagance and hedonism, sexual deviations, exploitation, short-changing, and fraud. This shows that the repetition of these stories in the Quran follows a precise calculation and pursues a specific goal in each instance.

  1. It is noteworthy that the nations mentioned in various sections of this Surah, in addition to deviating from the principle of Tawhid toward Shirk (polytheism) and idolatry—which was the common denominator among them all—possessed specific moral and social deviations that distinguished them:

Some were people of boasting and arrogance (the people of Hud). Some were people of extravagance and luxury (the people of Salih). Some possessed sexual deviations (the people of Lut). Some had an inordinate love for wealth that led them to various types of fraud in transactions (the people of Shu'ayb). And some possessed the pride of wealth (the people of Nuh).

Yet, the punishment of all of them bore some similarity to one another: some were destroyed by thunderbolts and earthquakes (the people of Shu'ayb, Lut, Salih, and Hud). And some by storms and floods (the people of Nuh).

In truth, the earth, which was the cradle of their comfort, was commanded to destroy them, and the "water" and "air" that sustained their lives accepted the command for their death. How strange is the condition of man, whose life is placed in the heart of death and his death in the heart of life, yet he remains heedless and proud.

[^1]: "Musahhar," as we have previously stated, refers to someone who has been repeatedly subjected to "Sihr" (magic), such that magicians have influenced his intellect and rendered it dysfunctional.

Ayahs

وَإِنَّهُۥ لَتَنزِيلُ رَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (١٩٢)
This is indeed [a Book] sent down by the Lord of all the worlds, (192)
نَزَلَ بِهِ ٱلرُّوحُ ٱلْأَمِينُ (١٩٣)
brought down by the Trustworthy Spirit (193)
عَلَىٰ قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُنذِرِينَ (١٩٤)
upon your heart (so that you may be one of the warners), (194)
بِلِسَانٍ عَرَبِىٍّ مُّبِينٍ (١٩٥)
in a clear Arabic language. (195)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَفِى زُبُرِ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٩٦)

أَوَلَمْ يَكُن لَّهُمْ ءَايَةً أَن يَعْلَمَهُۥ عُلَمَـٰٓؤُا۟ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ (١٩٧)
Is it not a sign for them that the learned of the Children of Israel recognize it? (197)

Commentary

The Greatness of the Quran in Previous Scriptures

After presenting seven stories from the accounts of previous Prophets and the instructive lessons hidden within their history, the Quran once again returns to the same discussion with which the Surah began—the discussion of the greatness of the Quran and the truthfulness of this manifest Divine Word. It says:

"And indeed, it is a revelation from the Lord of the worlds" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَتَنْزِيلُ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ‌).

In principle, the narration of various parts of the lives of previous Prophets with such precision and elegance, and being free from any superstitions or false legends—especially in an environment that was the center of myths and fables, and by someone who was absolutely unschooled—is itself a proof that this Book has been revealed by the Lord of the worlds, and this is a sign of the Quran's miracle.

Therefore, it adds: "The Trustworthy Spirit has brought it down" (نَزَلَ بِهِ الرُّوحُ الْأَمِينُ‌). If that Angel of revelation, and that "Trustworthy Spirit of the Lord," had not brought it from Allah, it would not be so radiant, pure, and free from the contamination of superstitions and vanities.

It is noteworthy that the Angel of revelation is described here with two titles: the title "Spirit" (Ruh) and the title "Trustworthy" (Amin). A spirit which is the source of life, and trustworthiness which is the primary condition for guidance and leadership.

Yes, this "Trustworthy Spirit" revealed the Quran "Upon your heart [O Prophet], from your Lord, that you may be of the warners" (عَلى‌ قَلْبِكَ لِتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ‌) [1].

[1]: It is clear that the meaning of "heart" (qalb) here is the pure soul of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, not the physical heart which is a pump for blood circulation. The choice of this expression here indicates that you received the Quran with your soul and being, and the base of this great heavenly miracle is your heart and soul.

The goal was for you to warn the people and make them aware of the dangerous fate that would befall them due to deviating from Monotheism (Tawhid). The goal was not to recount the history of predecessors as entertainment or storytelling; the goal is to create a sense of responsibility and awakening; the goal is cultivation and the building of human character.

And so that no excuse or pretext remains for anyone, "He revealed it in a clear Arabic language" (بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ‌). This Quran was revealed in eloquent Arabic, free from any ambiguity, so that it would be sufficiently expressive for warning and awakening, especially in that environment which had very pretext-seeking and stubborn people.

The very Arabic language that draws from the most complete of languages and the richest and most fertile literatures.

It is necessary to note that one of the meanings of "Arabic" (Arabi) is eloquence and clarity—regardless of the nature of the tongue—as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat: "Al-Arabi refers to eloquent and clear speech" (this meaning is also mentioned in Lisan al-Arab).

In this case, the goal is not to emphasize the language of the Arabs, but rather the clarity of the Quran and the brightness of its concepts. Future verses also confirm this meaning, and in verse 44 of Surah Fussilat it is stated: وَ لَوْ جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً أَعْجَمِيًّا لَقالُوا لَوْ لا فُصِّلَتْ آياتُهُ‌: "And if We had made it a non-Arabic [unclear] Quran, they would have said, 'Why are its verses not explained in detail?'" Here, A'jami means non-eloquent speech.

Then, it points to another proof of the truthfulness of the Quran, saying: "And indeed, it is [mentioned] in the scriptures of the former peoples, and they gave glad tidings of its future appearance" (وَ إِنَّهُ لَفِي زُبُرِ الْأَوَّلِينَ‌) [1]. Especially in the Torah of Moses عليه السلام, the attributes of this Prophet and the attributes of this Heavenly Book were mentioned, such that the scholars of the Children of Israel were well aware of it. It is even said that the faith of the two tribes "Aws" and "Khazraj" in the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was due to the predictions that Jewish scholars made regarding the appearance of this Prophet and the revelation of this Heavenly Book.

Therefore, the Quran adds here: "Is it not a sign for them that the scholars of the Children of Israel know it?" (أَ وَ لَمْ يَكُنْ لَهُمْ آيَةً أَنْ يَعْلَمَهُ عُلَماءُ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ‌). It is clear that in an environment where so many scholars of the Children of Israel existed and were fully associated with the polytheists, it was impossible for the Quran to say such a thing about itself extravagantly, because immediately the cry of denial would rise from every side. This itself shows that in the environment of the revelation of the verses, this matter was so clear that there was no room for denial.

In verse 89 of Surah Al-Baqarah, we also read:

[1]: "Zubur" is the plural of "Zabur," meaning book, and it originally comes from the root zabr (rhyming with abr), meaning writing.

وَ كانُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ يَسْتَفْتِحُونَ عَلَى الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمْ ما عَرَفُوا كَفَرُوا بِهِ‌: "And before that they [the Jews] used to pray for victory against those who disbelieved [through the appearance of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله], but when there came to them that which they recognized, they disbelieved in it."

All of these are clear witnesses to the truth of the Quran's statement and the authenticity of its call.

Ayahs

وَلَوْ نَزَّلْنَـٰهُ عَلَىٰ بَعْضِ ٱلْأَعْجَمِينَ (١٩٨)
Had We sent it down upon some non-Arab (198)
فَقَرَأَهُۥ عَلَيْهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنِينَ (١٩٩)
and had he recited it to them, they would not have believed in it. (199)
كَذَٰلِكَ سَلَكْنَـٰهُ فِى قُلُوبِ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٢٠٠)
This is how We let it pass through the hearts of the guilty: (200)
لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِۦ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ (٢٠١)
they do not believe in it until they sight the painful punishment. (201)
فَيَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٢٠٢)
It will overtake them suddenly while they are unaware. (202)
فَيَقُولُوا۟ هَلْ نَحْنُ مُنظَرُونَ (٢٠٣)
Thereupon they will say, ‘Shall we be granted any respite?’ (203)

Commentary

If the Quran had been revealed to a non-Arab! ...

In these verses, the text first addresses one of the potential excuses of the disbelievers and preempts it, completing the discussion initiated in the previous verses regarding the revelation of the Quran in "clear Arabic."

It says: "And even if We had revealed it (this Quran) to some of the non-Arabs (those who are not Arab or not eloquent in Arabic)" ... (وَلَوْ نَزَّلْنَاهُ عَلَىٰ بَعْضِ الْأَعْجَمِينَ).

"And he had recited it to them, they would never have believed in it" (فَقَرَأَهُ عَلَيْهِم مَّا كَانُوا بِهِ مُؤْمِنِينَ). As previously mentioned, the word "Arabi" (Arabic/Arab) sometimes refers to someone of the Arab race and sometimes to "eloquent speech." Similarly, "Ajami" (non-Arab/non-eloquent) has two meanings: the non-Arab race and non-eloquent speech. In the above verse, both meanings are possible, but it seems more likely to refer to the "non-Arab race."

This means that their racism and ethnic prejudices are so intense that if the Quran had been revealed to a non-Arab individual, the waves of prejudice would have prevented them from accepting it. Even now, when it has been revealed to a noble man from an authentic Arab family, with an expressive and eloquent delivery, and its glad tidings have appeared in previous heavenly books, and the scholars of the Children of Israel have testified to it, many of them still do not believe—let alone if their Prophet did not possess such conditions at all.

Then, for further emphasis, it adds: "Thus We have caused it to enter the hearts of the criminals" (كَذَٰلِكَ سَلَكْنَاهُ فِي قُلُوبِ الْمُجْرِمِينَ). With an eloquent delivery, in the language of a man who rose from among them and whose character and tone of speech they are familiar with, and with content whose confirmation appeared in previous books—in short, We send it into the hearts of these sinful people with all the conditions that make its acceptance easy, smooth, and pleasant. Yet, these diseased hearts refuse to accept it.

It is like healthy, life-giving food that an unhealthy stomach rejects. (Note that "salaknahu" is derived from the root "suluk," meaning "to pass through a path," entering from one side and exiting from the other).

Therefore, it says: Despite this, these stubborn people "will not believe in it until they see the painful punishment" (لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِهِ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا الْعَذَابَ الْأَلِيمَ). Some commentators have suggested another possibility for the interpretation of the verse: that "Thus We have caused it to enter the hearts of the criminals" means We have caused this fanaticism, stubbornness, and impenetrability to enter the hearts of the criminals as a result of their crimes and sins.

According to this meaning, the above verse would be similar to the verse: "Allah has set a seal upon their hearts" (خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ). However, the first interpretation is more consistent with the preceding and succeeding verses; hence, a vast majority of commentators have chosen it. [1]

Yes, they will not believe "until the divine punishment overtakes them suddenly and unexpectedly while they perceive not" (فَيَأْتِيَهُم بَغْتَةً وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ). 2 Undoubtedly, this divine punishment that overtakes them suddenly refers to

[1]: In the few verses above, five singular pronouns appear in the phrases "nazzalnahu" (revealed it), "qara'ahu" (recited it), "ma kanu bihi" (not in it), "salaknahu" (caused it to enter), and "la yu'minuna bihi" (do not believe in it). According to the first interpretation, all of them refer to the Quran. However, according to the second interpretation, some refer to the Quran and some to prejudice, stubbornness, and the state of impenetrability, which is difficult to sustain without a clear contextual clue.

2: It should be noted that the phrase "faya'tiyahum" is in the subjunctive mood (mansub) and is conjoined to the phrase "hatta yaraw," and its meaning must be expressed in this context.

worldly punishments, destructive calamities, and the punishment of "extirpation" (istisal).

Therefore, it continues: At that moment, they will come to their senses, regretting their shameful past and deeply anxious about their terrifying future, and "they will say: 'Are we to be reprieved?'" so that we may believe and rebuild our ruined past?! (فَيَقُولُوا هَلْ نَحْنُ مُنظَرُونَ).

Key Points

1- Intense Racial and Tribal Prejudices

Undoubtedly, a human being feels love for whatever land, tribe, or race they belong to. This bond of affection with one's land and people is not only not a flaw but is a constructive factor for social cooperation. However, this has a limit; if it exceeds the boundary, it becomes destructive and sometimes catastrophic. The racial and tribal prejudice that is condemned is precisely this "excess."

"Ta'assub" (prejudice/fanaticism) and "asabiyyah" are originally derived from the root "asab," meaning the nerves that connect the joints. Subsequently, any kind of connection and cohesion was called "ta'assub" or "asabiyyah," but usually, this word is used in its extreme and blameworthy sense.

Extreme defense of tribe, race, and homeland has been the source of many wars throughout history and a factor for transferring superstitions and vices—under the guise of tribal and racial customs—to other nations.

This extreme defense and partisanship sometimes reach a point where the worst individuals of one's tribe appear beautiful to them, and the best individuals of another tribe appear ugly and ominous. Similarly, customs and traditions, whether ugly or beautiful, are viewed through this lens. In other words, racial prejudice is a veil of selfishness and ignorance that settles over human thought, perception, and intellect, disabling correct judgment.

This state of "asabiyyah" is more acute among certain peoples, including a group of Arabs who were famous and well-known for prejudice. We read in the above verses that the prejudice of the pre-Islamic era (Jahiliyyah) was to such an extent that if the Quran had been revealed to a non-Arab, they would never have believed in it.

In Islamic narrations, the subject of prejudice is also severely condemned as a blameworthy trait, to the extent that we read in a hadith from the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله: "Whoever has in his heart prejudice equal to a mustard seed, Allah will resurrect him on the Day of Judgment with the Arabs of the Jahiliyyah." [1] And in another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read: "Whoever practices prejudice or has prejudice practiced for him has removed the bond of faith from his neck." 2 It is also understood from Islamic narrations that Iblis (Satan) was the first to practice prejudice.

Ali عليه السلام—as recorded in Nahj al-Balagha—delivered an eloquent, expressive, and crushing discourse in the "Sermon of Disparagement" (al-Qasi'ah) regarding prejudice, a portion of which you see below:

"As for Iblis, he practiced prejudice against Adam because of his origin and challenged him regarding his creation, saying: 'I am of fire and you are of clay!'" Then he adds: "If there must be prejudice, let your prejudice be for noble traits, praiseworthy deeds, and good matters." [1] Furthermore, it is clearly evident from this hadith that standing firm in support of a desirable reality is not only not blameworthy prejudice but can fill the human spiritual void left by incorrect Jahiliyyah bonds.

Thus, we read in a hadith from Imam Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه السلام that when he was asked about "prejudice," he said: "The prejudice for which its possessor is sinful is that a man considers the wicked of his people better than the good of another people. It is not prejudice for a man to love his people, but it is prejudice to help his people in oppression." 2 Another term used for prejudice in verses and narrations is "hamiyyah" or "hamiyyat al-jahiliyyah" (the zeal of ignorance).

[1] and 2: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2 (Chapter on Prejudice), p. 232.

[1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 192 (al-Qasi'ah).

2: Usul al-Kafi (Chapter on Prejudice), Vol. 2, p. 233.

Although there is much to say on this subject, we conclude our discussion with two hadiths. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, said: "Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, punishes six groups for six traits: the Arabs for their prejudice, the landowners (and owners of land and wealth) for their pride, the rulers for their injustice, the jurists for their envy, the merchants for their dishonesty, and the rural folk for their ignorance." [1] The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله used to seek refuge in Allah every day from six things: from doubt, polytheism, prejudice (hamiyyah), anger, oppression, and envy. 2

2- The Request to Return to the World

From the moment of death, the sighs and regrets of the criminal individuals begin, and the desire to return flares up in the human soul. They let out useless cries and offer unanswered prayers in this regard.

Many examples of this are seen in the verses of the Quran, the simplest of which we read in the verses under discussion: "Are we to be reprieved?" (هَلْ نَحْنُ مُنظَرُونَ). In Surah al-An'am, verse 27, we read: "Would that we were sent back! Then we would not deny the signs of our Lord." (يَا لَيْتَنَا نُرَدُّ وَلَا نُكَذِّبَ بِآيَاتِ رَبِّنَا).

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 73, p. 289.

And in Surah al-Ahzab, verse 66, it says: "Would that we had obeyed Allah and obeyed the Messenger." (يَا لَيْتَنَا أَطَعْنَا اللَّهَ وَأَطَعْنَا الرَّسُولَا).

In Surah al-Mu'minun, verses 99 and 100, it says: "Until, when death comes to one of them, he says: 'My Lord, send me back, that I might do righteousness in that which I left behind!'" (حَتَّىٰ إِذَا جَاءَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قَالَ رَبِّ ارْجِعُونِ لَعَلِّي أَعْمَلُ صَالِحًا فِيمَا تَرَكْتُ). This situation continues even when the sinners are placed by the fire of Hell; they repeat this statement, as we read in Surah al-An'am, verse 27: "If you could but see when they are made to stand before the Fire and will say: 'Oh, would that we could be sent back and not deny the signs of our Lord and be among the believers!'" (وَلَوْ تَرَىٰ إِذْ وُقِفُوا عَلَى النَّارِ فَقَالُوا يَا لَيْتَنَا نُرَدُّ وَلَا نُكَذِّبَ بِآيَاتِ رَبِّنَا وَنَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ). But it is obvious that this return is not possible according to the Divine Sunnah. If an unripe fruit that has been separated from the tree could return to the tree, or a fetus born prematurely could rejoin the mother's womb, then such a return would be possible.

Therefore, the only rational way to prevent these regrets is by performing righteous deeds and repenting from sin while the opportunity is at hand; otherwise, the rest is in vain.

3- The Excellence of the Non-Arabs (Ajam)

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, which appears in the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim regarding the verses under discussion, we read: "If the Quran had been revealed to the non-Arabs, the Arabs would not have believed in it; but it was revealed to the Arabs and the non-Arabs believed in it, and this is an excellence for the non-Arabs." [1] In this regard, some indications also appeared in Volume 4 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 418 (under verse 54 of Surah al-Ma'idah).

[1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 4, p. 165.

Ayahs

أَفَبِعَذَابِنَا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (٢٠٤)
So do they seek to hasten on Our punishment? (204)
أَفَرَءَيْتَ إِن مَّتَّعْنَـٰهُمْ سِنِينَ (٢٠٥)
Tell me, should We let them enjoy for some years, (205)
ثُمَّ جَآءَهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُوعَدُونَ (٢٠٦)
then there comes to them what they have been promised, (206)
مَآ أَغْنَىٰ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يُمَتَّعُونَ (٢٠٧)
of what avail to them will be that which they were given to enjoy? (207)
وَمَآ أَهْلَكْنَا مِن قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا لَهَا مُنذِرُونَ (٢٠٨)
We have not destroyed any town without its having warners, (208)
ذِكْرَىٰ وَمَا كُنَّا ظَـٰلِمِينَ (٢٠٩)
for the sake of admonition, and We were not unjust. (209)
وَمَا تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ (٢١٠)
It has not been brought down by the devils. (210)
وَمَا يَنۢبَغِى لَهُمْ وَمَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ (٢١١)
Neither does it behoove them, nor are they capable [of doing that]. (211)
إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ ٱلسَّمْعِ لَمَعْزُولُونَ (٢١٢)
Indeed, they are kept at bay [even] from hearing it. (212)

Commentary

Another Slander Against the Quran

Since the previous verses concluded with the fact that the criminals and sinners—after witnessing the divine punishment and when they are on the threshold of death—request a respite and a return to make amends, the verses under discussion respond to them in two ways:

First: "Do they then seek to hasten Our punishment?" (أَ فَبِعَذابِنا يَسْتَعْجِلُونَ).

This refers to the fact that you repeatedly, with mockery and derision, asked your Prophet to bring as quickly as possible the punishment he promised. However, when you are caught in its grip, you request a respite to compensate for the past. One day you considered all of this a joke, but the next day you see it is more serious than serious.

In any case, the Sunnah (way) of the Lord is that He will not seize any people with punishment until He has completed the proof (exhausted all excuses) and given sufficient time. But when everything that needed to be said has been said, and they have had the necessary opportunity yet did not follow the right path, then comes a punishment from which there is no way back.

Second: "Have you considered if We let them enjoy [this life] for years..." (أَ فَرَأَيْتَ إِنْ مَتَّعْناهُمْ سِنِينَ).

"Then there comes to them that which they were promised..." (ثُمَّ جاءَهُمْ ما كانُوا يُوعَدُونَ).

"That which they were let to enjoy will not avail them" (ما أَغْنى‌ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يُمَتَّعُونَ).

Supposing they were given a new respite—which will not be given after the completion of proof—and supposing they remained in this world for more years and became drowned in pride and negligence, would they do anything other than enjoy and benefit more from material blessings? Would they set out to compensate for the past? Certainly not. Could these things untie any knot for them at the time of the re-descent of punishment, or create any change in their destiny?

There is also this possibility in the interpretation of the above verses: that they do not request a respite to return to the Truth and compensate for the past, but rather for more enjoyment and greater benefit from the fleeting blessings of this world. However, this enjoyment will be of no use to them, and sooner or later they must hasten from this unstable abode to the eternal abode and face the consequences of their actions.

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 15, p. 361

  • Here, a question or questions arise which the subsequent verses address: Fundamentally, considering Allah's knowledge of the future of every people and group, what is the need for a respite?

Furthermore, despite the fact that past nations repeatedly rose to deny the Prophets, and according to the phrase "ما كانَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ مُؤْمِنِينَ" (most of them were not believers)—which is repeated at the end of many of their stories—the majority of them did not believe, why then do the Prophets come one after another to warn and preach?

The Quran says in response: This is Our Sunnah; "We never destroyed any city but that it had warners" and Prophets who rose to complete the proof and provide sufficient exhortation and advice (وَ ما أَهْلَكْنا مِنْ قَرْيَةٍ إِلَّا لَها مُنْذِرُونَ).

"To remind them" so they might become mindful and awaken, and have the possibility to return (ذِكْرى) [1]. And if We had punished them without the completion of proof by means of divine warners and the wake-up calls of the messengers of the Lord, it would have been injustice, whereas "We were never unjust, and indeed injustice and oppression are not worthy of Us" (ما كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ).

It is injustice to destroy a group other than the wrongdoers, or to destroy the wrongdoers without sufficient completion of proof.

What has come in these verses is, in reality, an expression of that famous rational rule, namely the "Rule of the Evil of Punishment without Clarification" (Qubh al-Iqab bila Bayan). A similar concept also appears in Surah Al-Isra, verse 15: "وَ ما كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّى نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًا" (And We never punish until We have sent a messenger [to explain the truths]).

Yes, punishment without sufficient clarification is evil and unjust, and the All-Wise and Just Allah never does such a thing. This is the same principle that is referred to in the science of Usul (Principles of Jurisprudence) as the "Principle of Innocence" (Asl al-Bara'ah); meaning any ruling for which there is no sufficient evidence of its establishment is negated by this principle. (For more explanation, refer to Volume 12 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 57, under verse 15 of Surah Al-Isra).

Then, it proceeds to answer another of the excuses or false accusations of the enemies of the Quran who said: Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) is in contact with an individual from the Jinn, and he teaches him these verses! While the Quran emphasizes that these verses are "a revelation from the Lord of the worlds."

Here it adds: "The devils (Shayatin) and the Jinn did not bring these verses down" (وَ ما تَنَزَّلَتْ بِهِ الشَّياطِينُ).

Then, explaining the response to this hollow accusation of the enemies, it says: "It is not fitting for the Jinn and devils to bring down such a book" (وَ ما يَنْبَغِي لَهُمْ).

This means the content of this great Book—which is on the path of Truth and calls to purity, justice, piety, and the negation of any kind of polytheism—clearly shows that it has no similarity to devilish thoughts and the suggestions of the devils. The devils have no business but evil and corruption, while this Book is one of goodness and righteousness. Therefore, the study of its content alone demonstrates its authenticity. Furthermore, "they are not capable of such a thing" (وَ ما يَسْتَطِيعُونَ).

If they had such power, then others in the environment where the Quran was revealed, such as the "soothsayers" who were in contact with devils (or at least the polytheists considered their connection certain), should have been able to bring something like it. Yet they all remained helpless, and through their helplessness, it was proven that these verses are beyond their power.

Furthermore, the "soothsayers" themselves admitted that after the birth of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), the connection of the devils who were in contact with them was cut off from the news of the heavens, "and they are removed and barred from hearing (the news of the heaven)" (إِنَّهُمْ عَنِ السَّمْعِ لَمَعْزُولُونَ).

It appears from other verses of the Quran that the devils previously went to the heavens and eavesdropped, and occasionally transferred matters reflected among the angels to their friends. However, with the birth and emergence of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), eavesdropping was completely cut off and that informational connection vanished. The polytheists themselves knew this fact, and even if they did not know, the Quran informed them of it [1]. For this reason, the Quran relies on it as a proof in the above verses.

In this way, this accusation has been answered through three paths: 1. The lack of harmony between the content of the Quran and devilish suggestions. 2. The lack of power of the devils for such a task. 3. The devils being barred from eavesdropping!

[1]: Regarding the grammatical position of "ذِكْرى" here, commentators have given four possibilities: First, that it is an "object of purpose" (Maf'ul lahu) for "منذرون" (the above commentary is based on this). Second, that it is an "absolute object" (Maf'ul mutlaq) for "منذرون", because warning (indhar) is close in meaning to reminding (tadhkirah). Third, that it is a "circumstantial qualifier" (Hal) for the pronoun in "منذرون". And fourth, that it is the "predicate" (Khabar) of an omitted subject (This is a reminder).

[1]: For more explanation regarding the barring of devils from "eavesdropping," refer to the first volume of Sirah Ibn Hisham, page 217 onwards—and we have explained this subject from an interpretative perspective and the meaning of the devils being driven away by the "piercing flame" (Shihab Thaqib) from "eavesdropping in the heavens" in Volume 11 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 42 onwards.

Ayahs

فَلَا تَدْعُ مَعَ ٱللَّهِ إِلَـٰهًا ءَاخَرَ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُعَذَّبِينَ (٢١٣)
So do not invoke any god besides Allah, lest you should be among the punished. (213)

وَٱخْفِضْ جَنَاحَكَ لِمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٢١٥)
