فَإِنْ عَصَوْكَ فَقُلْ إِنِّى بَرِىٓءٌ مِّمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٢١٦)
But if they disobey you, say, ‘I am absolved of what you do.’ (216)
وَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلرَّحِيمِ (٢١٧)
And put your trust in the All-mighty, the All-merciful, (217)
ٱلَّذِى يَرَىٰكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ (٢١٨)
who sees you when you stand [for prayer], (218)
وَتَقَلُّبَكَ فِى ٱلسَّـٰجِدِينَ (٢١٩)
and your going about among those who prostrate. (219)
إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٢٢٠)
Indeed He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing. (220)

Commentary

Invite Your Closest Kin to Islam

Following the discussions in the previous verses regarding the stances of the polytheists against Islam and the Quran, Allah, in the verses under discussion, outlines the program and policy of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله through five instructions.

Before anything else, He invites the Prophet himself to a more steadfast belief in Monotheism (Tawhid)—the Monotheism that forms the root and foundation of all the prophets' calls—saying:

"Do not call upon any other deity with Allah, or you will be punished!" (فَلا تَدْعُ مَعَ اللَّهِ إِلهاً آخَرَ فَتَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُعَذَّبِينَ).

Although the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله was undoubtedly the herald of Monotheism and any deviation from this principle was inconceivable for him, the importance of this matter is so great that Allah addresses him personally first, so that others may take account of themselves. Furthermore, the reformation of others must begin with self-reformation.

Then, moving to a broader stage, He commands: "Warn your closest relatives, and frighten them of polytheism and defying the command of your Lord" (وَ أَنْذِرْ عَشِيرَتَكَ الْأَقْرَبِينَ) [1]. Undoubtedly, to launch a vast revolutionary program, one must start from the smaller, tighter circles. What better way than for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to begin his first call with his relatives, who know his history of purity better than anyone else, and whose close bonds of kinship require them to listen to his words more than others, being further removed from jealousy, malice, and hostile stances.

Moreover, this shows that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله has no compromise or flattery with anyone; he does not even exempt his polytheistic relatives from the call toward Monotheism, truth, and justice.

When this verse was revealed, the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله organized a plan to execute this command, the details of which you will read, God willing, in the "Key Points" of these verses.

In the third stage, a wider circle is considered, as He says: Welcome with love and humility the believers who follow you, "and lower your wing to them" (وَ اخْفِضْ جَناحَكَ لِمَنِ اتَّبَعَكَ مِنَ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ). This beautiful expression is a metaphor for humility coupled with love, affection, and kindness, just as birds, when they wish to show affection to their chicks, spread and lower their wings, taking them under their plumage to protect them from potential dangers and keep them from scattering. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is likewise commanded to take the true believers under his wing.

This meaningful expression conveys various subtleties regarding love for the believers, which become clear with a little reflection.

Additionally, mentioning this sentence after the issue of warning (Inzar) highlights the reality that if, for educational purposes, there is an emphasis on firmness and fear in one place, it is immediately followed by an emphasis on love, mercy, and affection, so that a suitable blend of the two is formed.

Then, addressing the fourth instruction, He says: "If they disobey you and rise in opposition, do not be distressed, but tell them: I am free of what you do," and in this way, make your position clear to them (فَإِنْ عَصَوْكَ فَقُلْ إِنِّي بَرِي‌ءٌ مِمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ). It appears that the pronoun in the phrase "disobey you" (عصوك) refers to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله close relatives; meaning, if after inviting them to the truth they do not submit to you and continue in polytheism and opposition, define your stance against them. This Quranic prediction came to pass, as will be mentioned in the "Key Points," and all of them except Ali عليه السلام refrained from accepting the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call; some remained silent, while others manifested their opposition through mockery and derision.

Finally, in the fifth instruction to complete the previous programs, He says to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله: "And put your trust in the Almighty, the Merciful" (وَ تَوَكَّلْ عَلَى الْعَزِيزِ الرَّحِيمِ). Let these oppositions never discourage you, and let the fewness of friends and followers not affect your iron will. You are not alone; your support is Allah, the Invincible and the Most Merciful.

The same Allah whose description as "Almighty and Merciful" you heard at the end of the stories of previous prophets.

The same Allah who, with His power, broke the oppression of the Pharaohs, the pride of the Nimrods, the selfishness and arrogance of the people of Noah, the worldliness of the people of 'Ad, and the hedonism of the people of Lot, and delivered these great prophets and the believers who were in the minority, encompassing them in His mercy.

"The same God who sees you when you stand up" (الَّذِي يَراكَ حِينَ تَقُومُ).

"And observes your movement among those who prostrate" (وَ تَقَلُّبَكَ فِي السَّاجِدِينَ).

Yes, "He is the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing" (إِنَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ). Thus, after describing Allah as Almighty and Merciful, three other attributes—each more heartening and hopeful than the last—appear in these verses: The God who sees the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله hardships and is aware of his standing, prostrating, movement, and stillness.

The God who hears his voice.

And the God who is aware of his desires and needs.

Yes, upon such a God you must rely and entrust the reins of your affairs to Him.

Key Points

1- Commentary on "تَقَلُّبَكَ فِي السَّاجِدِينَ"

Regarding what is meant by the two phrases "Who sees you when you stand up" and "your movement among those who prostrate," commentators have offered various interpretations.

The apparent meaning of the verse is what we stated above: Allah sees you when you stand and observes your movement and transition among those who prostrate.

This "standing" might be standing for prayer, or rising from sleep for worship, or standing in the sense of "individual prayer" in contrast to "your movement among those who prostrate," which refers to congregational prayer—or all of these.

"Taqallub" means moving, circulating, and transitioning from one state to another. This expression might refer to the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله prostration among those prostrating during prayer, or the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله movement among his companions who were busy with worship as he checked on their condition.

Altogether, it indicates that no state of yours, and no effort of your efforts—whether individual or collective with other believers to organize the affairs of the servants and spread the true religion—is hidden from Allah (note that the verbs in the verse are in the present/future tense, indicating ongoing action).

However, two other interpretations are mentioned here which are not very consistent with the literal meaning and might be from the inner depths (batn) of the verse.

First, the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله looking at the worshippers behind him, because he was able to see people and objects from behind just as he could see them from the front. As stated in a Hadith, he said:

"Do not raise your heads before me and do not go into prostration before me, for I see you from behind me just as I see you from in front of me!" Then the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recited the above verse as evidence for his words [1].

[1]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verse discussed.

The other interpretation is that it refers to the transfer of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله through the loins of the prophets from Adam to Abdullah, all occurring under the watchful grace of the Lord. That is, when your pure essence was transferred from one monotheistic and prostrating prophet to another, Allah was aware of it all.

In the Tafsir of Ali ibn Ibrahim, from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the phrase "وَ تَقَلُّبَكَ فِي السَّاجِدِينَ," it is stated:

"In the loins of the prophets, may the blessings of Allah be upon them" [1]. And in the Tafsir "Majma' al-Bayan," explaining this same phrase from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام and Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is said:

"In the loins of the prophets, one prophet after another, until He brought him forth from the loin of his father through a marriage free from any impurity, from the time of Adam onwards" [2]. Of course, regardless of the above verses and their interpretation, we have evidence showing that the fathers and ancestors of the prophets of God were never polytheists and were born in an environment pure from the pollutions of polytheism and indecency (for more explanation, refer to Volume 5 of Tafsir Nemouneh, page 305, under verse 74 of Surah Al-An'am), but the above interpretations are considered part of the inner meanings of the verse.

2- Warning the Close Relatives (The Hadith of Yawm al-Dar)

Based on what is recorded in Islamic histories, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was commanded to convey this call in the third year of the Bi'thah (the start of his mission), because until then, the call was conducted secretly,

[1]: Tafsir "Nur al-Thaqalayn," Vol. 4, p. 69. [2]: "Majma' al-Bayan," under the verses discussed.

and only a few had accepted Islam. However, when the verse "And warn your closest relatives" and the verse "Proclaim what you are commanded and turn away from the polytheists" (Surah Al-Hijr, verse 94) were revealed, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was commanded to make his call public and to start first with his relatives [1]. The manner of this proclamation and warning was, in brief, that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله invited his close relatives to the house of Abu Talib. They were about forty people that day, and the Prophet's uncles Abu Talib, Hamzah, and Abu Lahab were present. After the meal, when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله wanted to convey his duty, Abu Lahab disrupted the atmosphere with his words. Therefore, the next day, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله invited them again for a meal.

After the meal, he said: "O children of Abd al-Muttalib! By Allah, I know of no young man among the Arabs who has brought to his people something better than what I have brought to you. I have brought you the good of this world and the Hereafter, and Allah has commanded me to invite you to this religion. Which of you will assist me in this matter, to be my brother, my legatee (wasi), and my successor (khalifah) among you?"

The entire group refused except Ali عليه السلام, who was the youngest of them; he stood up and said: "O Prophet of Allah! I am your helper and assistant in this path." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله placed his hand on Ali's عليه السلام neck and said:

"This is my brother, my legatee, and my successor among you; listen to him and obey him."

The crowd stood up while laughing mockingly, saying to Abu Talib: "He commands you to listen to your son and obey him!" This Hadith has been narrated by many Sunni scholars, such as Ibn Abi Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn Mardawayh, Abu Nu'aym, Bayhaqi, Tha'labi, and Tabari. Ibn al-Athir recorded this in the second volume of "Al-Kamil," as did Abu al-Fida in the first volume of his history, and a group of others [1]. This Hadith shows how alone the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was that day, and how there was no reaction to his call except mockery and derision, and how Ali عليه السلام stood by his side and defended him from the very first days of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله call, when he suffered greatly from loneliness.

In another Hadith, it is mentioned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله called the tribes of Quraysh one by one and warned them of the divine fire. Sometimes he would say:

"O children of Ka'b, save yourselves from the Fire."

And sometimes he would address the tribes of "Bani Abd al-Shams," "Bani Abd Manaf," "Bani Hashim," and "Bani Abd al-Muttalib," saying:

"Save yourselves from the Fire!" for I have no power to defend you in the case of disbelief [2].

[1]: Sirah Ibn Hisham, Vol. 1, p. 280. [1]: For more explanation, refer to the book "Al-Muraja'at," p. 130 onwards, and the book "Ihqaq al-Haq," Vol. 4, p. 62 onwards. [2]: "Tafsir Qurtubi," Vol. 7, p. 4859, under the verses discussed, with slight abridgment.

Ayahs

هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ عَلَىٰ مَن تَنَزَّلُ ٱلشَّيَـٰطِينُ (٢٢١)
Should I inform you on whom the devils descend? (221)
تَنَزَّلُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ (٢٢٢)
They descend on every sinful liar. (222)
يُلْقُونَ ٱلسَّمْعَ وَأَكْثَرُهُمْ كَـٰذِبُونَ (٢٢٣)
They eavesdrop, and most of them are liars. (223)
وَٱلشُّعَرَآءُ يَتَّبِعُهُمُ ٱلْغَاوُۥنَ (٢٢٤)
As for the poets, [only] the perverse follow them. (224)
أَلَمْ تَرَ أَنَّهُمْ فِى كُلِّ وَادٍ يَهِيمُونَ (٢٢٥)
Have you not regarded that they rove in every valley, (225)
وَأَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ مَا لَا يَفْعَلُونَ (٢٢٦)
and that they say what they do not do? (226)
إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَعَمِلُوا۟ ٱلصَّـٰلِحَـٰتِ وَذَكَرُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ كَثِيرًا وَٱنتَصَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ظُلِمُوا۟ ۗ وَسَيَعْلَمُ ٱلَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوٓا۟ أَىَّ مُنقَلَبٍ يَنقَلِبُونَ (٢٢٧)
Barring those who have faith, do righteous deeds, and remember Allah much often, and vindicate themselves after they have been wronged. And the wrongdoers will soon know at what goal they will end up. (227)

Commentary

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) is Not a Poet

The above verses, which are the final verses of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, return once again to the discussion of the previous verses regarding the enemies' slander against the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), claiming that the Quran is the inspiration of devils, and responds to them again with an eloquent and crushing statement.

It says: "Shall I inform you upon whom the devils descend?" (هَلْ أُنَبِّئُكُمْ عَلى‌ مَنْ تَنَزَّلُ الشَّياطِينُ).

"They descend upon every sinful liar" (تَنَزَّلُ عَلى‌ كُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ). [1]

"The devils fling what they hear, mixed with many lies, to their friends, and most of them are liars" (يُلْقُونَ السَّمْعَ وَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ كاذِبُونَ). In short, satanic inspirations have clear signs by which they can be recognized. Satan is a destructive, harmful, and ruinous being.

[1]: "Affak" (أفاك) is derived from "Ifk" (إفك), meaning a great lie; therefore, "Affak" means one who frequently tells great lies. "Athim" (أثيم) is derived from "Ithm" (إثم), which originally means an act that delays a person from reward and is commonly used to mean sin; thus, "Athim" means a sinner.

And his inspirations are in the direction of corruption and destruction, and his clients are sinful liars. None of these matters apply to the Quran or its bringer, nor do they bear the slightest resemblance to it.

The people of that environment knew the way and character of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) in truthfulness, trustworthiness, and righteousness. The content of the Quran is also nothing but a call to monotheism, truth, justice, and reform in all fields. Therefore, how can you accuse it of being the inspirations of Satan?

The meaning of "every sinful liar" (أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ) refers to those soothsayers who were in contact with devils. The devils would sometimes hear some truthful words from the angels through eavesdropping and mix them with many falsehoods, passing them to the soothsayers. They, in turn, would add lies to them and tell the people, weaving dozens of lies alongside one truthful word! Especially after the revelation of the Wahy and the devils being barred from ascending to the heavens and eavesdropping, what they inspired to the soothsayers was a handful of lies and nonsense. Given this, how could the content of the Quran be compared to that? And how could the trustworthy and truthful Prophet be measured against a bunch of lying, sinful soothsayers?!

Various interpretations have been given for the phrase "they fling what they hear" (يُلْقُونَ السَّمْعَ):

First, the pronoun within it refers to the devils, and "Sam'" (hearing) means the things heard. That is, the devils fling what they have heard to their friends, and most of them are liars (adding many lies to it).

Second, the pronoun refers to the sinful liars who listened to the words of the devils or flung what they had heard from the devils to the masses of people.

However, the first interpretation seems more correct. [1]

In the fourth verse under discussion, it addresses another of the infidels' slanders against the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), where they called him a poet. As mentioned in verse 5 of Surah Al-Anbiya, they would sometimes say: "Nay, he is a poet" (بَلْ هُوَ شاعِرٌ), and sometimes they even called him a "mad poet," as stated in verse 36 of Surah As-Saffat: "And they say: Shall we indeed abandon our gods for a mad poet?" (وَ يَقُولُونَ أَ إِنَّا لَتارِكُوا آلِهَتِنا لِشاعِرٍ مَجْنُونٍ).

The Quran, in the verse under discussion, states with a very logical expression: The path of the Prophet is separate from the path of poets. Poets move in the world of imagination and fancy, while he moves in a world full of realism to bring order to the human world. Poets are often seekers of pleasure and are bound by the tresses and beauty marks of the beloved (especially the poets who lived in that era and in the environment of Hijaz, as is evident from the samples of their poetry).

For this reason, "As for the poets, it is the erring who follow them" (وَ الشُّعَراءُ يَتَّبِعُهُمُ الْغاوُونَ).

Then it adds this sentence: "Do you not see that they wander distracted in every valley?" (أَ لَمْ تَرَ أَنَّهُمْ فِي كُلِّ وادٍ يَهِيمُونَ). [2] They are drowned in their poetic fancies and metaphors, even when the rhymes

[1]: Because "Yulqun" (يُلْقُونَ) from the root "Ilqa" (إلقاء) in these cases means transferring news and matters, as mentioned in verse 53 of Surah Al-Hajj: "That He may make what Satan flings a trial for those in whose hearts is a disease." Also, the phrase "most of them are liars" (أَكْثَرُهُمْ كاذِبُونَ) is consistent with the work of devils; otherwise, those who are "Affak" (habitual liars) are all liars, not most of them (take note).

[2]: "Yahimun" (يَهِيمُونَ) is from the root "Hiyam," meaning to walk without a goal!

pull them this way and that, they wander aimlessly in every valley.

They are often not bound by logic and reasoning, and their poems spring from their emotions. These emotions and imaginative leaps drive them to a different valley at every moment.

When they are pleased with someone, they raise him to the heights of the heavens with their praises, even if he deserves the depths of the earth, and they make a beautiful angel out of him even if he is an accursed devil! And when they are offended by someone, they satirize him so much as if they want to drag him to the lowest of the low, even if he is a celestial and pure being.

Does the calculated content of the Quran have any resemblance to the intellectual backgrounds of poets, especially the poets of that environment who had no business other than describing wine, the beauty of the beloved, the features of the companion, praising specific tribes, and slandering and satirizing enemies?

Furthermore, poets are usually men of the banquet, not warriors of the battlefield; they are people of words, not action. Therefore, in the next verse, it adds: "And that they say what they do not do" (وَ أَنَّهُمْ يَقُولُونَ ما لا يَفْعَلُونَ). But the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) is action from head to toe, and even his enemies praise him for his firm resolve, his extraordinary perseverance, and the importance he gave to the practical aspects of matters. Where is the poet, and where is the Prophet of Islam?

From the summary of the above statements, it is understood that the Quran has mentioned three signs for this group of poets:

First, their followers are the group of the erring, who flee from realities with imaginary and fanciful models.

Second, they are aimless people, and their line of thought changes quickly, being easily influenced by emotions.

Third, they say things that they do not do; even when they state a reality, they themselves are not men of action.

However, none of these three attributes apply to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله); he is the exact opposite of these descriptions.

But since among poets there are pure and purposeful individuals who are people of action and truth, and who call towards honesty and purity (though such poets were rarely found in that environment), the Quran, so that the rights of these faithful artists and sincere strivers are not lost, separates their rank from others with an exception, saying: "Except those who believe and do righteous deeds" (إِلَّا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَ عَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ). Poets whose goal is not merely poetry, but who seek divine and human goals through poetry; poets who do not drown in poems and become heedless of God, but rather "remember Allah much" and whose poems remind people of God (وَ ذَكَرُوا اللَّهَ كَثِيراً).

"And who defend themselves after they have been wronged" using their talent to defend themselves and the believers (وَ انْتَصَرُوا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما ظُلِمُوا).

And if they engage in satirizing and slandering a group with their poems, it is because they are defending the truth against their poetic assault and aggression.

Thus, four attributes are mentioned for these purposeful poets: "Faith," "Righteous deeds," "Remembering Allah much," and "Rising against oppression and using the power of poetry to repel it."

And since most of the verses of this Surah are a consolation to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the few believers of that day against the multitude of enemies, and also since many verses of this Surah were revealed to defend the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) against unjust accusations, the Surah ends with a meaningful and threatening sentence to these stubborn enemies, saying:

"And those who do wrong will soon know to what final place of return they shall return" (وَ سَيَعْلَمُ الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا أَيَّ مُنْقَلَبٍ يَنْقَلِبُونَ).

Although some commentators have wanted to identify this return and fate exclusively as the fire of Hell, we have no reason to limit it. Rather, it is possible that the successive defeats they suffered in the battles of Badr and the like, and the weakness and humiliation that finally befell them in this world, in addition to the defeat in the Hereafter, are all included in the concept of this threat.

Key Points

1- Why was the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) accused of poetry?

As we have said, it is understood from various verses of the Quran that one of the common slanders they attributed to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) was poetry and being a poet, and the above verses were a response to such an accusation.

They knew very well that the Quran has the least resemblance to poetry, neither in terms of appearance—that is, poetic rhythm, meter, and rhyme—nor in terms of content, metaphors, imaginations, and poetic lyrics.

However, because they saw the extraordinary attraction of the Quran in the minds of the people and felt its pleasant melody within their own souls, to cast a veil over this Divine light, they sometimes called it sorcery, because it had a mysterious influence on thoughts, and sometimes they called it poetry, because it shook hearts and carried them along! In reality, they wanted to criticize, but with these words, they were praising, and their speech was a living document of the extraordinary influence of the Quran on thoughts and hearts.

Regarding the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله), the Quran says: "And We have not taught him poetry, nor is it meet for him. This is only a Reminder and a plain Quran, to warn him who is alive" (Ya-Sin: 69-70).

2- Poetry and Poets in Islam

Undoubtedly, the talent for poetry and the art of being a poet, like all of man's existential assets, is valuable if it is employed in a correct path and used positively and constructively. However, if it is used as a destructive tool to ruin the foundations of belief and morality in society and to encourage corruption and licentiousness, or if it leads humans toward futility, vanity, and fantasy, or is regarded merely as contentless entertainment, it is worthless and even harmful.

With this statement, the answer to the question becomes clear: ultimately, do we understand from the above verses that being a poet is good or bad? Is it ugly or beautiful? And is Islam for or against poetry?

The answer is that Islam's evaluation in this field is based on "goals," "orientations," and "results." As stated by the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), when a group of his companions on one of the nights of the holy month of Ramadan, at the time of Iftar, turned the conversation to poetry and poets, he addressed them and said:

"Know that the criterion of your affair is religion, your protection is piety, your ornament is manners, and the fortresses of your honor are forbearance." [1] This indicates that poetry is a tool, and the criterion for its evaluation is the goal for which the poetry is employed.

Unfortunately, throughout the history of the literature of the nations and peoples of the world, poetry has been greatly misused, and this delicate Divine talent has been so disgraced in polluted environments that it has sometimes been among the most effective factors of corruption and destruction. Especially in the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), which was a period of intellectual and moral decline for the Arab people, "poetry," "wine," and "plunder" were always found together!

But who can deny the truth that constructive and purposeful poems have created many epics throughout history, and have sometimes mobilized a people or nation against bloodthirsty and oppressive enemies such that, heedless of everything, they charged the enemy's ranks and pierced their heart?

During the period when we witnessed the fruition of the Islamic Revolution, we saw with our own eyes what passion, excitement, and movement were created by rhythmic poems and slogans cast in the form of poetry, and how they made the blood boil in the veins and caused the ranks of people to roar, and how these short and simple, yet epic and exciting poems sent shivers down the enemy's spine and shook the foundations of their palaces.

And who can deny that sometimes an ethical poem penetrates so deeply into the depths of the human soul that a large and content-rich book cannot do the same?

Yes, as narrated in the famous Hadith from the noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله):

"Indeed, some poetry is wisdom, and indeed, some eloquence is sorcery!" [1] Sometimes poems create a revolution.

Sometimes rhythmic poetic words have the sharpness of a sword and the penetration of an arrow into the heart of the enemy, as we read in a Hadith from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) regarding such poems:

"By Him in whose hand is the soul of Muhammad, it is as if you are pelting them with arrows!" [2] He said this when the enemy was trying to weaken the morale of the Muslims with their satirical poems; the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) ordered that they compose poetry in their dispraise and to strengthen the morale of the believers.

And regarding one of the poets defending Islam, he said:

"Satirize them, for indeed Gabriel is with you!" [3] Especially when Ka'b ibn Malik, a faithful poet who composed poetry to strengthen Islam, asked the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله): "O Messenger of Allah, these verses of dispraise have been revealed regarding poetry, what should I do?" He replied:

"The believer strives with his soul, his sword, and his tongue." [4] From the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليهم السلام), there are also many descriptions regarding purposeful poems and poets, prayers for them, and great rewards given to them, which would take too long to narrate.

[1]: This Hadith has been cited by a large group of Islamic scholars, both Shi'a and Sunni, in their books (refer to Al-Ghadir, vol. 2, p. 9). [2]: Musnad Ahmad, vol. 3, p. 460. [3]: Musnad Ahmad, vol. 4, p. 299. [4]: Tafsir al-Qurtubi, vol. 7, p. 4869.

But alas, throughout history, a group has polluted this great art and delicate celestial talent, which is among the most beautiful manifestations of creation, and dropped it from the heights of heaven to the depths of materialism. They told so many lies that the famous proverb "The best of it is the most lying of it" (احسنه اكذبه) came into existence.

Sometimes they placed it in the service of oppressors and tyrants, and for the sake of a small reward, they engaged in such flattery and sycophancy that their thoughts placed the throne of the heavens under their feet "just to kiss the stirrup of Qizil Arslan!" And sometimes they went so far in describing pleasure, wine, scandal, and disgrace that the pen is ashamed to mention it.

Sometimes they ignited the fires of wars with their poems and set humans against each other for plunder and slaughter, staining the face of the earth with the blood of the innocent.

But in contrast, there were faithful and high-minded poets who did not pay tribute to the heavens and used this celestial talent in the path of human freedom, purity, piety, and the struggle against thieves, looters, and tyrants, reaching the pinnacle of glory.

Sometimes, in defense of the truth, they composed poems such that with every verse, they bought a house (bayt) in Paradise for themselves. [1] And sometimes, during suffocating eras when tyrannical rulers like the Umayyads and Abbasids had suppressed breaths in chests, they polished hearts by reciting a qasida like the "Madaris Ayat" qasida, and pushed aside the veils of lies and deception, as if the Holy Spirit (Ruh al-Qudus) was flowing these poems through their tongues. [2] And sometimes, to create movement in the suffering masses who felt humiliated, they composed poems and created passion, epic, and excitement.

And the Quran says about these: "Except those who believe and do righteous deeds and remember Allah much and defend themselves after they have been wronged."

Interestingly, this group of poets sometimes left behind such eternal works that the great leaders of Islam, according to some narrations, recommended people to memorize their poems. As narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) regarding the poems of "Abdi":

"O assembly of Shi'a, teach your children the poetry of Abdi, for he is upon the religion of Allah." [2] We end this discussion with one of the famous poems of Abdi composed regarding the caliphate and succession of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله):

"They said the Messenger of Allah did not choose an Imam after him, but we chose for ourselves! We appointed an Imam; if he stays on guidance, we obey, and if he strays from guidance, we straighten him! So we said: In that case, you are the Imam of your Imam; by the praise of the All-Merciful, you have wandered aimlessly, but we have not. But we chose the one whom our Lord chose for us on the day of Khum; we have not transgressed nor have we turned away! And we are upon a clear light from Allah; O Lord, increase us in light from Yourself and keep us firm!" [1]

3- Remembrance of Allah

In the above verses, we read that one of the characteristics of purposeful poets is that they remember Allah much.

In a Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read that he said: The meaning of "much remembrance" (ذكر كثير) is the Tasbih of Fatima al-Zahra (سلام الله عليها) (which consists of Takbir, Hamd, and Tasbih).

And in another Hadith from the same Imam (عليه السلام), we read that he said: "One of the hardest and most important things that Allah has made obligatory upon the creation is the frequent remembrance of Allah." Then he said: "I do not mean that they should just say 'Subhan Allah, wal-Hamdu lillah, wa la ilaha illallah, wallahu Akbar,' although this is also a part of it, but rather: Remembering Allah when one faces what is permissible (halal) and what is forbidden (haram); if it is an act of obedience to Allah, he performs it, and if it is a sin, he leaves it." [2]

[1]: It is narrated from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he said: "Whoever recites a verse of poetry about us, Allah builds a house for him in Paradise" (Al-Ghadir, vol. 2, p. 3). [2]: In another Hadith from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام), we read: "No reciter recites a verse of poetry about us except that he is aided by the Holy Spirit" (Uyun Akhbar al-Rida). [3]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, vol. 4, p. 71. [4]: Al-Kuna wal-Alqab, vol. 2, p. 455. [5]: Usul al-Kafi.

O Lord! Fill our hearts with Your remembrance so that in every situation we choose what pleases You and turn away from what causes Your anger and wrath.

O God: Make our tongues eloquent, our pens powerful, and our hearts full of sincerity, so that we may use them all in Your path and for Your pleasure—Amin, O Lord of the Worlds.

End of Surah Ash-Shu'ara, 10th of Rajab 1403 AH, the birth anniversary of Imam al-Jawad (عليه السلام), Ordibehesht 1362 SH.
